Deduce, stating any properties of Möbius transforms that you need, that $M_{a}$ maps the unit disc $D = \{z:|z| < 1\}$ to itself and interchanges 0 and $a$. Show that the set $\mathcal{H}'$ of Möbius maps $T$ such that $T|_{D}:D\to D$ is bijective is a subgroup of the group $\mathcal{M}$ of all Möbius transformations generated by the transformations $M_{a}$ with $|a| < 1$ and the rotations.

Lemma 10.62 reduces our problem to one of finding a metric $d$ on the half plane $H = \{z : \Im z > 0\}$ such that $d(Tz_1, Tz_2) = d(z_1, z_2)$ for all $z_{1}, z_{2} \in H$, whenever $T$ is one of the transformations $T_{a}$ with $a \in \mathbb{R}$, $D_{\lambda}$ with $\lambda > 0$, and $J$ defined by

$$T_a(z) = a + z,$$
$$D_{\lambda}(z) = \lambda z,$$
$$J(z) = -z^{-1}.$$

We try using the ideas of this section and defining

$$d(z_1, z_2) = \inf \left\{\int_{\Gamma} g(z) ds: \Gamma \text{ joining } z_1 \text{ and } z_2 \right\},$$

for some appropriate strictly positive function $g: H \to \mathbb{R}$. (Throughout, we write $z = x + iy$ and identify the plane $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $\mathbb{C}$ in the usual manner.)

Arguing informally, this suggests that, to first order,

$$d(z + \delta z, z) = g(z) \delta s = g(z) |(z + \delta z) - z|.$$

If $T$ is one of the transformations we are considering, then we must have, to first order,

$$\begin{array}{l} g(z) |(z + \delta z) - z| = d(z + \delta z, z) = d(T(z + \delta z), Tz) \\ = g(Tz) |T(z + \delta z) - T(z)| = g(Tz) |T'(z)| |\delta z|, \end{array}$$

and so

$$g(z) = g(Tz) |T'(z)|.$$

Taking $T = T_{a}$, we obtain $g(z) = g(z + a)$ for all real $a$, so $g(x + iy) = h(y)$ for some well-behaved function $h:(0,\infty)\to (0,\infty)$.

Taking $T = D_{\lambda}(z)$, we obtain $g(z) = \lambda g(\lambda z)$ for all real strictly positive $\lambda$. Thus $h(y) = \lambda h(\lambda y)$ for all $\lambda, y > 0$. Taking $\lambda = 1/y$, we obtain $h(y) = Ay$ for some $A > 0$. The factor $A$ merely scales everything, so we take $A = 1$ and decide to experiment with $g(x + iy) = 1/y$.


### ex_10_65 [ex_10_65]

Verify that, if $g(x + iy) = 1/y$ and $T = J$, then the equation $g(z) = g(Tz)|T'(z)|$ holds.


### ex_10_66 [ex_10_66]

Suppose that $\gamma: [0,1] \to H$ is well behaved and $T \in \mathcal{H}$. Let $\tilde{\gamma} = T \circ \gamma$ (that is, $\tilde{\gamma}(z) = T(\gamma(z))$). If $\Gamma$ is the path described by $\gamma$ and $\tilde{\Gamma}$ is the path described by $\tilde{\gamma}$, show, using whatever definition of the line integral you wish, that

$$\inf \left\{\int_{\Gamma} g(z) ds\right\} = \inf \left\{\int_{\tilde{\Gamma}} g(z) ds\right\}$$

for $T = T_{a}$, $T = D_{\lambda}$ and $T = J$. Conclude that the equality holds for all $T \in \mathcal{H}$.

Exercise 10.66 shows that, if we set

$$d(z_1, z_2) = \inf \left\{\int_{\Gamma} \frac{1}{y} ds: \Gamma \text{ joining } z_1 \text{ and } z_2 \right\},$$

then we do, indeed, get an invariant metric (that is to say, a metric with $d(Tz_1, Tz_2) = d(z_1, z_2)$ for all $z_1, z_2 \in H$ whenever $T \in \mathcal{H}$).

To find out more about this metric we need to find the geodesics, and to do this we use the methods of the calculus of variation described in Section 8.4. We shall use the methods in a purely exploratory manner with no attempt at rigour. (Notice that, even if we did things rigorously, we would only get necessary conditions.) Suppose that we wish to find the path $\Gamma_0$ which minimises $\int_{\Gamma} \frac{1}{y} ds$ among all paths $\Gamma$ from $z_1 = x_1 + iy_1$ to $z_2 = x_2 + iy_2$ with $x_1 < x_2$ and $y_1, y_2 > 0$. It seems reasonable to look for a path given by $z = x + iy(x)$, where $y: [x_1, x_2] \to (0, \infty)$ is well behaved. We thus wish to minimise

$$\int_{x_1}^{x_2} \frac{1}{y(x)} (1 + y'(x)^2)^{1/2} dx = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} G(y(x), y'(x)) dx,$$

where $G(v, w) = v^{-1}(1 + w^2)^{1/2}$. The Euler-Lagrange equation gives, via Exercise 8.63 (i),

$$G(y(x), y'(x)) - y'(x) G_{,2}(y(x), y'(x)) = c,$$

where $c$ is a constant. Writing the equation more explicitly, we get

$$\frac{(1 + y'^2)^{1/2}}{y} - \frac{y'^2}{y(1 + y'^2)^{1/2}} = c,$$

so that

$$1 = cy(1 + y'^2)^{1/2}.$$

Setting $a = c^{-1}$, we obtain

$$a^2 = y^2(1 + y'^2),$$

so that, solving formally,

$$\frac{y dy}{(a^2 - y^2)^{1/2}} = dx$$

and

$$-(a^2 - y^2)^{1/2} = x - b,$$

for some constant $b \in \mathbb{R}$. Thus

$$(x - b)^2 + y^2 = a^2.$$

We expect the geodesic to be the arc of a circle with its centre on the real axis and passing through the two points $z_{1}$ and $z_{2}$.


### ex_10_67 [ex_10_67]

Suppose $(x_{1},y_{1})$, $(x_{2},y_{2}) \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ and $x_{1} \neq x_{2}$. Show that there is one and only one circle with its centre on the real axis passing through the two points $(x_{1},y_{1})$ and $(x_{2},y_{2})$.

What happens if $\Re z_1 = \Re z_2$? One way of guessing is to consider the geodesic path between $z_1$ and $z_2 + \delta$, where $\delta$ is real and non-zero. If we let $\delta \to 0$, the appropriate circle arcs approach a straight line joining $z_1$ and $z_2$, so we would expect this to be the solution.


### ex_10_68 [ex_10_68]

Attack the geodesic problem by considering paths given by $z = x(y) + iy$, where $y: [y_1, y_2] \to \mathbb{R}$ is well behaved. (The difficulties are mainly notational; the formulae of the variational calculus assume that $y$ is a function of $x$, and it requires a certain amount of clear headedness to deal with the case when $x$ is a function of $y$. You should be able to make use of Exercise 8.63 (ii).) Check, by choosing appropriate constants, that your solutions include straight lines perpendicular to the $x$-axis.

Now that we know (or at least guess) what the geodesics are, we can see (at least if we know a little about Möbius maps) a different way of showing this.


### ex_10_69 [ex_10_69]

We work in $\mathbb{C}^* = \mathbb{C}\cup \{\infty\}$.

(i) Show that, if $\Gamma$ is a circle with centre on the real axis, there is a $T \in \mathcal{H}$ such that $T(\Gamma)$ is a circle with centre on the real axis passing through the origin.

(ii) Show that if $\Gamma$ is a circle with centre on the real axis passing through the origin, there is a $T \in \mathcal{H}$ such that $T(\Gamma)$ is a line perpendicular to the real axis.

(iii) Show that if $\Gamma$ is a circle with centre on the real axis or a line perpendicular to the real axis, there is a $T \in \mathcal{H}$ such that $T(\Gamma)$ is the imaginary axis.

Exercise 10.66 and Exercise 10.69 show that the following two theorems are equivalent.


### thm_10_70 [thm_10_70]

The geodesic path between $iy_1$ and $iy_2$ [$y_1, y_2$ real, unequal and strictly positive] is a straight line.


### thm_10_71 [thm_10_71]

If $z_1, z_2 \in H$ with $z_1 \neq z_2$, the geodesic path between them is an arc of the circle through $z_1$ and $z_2$, unless $\Re z_1 = \Re z_2$, in which case, it is the straight line between the two points.


### ex_10_72 [ex_10_72]

Explain why Theorem 10.70 implies Theorem 10.71.

We now turn to the proof of Theorem 10.70.


### thm_10_70_proof [thm_10_70_proof]

Sketch proof. Let $Z: [0,1] \to H$ be a well-behaved function such that $Z(0) = y_{1}$ and $Z(1) = y_{2}$ with $y_{2} > y_{1}$. We write $Z(t) = X(t) + iY(t)$ with $X(t)$ and $Y(t)$ real, and take $\Gamma$ to be the path described by $Z$. We observe that

$$\begin{array}{l} \int_{\Gamma} \frac{1}{y} ds = \int_0^1 \frac{1}{Y(t)} (X'(t)^2 + Y'(t)^2)^{1/2} dt \geq \int_0^1 \frac{(Y'(t)^2)^{1/2}}{Y(t)} dt \\ = \int_0^1 \frac{|Y'(t)|}{Y(t)} dt \geq \int_0^1 \frac{Y'(t)}{Y(t)} dt = [\log Y(t)]_0^1 \\ = \log y_2 - \log y_1 = \int_{y_1}^{y_2} \frac{1}{t} dt = \int_{\Gamma_0} \frac{1}{y} ds, \end{array}$$

where $\Gamma_0$ is the straight line path from $y_{1}$ to $y_{2}$. We observe that the argument above shows that

$$\int_{\Gamma} \frac{1}{y} ds = \int_{\Gamma_0} \frac{1}{y} ds$$

only if $X'(t)^2 = 0$ and $Y'(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \in [0,1]$ and so, by simple arguments, $X(t) = 0$ and $Y'(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \in [0,1]$. Thus $\Gamma_0$ is the unique geodesic.

Remark: This is only a sketch of a proof, because we have not really decided which curves will be eligible for paths. The proof strategy of 'project the path onto the $y$-axis and compare' ought to work for any reasonable definition of line integral and any eligible path.


### ex_10_73 [ex_10_73]

We work in $\mathbb{R}^2$. Suppose that we want a metric defined by

$$d(A, B) = \inf \left\{\int_{\Gamma} g(x, y) ds: \Gamma \text{ joining } A \text{ and } B \right\},$$

which is invariant under translation and rotation. Copy the investigation above going through the following steps.

(i) By considering points which are close and working to first order, show that we should try $g$ constant. Without loss of generality take $g = 1$.

(ii) Use the calculus of variations to suggest the form of the geodesics.

(iii) Prove that your guess is correct.

(iv) Show that our metric is the usual Euclidean metric.


### ex_10_74 [ex_10_74]

In this section we defined a metric $d$ on $H$ invariant under $\mathcal{H}$, but did not calculate it. We could find $d$ by using the strategy of Exercise 10.69, but, in this exercise, we follow the easier path of allowing someone else to find the answer and then verifying it.

If $z, w \in H$, we set

$$\rho(z, w) = \log \frac{|z - w^*| + |z - w|}{|z - w^*| - |z - w|}.$$

(i) Show that $|z - w^*| - |z - w| > 0$ for all $z, w \in H$, so $\rho$ is well defined.

(ii) Show that $\rho(Tz, Tw) = \rho(z, w)$ for all $T \in \mathcal{H}$.

(iii) Show that $d(iy_1, iy_2) = \rho(iy_1, iy_2)$ for all $y_1, y_2 > 0$.

(iv) Deduce that $d = \rho$.

# Chapter 11

# 11.1. Completeness

If we examine the arguments of Section 10.3 we see that they are all mere algebra. What must we introduce to do genuine analysis on metric spaces? We cannot use a variant of the fundamental axiom because there is no order on our spaces$^1$. Instead, we use a generalisation of the general principle of convergence.


### def_11_1 [def_11_1]

If $(X,d)$ is a metric space, we say that a sequence of points $x_{n}\in X$ is Cauchy if, given any $\epsilon >0$, we can find $n_0(\epsilon)$ such that $d(x_{p},x_{q}) < \epsilon$ for all $p,q\geq n_0(\epsilon)$.


### def_11_2 [def_11_2]

A metric space $(X,d)$ is complete if every Cauchy sequence converges.

In this context, Theorem 4.68 states that $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the Euclidean metric is complete.

The contraction mapping theorem (Theorem 12.3) and its applications will provide a striking example of the utility of this concept. However, this section is devoted to providing background examples of spaces which are and are not complete.

If you want to see completeness in action immediately, you should do the next example.


### ex_11_3 [ex_11_3]

We say that a metric space $(X,d)$ has no isolated points if, given $y\in X$ and $\epsilon >0$, we can find an $x\in X$ such that $0 < d(x,y) < \epsilon$. Show by the methods of Exercise 1.41 that a complete non-empty metric space with no isolated points is uncountable.

Give an example of a countable metric space. Give an example of an uncountable metric space all of whose points are isolated.

The next lemma gives a good supply of metric spaces which are complete and of metric spaces which are not complete.


### lem_11_4 [lem_11_4]

Let $(X,d)$ be a complete metric space. If $E$ is a subset of $X$ and we define $d_E: E^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ by $d_E(u,v) = d(u,v)$ whenever $u, v \in E$, then $(E,d_E)$ is complete if and only if $E$ is closed in $(X,d)$.


### lem_11_4_proof [lem_11_4_proof]

Proof. This is just a matter of definition chasing.

Observe that any Cauchy sequence $x_{n}$ in $E$ is a Cauchy sequence in $X$ and so converges to a point $x$ in $X$. If $E$ is closed, then $x \in E$ and $d_{E}(x_{n}, x) = d(x_{n}, x) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. Thus $(E, d_{E})$ is complete whenever $E$ is closed.

Suppose now that $(E, d_E)$ is complete. If $x_n \in E$ and $d(x_n, x) \to 0$ for some $x \in X$, we know (by the argument of Lemma 4.67 if you need it) that $x_n$ is a Cauchy sequence in $X$ and so a Cauchy sequence in $E$. Since $(E, d_E)$ is complete, we can find a $y \in E$ such that $d_E(x_n, y) \to 0$. Now $d(x_n, y) = d_E(x_n, y) \to 0$, so, by the uniqueness of limits, $y = x$ and $x \in E$. Thus $E$ is closed in $(X, d)$.

Thus, for example, the closed interval $[a,b]$ is complete for the usual metric, but the open interval $(a,b)$ is not.


### ex_11_5 [ex_11_5]

Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space and $E$ a subset of $X$. Define $d_E: E^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ as in Lemma 11.4.

(i) Show that, if $E$ is not closed in $(X,d)$, then $(E,d_E)$ is not complete.

(ii) Give an example where $E$ is closed in $(X, d)$ but $(E, d_E)$ is not complete.

(iii) Give an example where $(X,d)$ is not complete but $(E,d_{E})$ is.

The reader is warned that, at least in my opinion, it is harder than it looks to prove that a metric space is or is not complete, and it is easy to produce plausible but unsatisfactory arguments in this context.

If we wish to show that a metric space $(X,d)$ is incomplete, the natural way to proceed is to find a Cauchy sequence $x_{n}$ and show that it does not converge. However, we must show that $x_{n}$ does not converge to any point in $X$, and not that $x_{n}$ does not converge to the point that it looks as though it ought to converge to'. Among the methods available for a correct proof are the following.

(1) Embed $(X,d)$ in a larger metric space $(\tilde{X},\tilde{d})$ (that is, find $(\tilde{X},\tilde{d})$ such that $\tilde{X}\supseteq X$ and $\tilde{d}(x,y) = d(x,y)$ for $x,y\in X$) and show that there is an $x\in \tilde{X}\setminus X$ such that $\tilde{d}(x_n,x)\to 0$. (See Exercise 11.5.)

(2) For each fixed $x \in X$ show that there is a $\delta(x) > 0$ and an $N(x)$ (both depending on $x$) such that $d(x_{n}, x) > \delta(x)$ for $n > N(x)$.

(3) Show that the assumption $d(x_{n},x)\to 0$ for some $x\in X$ leads to a contradiction.

Of course, no list of this sort can be exhaustive. None the less, it is good practice to ask yourself not simply whether your proof is correct but also what strategies it employs.

Here are a couple of examples.


### ex_11_6 [ex_11_6]

Consider $s_{00}$, the space of real sequences $\mathbf{a} = (a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ such that all but finitely many of the $a_n$ are zero, introduced in Exercise 10.47. The norm defined by

$$\|\mathbf{a}\|_1 = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} |a_n|$$

is not complete.


### ex_11_6_proof [ex_11_6_proof]

Proof. Set

$$\mathbf{a}(n) = \left(1, 2^{-1}, 2^{-2}, \ldots, 2^{-n}, 0, 0, \ldots\right).$$

We observe that, if $m \geq n$,

$$d(\mathbf{a}(n), \mathbf{a}(m)) = \sum_{j=n+1}^{m} 2^{-j} \leq 2^{-n} \rightarrow 0$$

as $n\to \infty$, and so the sequence $\mathbf{a}(n)$ is Cauchy.

However, if $\mathbf{a} \in s_{00}$, we know that there is an $N$ such that $a_{j} = 0$ for all $j \geq N$. It follows that

$$d(\mathbf{a}(n), \mathbf{a}) \geq 2^{-N}$$

whenever $n \geq N$, and so the sequence $\mathbf{a}(n)$ has no limit.

[The proof above used method 2. For an alternative proof using method 1, see page 255. For a generalisation of the result see Exercise K.207.]

The next example needs a result which is left to the reader to prove. (You will need Lemma 8.33.)


### ex_11_7 [ex_11_7]

Let $b > a$. Consider $C([a,b])$, the set of continuous functions $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$. If we set

$$\|f - g\|_1 = \int_a^b |f(x) - g(x)| dx,$$

show that $\|\cdot\|_1$ is a norm.


### lem_11_8 [lem_11_8]

The normed space of Exercise 11.7 is not complete.


### lem_11_8_proof [lem_11_8_proof]

Proof. This proof uses method 3. With no real loss of generality, we take $[a,b] = [-1,1]$. Let

$$\begin{array}{l} f_n(x) = -1 \quad \text{for } -1 \leq x \leq -1/n, \\ f_n(x) = nx \quad \text{for } -1/n \leq x \leq 1/n, \\ f_n(x) = 1 \quad \text{for } 1/n \leq x \leq 1. \end{array}$$

If $m\geq n$, then

$$\|f_n - f_m\|_1 = \int_{-1}^{1} |f_n(x) - f_m(x)| dx \leq \int_{-1/n}^{1/n} 1 dx = \frac{2}{n} \rightarrow 0$$

as $n\to \infty$, and so the sequence $f_{n}$ is Cauchy.

Suppose, if possible, that there exists an $f \in C([-1,1])$ such that $\|f - f_n\|_1 \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. Observe that, if $n \geq N$, we have

$$\int_{1/N}^{1} |f(x) - 1| dx = \int_{1/N}^{1} |f(x) - f_n(x)| dx \leq \int_{-1}^{1} |f(x) - f_n(x)| dx \rightarrow 0$$

as $n\to \infty$. Thus

$$\int_{1/N}^{1} |f(x) - 1| dx = 0$$

and, by Lemma 8.33, $f(x) = 1$ for $x \in [1/N, 1]$. Since $N$ is arbitrary, $f(x) = 1$ for all $0 < x \leq 1$. A similar argument shows that $f(x) = -1$ for all $-1 \leq x < 0$. Thus $f$ fails to be continuous at 0, and we have a contradiction. By reductio ad absurdum, the Cauchy sequence $f_n$ has no limit.

Lemma 11.8 is important as an indication of the unsatisfactory results of using too narrow a class of integrable functions.

The next exercise goes over very similar ground but introduces an interesting set of ideas.


### ex_11_9 [ex_11_9]

Let $b > a$. Consider $C([a,b])$, the set of continuous functions $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$. If $f, g \in C([a,b])$ and we define

$$\langle f, g \rangle = \int_a^b f(t) g(t) dt,$$

show that $(C([a,b]),\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle)$ is an inner product space. More formally, show that

(i) $\langle f, f \rangle \geq 0$ with equality if and only if $f = 0$,

(ii) $\langle f,g\rangle = \langle g,f\rangle$,

(iii) $\langle \lambda f,g\rangle = \lambda \langle f,g\rangle$,

(iv) $\langle f,g + h\rangle = \langle f,g\rangle +\langle f,h\rangle$

for all $f, g, h \in C([a,b])$ and all $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$.

Use the arguments of Lemmas 4.2 and 4.4 to show that setting

$$\|f\|_2 = \langle f, f \rangle^{1/2} = \left(\int_a^b f(t)^2 dt\right)^{1/2}$$

gives a norm on $C([a,b])$.

Show, however, that $(C([a,b]),\|\cdot\|_2)$ is not a complete normed space.

If we wish to show that a metric space $(X,d)$ is complete, the natural way to proceed is to take a Cauchy sequence $x_{n}$ and show that it must converge. However, we must show that $x_{n}$ actually converges to a point in $X$ and not that 'the sequence $x_{n}$ looks as though it ought to converge'. In many cases the proof proceeds through the following steps.

(A) The sequence $x_{n}$ converges in some sense (but not the sense we want) to an object $x$.

(B) The object $x$ actually lies in $X$.

(C) The sequence $x_{n}$ actually converges to $x$ in the sense we want.

Here is an example.


### ex_11_10 [ex_11_10]

The set $l^1$ of real sequences $\mathbf{a}$ with $\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} |a_j|$ convergent forms a vector space if we use the natural definitions of addition and scalar multiplication

$$(a_n) + (b_n) = (a_n + b_n), \quad \lambda (a_n) = (\lambda a_n).$$

If we set

$$\|\mathbf{a}\|_1 = \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} |a_j|,$$

then $(l^1, \|\cdot\|_1)$ is a complete normed space.


### ex_11_10_proof [ex_11_10_proof]

Proof. We know that the space of all sequences forms a vector space, so we only have to show that $l^1$ is a subspace. Clearly $\mathbf{0} \in l^1$, and, since $\sum_{j=1}^{N} |\lambda a_j| = |\lambda| \sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j|$, we have $\lambda \mathbf{a} \in l^1$ whenever $\mathbf{a} \in l^1$ and $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$.

Suppose $\mathbf{a}$, $\mathbf{b} \in l^1$. We have

$$\sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j + b_j| \leq \sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j| + \sum_{j=1}^{N} |b_j| \leq \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} |a_j| + \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} |b_j|,$$

so, since an increasing sequence bounded above tends to a limit, it follows that $\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} |a_j + b_j|$ converges and $\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b} \in l^1$. Hence $l^1$ is, indeed, a subspace of the space of all sequences. It is easy to check that $\|\cdot\|_1$ is a norm.

I shall label the next three paragraphs in accordance with the discussion just before this example.

Step $A$: Suppose $\mathbf{a}(n)$ is a Cauchy sequence in $(l^1, \|\cdot\|_1)$. For each fixed $j$,

$$|a_j(n) - a_j(m)| \leq \|\mathbf{a}(n) - \mathbf{a}(m)\|_1,$$

so $a_{j}(n)$ is a Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$. The general principle of convergence tells us that $a_{j}(n)$ tends to a limit $a_{j}$ as $n \to \infty$.

Step B: Since any Cauchy sequence is bounded, we can find a $K$ such that $\|\mathbf{a}(n)\|_1\leq K$ for all $n$. We observe that

$$\begin{array}{l} \sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j| \leq \sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j - a_j(n)| + \sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j(n)| \leq \sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j - a_j(n)| + \|\mathbf{a}(n)\|_1 \\ \leq \sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j - a_j(n)| + K \rightarrow K \end{array}$$

as $n \to \infty$. Thus $\sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j| \leq K$ for all $N$, and so $\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} |a_j|$ converges. We have shown that $\mathbf{a} \in l^1$.

Step $C$: We now observe that, if $n, m \geq M$,

$$\begin{array}{l} \sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j - a_j(n)| \leq \sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j - a_j(m)| + \sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j(m) - a_j(n)| \\ \leq \sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j - a_j(m)| + \|\mathbf{a}(m) - \mathbf{a}(n)\|_1 \\ \leq \sum_{j=1}^{N} |a_j - a_j(m)| + \sup_{p,q\geq M} \|\mathbf{a}(p) - \mathbf{a}(q)\|_1 \\ \to \sup_{p,q\geq M}\|\mathbf{a}(p) - \mathbf{a}(q)\|_1 \end{array}$$

as $m\to \infty$. Thus $\sum_{j=1}^{N}|a_j - a_j(n)|\leq \sup_{p,q\geq M}\|\mathbf{a}(p) - \mathbf{a}(q)\|_1$ for all $N$ and so

$$\|\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{a}(n)\|_1 \leq \sup_{p,q\geq M} \|\mathbf{a}(p) - \mathbf{a}(q)\|_1$$

for all $n \geq M$. Recalling that the sequence $\mathbf{a}(m)$ is Cauchy, we see that $\|\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{a}(n)\|_1 \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, and we are done.

The method of proof of Step C, and, in particular, the introduction of the 'irrelevant $m$' in the first set of inequalities, is very useful but requires some thought to master.


### ex_11_11 [ex_11_11]

In the proof above we stated that 'any Cauchy sequence is bounded'. Give one line proofs of the more precise statements that follow.

(i) If $(X, d)$ is a metric space, $x_{n}$ is a Cauchy sequence in $X$ and $a \in X$, then we can find a $K$ such that $d(a, x_{n}) < K$ for all $n \geq 1$.

(ii) If $(V, \|\cdot\|)$ is a normed vector space and $\mathbf{x}_n$ is a Cauchy sequence in $V$, then we can find a $K$ such that $\|\mathbf{x}_n\| < K$ for all $n \geq 1$.

Alternative proof of Example 11.6. We wish to show that $s_{00}$, with norm

$$\|\mathbf{a}\|_1 = \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} |a_j|,$$

is not complete. Observe that we can consider $s_{00}$ as a subspace of $l^1$ and that the norm on $l^1$ agrees with our norm on $s_{00}$. Now set

$$\mathbf{a}(n) = \left(1, 2^{-1}, 2^{-2}, \ldots, 2^{-n}, 0, 0, \ldots\right),$$

and

$$\mathbf{a} = (1, 2^{-1}, 2^{-2}, \ldots, 2^{-n}, 2^{-n-1}, 2^{-n-2}, \ldots).$$

Since $\mathbf{a}(n) \in s_{00}$ for all $n$ and $\|\mathbf{a}(n) - \mathbf{a}\|_1 \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, we see that $s_{00}$ is not closed in $l^1$, and so, by Exercise 11.5, $(s_{00}, \|\cdot\|_1)$ is not complete.

Here are two exercises using the method of proof of Example 11.10.


### ex_11_12 [ex_11_12]

Let $U$ be a complete vector space with norm $\|\cdot\|$. Show that the set $l^1(U)$ of sequences

$$\underline{\mathbf{u}} = (\mathbf{u}_1, \mathbf{u}_2, \mathbf{u}_3, \ldots)$$

with $\sum_{j=1}^{\infty}\|\mathbf{u}_j\|$ convergent forms a vector space if we use the natural definitions of addition and scalar multiplication

$$(\mathbf{u}_n) + (\mathbf{v}_n) = (\mathbf{u}_n + \mathbf{v}_n), \quad \lambda(\mathbf{u}_n) = (\lambda \mathbf{u}_n).$$

Show that, if we set

$$\|\underline{\mathbf{u}}\|_1 = \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} \|\mathbf{u}_j\|,$$

then $(l^1(U), \|\cdot\|_1)$ is a complete normed space.


### ex_11_13 [ex_11_13]

Show that the set $l^{\infty}$ of bounded real sequences forms a vector space if we use the usual definitions of addition and scalar multiplication.

Show further that, if we set

$$\|\mathbf{a}\|_{\infty} = \sup_{j\geq 1} |a_j|,$$

then $(l^{\infty},\|\cdot\|_{\infty})$ is a complete normed space.

[We shall prove a slight generalisation as Theorem 11.35, so the reader may wish to work through this exercise before she meets the extension.]


### ex_11_14 [ex_11_14]

Consider the set $c_0$ of real sequences $\mathbf{a} = (a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots)$ such that $a_n \to 0$. Show that $c_0$ is a subspace of $l^\infty$ and a closed subset of $(l^\infty, \|\cdot\|_\infty)$. Deduce that $(c_0, \|\cdot\|_\infty)$ is a complete normed space.

Exercise K.208 contains another example of an interesting and important infinite-dimensional complete normed space.

The final result of this section helps show why the operator norm is so useful in more advanced work.


### lem_11_15 [lem_11_15]

Let $U$ and $V$ be vector spaces with norms $\|\cdot\|_U$ and $\|\cdot\|_V$. Suppose further that $\|\cdot\|_V$ is complete. Then the operator norm is a complete norm on the space $\mathcal{L}(U, V)$ of continuous linear maps from $U$ to $V$.


### lem_11_15_proof [lem_11_15_proof]

Proof. Once again our argument falls into three steps.

Step A: Suppose that $T_{n}$ is a Cauchy sequence in $(\mathcal{L}(U,V),\|\cdot\|)$. For each fixed $\mathbf{u}\in U$,

$$\|T_n \mathbf{u} - T_m \mathbf{u}\|_V = \|(T_n - T_m)\mathbf{u}\|_V \leq \|T_n - T_m\| \|\mathbf{u}\|_V,$$

so $T_{n}\mathbf{u}$ is a Cauchy sequence in $V$. Since $V$ is complete, it follows that $T_{n}\mathbf{u}$ tends to a limit $T\mathbf{u}$, say.

Step B: In Step A we produced a map $T: U \to V$. We want to show that, in fact, $T \in \mathcal{L}(U, V)$. Observe first that, since $T_{n}$ is linear

$$\begin{array}{l} \|T(\lambda_1 \mathbf{u}_1 + \lambda_2 \mathbf{u}_2) - \lambda_1 T\mathbf{u}_1 - \lambda_2 T\mathbf{u}_2\|_V \\ = \|(T(\lambda_1 \mathbf{u}_1 + \lambda_2 \mathbf{u}_2) - \lambda_1 T\mathbf{u}_1 - \lambda_2 T\mathbf{u}_2) - (T_n(\lambda_1 \mathbf{u}_1 + \lambda_2 \mathbf{u}_2) - \lambda_1 T_n\mathbf{u}_1 - \lambda_2 T_n\mathbf{u}_2)\|_V \\ = \|(T(\lambda_1 \mathbf{u}_1 + \lambda_2 \mathbf{u}_2) - T_n(\lambda_1 \mathbf{u}_1 + \lambda_2 \mathbf{u}_2)) - \lambda_1(T\mathbf{u}_1 - T_n\mathbf{u}_1) - \lambda_2(T\mathbf{u}_2 - T_n\mathbf{u}_2)\|_V \\ \leq \|T(\lambda_1 \mathbf{u}_1 + \lambda_2 \mathbf{u}_2) - T_n(\lambda_1 \mathbf{u}_1 + \lambda_2 \mathbf{u}_2)\|_V + |\lambda_1|\|T\mathbf{u}_1 - T_n\mathbf{u}_1\|_V + |\lambda_2|\|T\mathbf{u}_2 - T_n\mathbf{u}_2\|_V \\ \to 0 \end{array}$$

as $n\to \infty$. Thus

$$\|T(\lambda_1 \mathbf{u}_1 + \lambda_2 \mathbf{u}_2) - \lambda_1 T\mathbf{u}_1 - \lambda_2 T\mathbf{u}_2\|_V = 0,$$

so

$$T(\lambda_1 \mathbf{u}_1 + \lambda_2 \mathbf{u}_2) = \lambda_1 T\mathbf{u}_1 + \lambda_2 T\mathbf{u}_2,$$

and $T$ is linear.

Next, observe that, since every Cauchy sequence is bounded, we can find a $K$ such that $\|T_n\| \leq K$ for all $n$. It follows that $\|T_{n}\mathbf{u}\|_{V}\leq K\|\mathbf{u}\|_{U}$ for each $n$. Thus

$$\|T\mathbf{u}\|_V \leq \|T\mathbf{u} - T_n\mathbf{u}\|_V + \|T_n\mathbf{u}\|_V \leq \|T\mathbf{u} - T_n\mathbf{u}\|_V + K\|\mathbf{u}\|_U \to K\|\mathbf{u}\|_U$$

as $n\to \infty$, and so $\|T\mathbf{u}\|_V\leq K\|\mathbf{u}\|_U$ for all $\mathbf{u}\in U$. Thus $T$ is continuous.

Step C: Finally we need to show that $\|T - T_{n}\| \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. To do this we use the trick of 'the irrelevant $m$' introduced in the proof of Example 11.10:

$$\begin{array}{l} \|T\mathbf{u} - T_n\mathbf{u}\|_V \leq \|T\mathbf{u} - T_m\mathbf{u}\|_V + \|T_m\mathbf{u} - T_n\mathbf{u}\|_V \\ \leq \|T\mathbf{u} - T_m\mathbf{u}\|_V + \|(T_m - T_n)\mathbf{u}\|_V \\ \leq \|T\mathbf{u} - T_m\mathbf{u}\|_V + \|T_m - T_n\|\|\mathbf{u}\|_U \\ \leq \|T\mathbf{u} - T_m\mathbf{u}\|_V + \sup_{p,q\geq M}\|T_p - T_q\|\|\mathbf{u}\|_U \\ \to \sup_{p,q\geq M}\|T_p - T_q\|\|\mathbf{u}\|_U \end{array}$$

as $m\to \infty$. Thus $\|T\mathbf{u} - T_n\mathbf{u}\|_V\leq \sup_{p,q\geq M}\|T_p - T_q\|\|\mathbf{u}\|_U$ for all $\mathbf{u}\in U$, and so

$$\|T - T_n\| \leq \sup_{p,q\geq M}\|T_p - T_q\|$$

for all $n \geq M$. Recalling that the sequence $T_{m}$ is Cauchy, we see that $\|T - T_{n}\| \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, and we are done.

Remark: In this book we are mainly concerned with the case when $U$ and $V$ are finite-dimensional. In this special case, $\mathcal{L}(U,V)$ is finite-dimensional and, since all norms on a finite-dimensional space are equivalent (Theorem 10.45), the operator norm is automatically complete.


### ex_11_16 [ex_11_16]

In Exercise 10.55, $U$ and $V$ are not complete. Give an example along the same lines involving complete normed spaces.

# 11.2. The Bolzano-Weierstrass property


## 11.2. The Bolzano-Weierstrass property [sec_11_2]


In the previous section we introduced the notion of a complete metric space as a generalisation of the general principle of convergence. The reader may ask why we did not choose to try for some generalisation of the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem instead. One answer is that it is generally agreed that the correct generalisation of the Bolzano-Weierstrass property is via the notion of compactness and that compactness is best studied in the context of topological spaces (a concept more general than metric spaces). A second answer, which the reader may find more satisfactory, is given in the discussion below which concludes in Theorem 11.23.

We make the following definition.


### def_11_17 [def_11_17]

We say that a metric space $(X,d)$ has the Bolzano-Weierstrass property if every sequence $x_{n}\in X$ has a convergent subsequence.


### lem_11_18 [lem_11_18]

A metric space $(X,d)$ with the Bolzano-Weierstrass property is complete.


### lem_11_18_proof [lem_11_18_proof]

Proof. Suppose that $x_{n}$ is a Cauchy sequence. By definition, given any $\epsilon > 0$, we can find $n_0(\epsilon)$ such that $d(x_p, x_q) < \epsilon$ for all $p, q \geq n_0(\epsilon)$. By the Bolzano-Weierstrass property, we can find $n(j) \to \infty$ and $x \in X$ such that $x_{n(j)} \to x$ as $j \to \infty$.

Thus, given any $\epsilon > 0$, we can find a $J$ such that $n(J) \geq n_0(\epsilon/2)$ and $d(x, x_{n(J)}) < \epsilon/2$. Since $n(J) \geq n_0(\epsilon/2)$, we know that, whenever $m \geq n_0(\epsilon/2)$, we have $d(x_{n(J)}, x_m) < \epsilon/2$, and so

$$d(x, x_m) \leq d(x, x_{n(J)}) + d(x_{n(J)}, x_m) < \epsilon/2 + \epsilon/2 = \epsilon.$$

Thus $x_{n}\to x$ as $n\to \infty$.


### ex_11_19 [ex_11_19]

We work in a metric space $(X,d)$.

(i) Show that if $x_{n} \to x$ as $n \to \infty$, then the sequence $x_{n}$ is Cauchy. (Any convergent sequence is Cauchy.)

(ii) If $x_{n}$ is a Cauchy sequence and we can find $n(j) \to \infty$ and $x \in X$ such that $x_{n(j)} \to x$ as $j \to \infty$, then $x_{n} \to x$. (Any Cauchy sequence with a convergent subsequence is convergent.)

To show that the converse of Lemma 11.18 is false, it suffices to consider $\mathbb{R}$ with the usual topology. To give another counterexample (in which, additionally, the metric is bounded) we introduce a dull but useful metric space.


### ex_11_20 [ex_11_20]

Let $X$ be any set. We define $d:X^2\to \mathbb{R}$ by

$$d(x, y) = 1 \quad \text{if } x \neq y,$$
$$d(x, x) = 0.$$

(i) Show that $d$ is a metric.

(ii) Show that $d(x_{n},x)\to 0$ as $n\to \infty$ if and only if there exists an $N$ such that $x_{n} = x$ for all $n\geq N$.

(iii) Show that $(X,d)$ is complete.

(iv) If $y \in X$, show that the closed unit ball $\bar{B}(y, 1) = \{x : d(x, y) \leq 1\} = X$.

(v) If $X$ is infinite, show, using (ii) or otherwise, that $X$ does not have the Bolzano-Weierstrass property.

(vi) Show also that every subset of $X$ is both open and closed.

We call the metric $d$ of the previous lemma the discrete metric.

In order to characterise metric spaces having the Bolzano-Weierstrass property, we must introduce a further definition.


### def_11_21 [def_11_21]

We say that $(X,d)$ is totally bounded if, given any $\epsilon >0$ we can find $y_{1},y_{2},\ldots ,y_{N}\in X$ such that $\bigcup_{j=1}^{N}B(y_{j},\epsilon) = X$.

In other words, $(X,d)$ is totally bounded if, given any $\epsilon >0$, we can find a finite set of open balls of radius $\epsilon$ covering $X$.


### lem_11_22 [lem_11_22]

If $(X,d)$ is a metric space with the Bolzano-Weierstrass property, then it is totally bounded.


### lem_11_22_proof [lem_11_22_proof]

Proof. If $(X,d)$ is not totally bounded, then we can find an $\epsilon >0$ such that no finite set of open balls of radius $\epsilon$ covers $X$. Choose any $x_{1}\in X$. We obtain $x_{2},x_{3},\ldots$ inductively as follows. Once $x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n}$ have been fixed, we observe that $\bigcup_{j=1}^{n}B(x_{j},\epsilon)\neq X$, so we can choose $x_{n+1}\notin \bigcup_{j=1}^{n}B(x_{j},\epsilon)$.

## 11.3. The uniform norm [sec_11_3]


Now consider the sequence $x_j$. By construction, $d(x_i, x_j) \geq \epsilon$ for all $i \neq j$, and so, if $x \in X$, we have $\max(d(x, x_i), d(x, x_j)) \geq \epsilon/2$ for all $i \neq j$. Thus the sequence $x_j$ has no convergent subsequence, and $(X, d)$ does not have the Bolzano-Weierstrass property.


### thm_11_23 [thm_11_23]

A metric space $(X, d)$ has the Bolzano-Weierstrass property if and only if it is complete and totally bounded.


### thm_11_23_proof [thm_11_23_proof]

Proof. Necessity follows from Lemmas 11.18 and 11.22. $\square$

To prove sufficiency, we first suppose that $(X, d)$ is complete and totally bounded. Let $x_n$ be a sequence in $X$. We wish to show that it has a convergent subsequence.

The key observation is contained in this paragraph. Suppose that $A$ is a subset of $X$ such that $x_n \in A$ for infinitely many values of $n$, and suppose $\epsilon > 0$. Since $X$ is totally bounded, we can find a finite set of open balls $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_M$, each of radius $\epsilon$, such that $\bigcup_{m=1}^{M} B_m = X$. It follows that $\bigcup_{m=1}^{M} A \cap B_m = A$, and, for at least one of the balls $B_m$, it must be true that $x_n \in A \cap B_m$ for infinitely many values of $n$. Thus we have shown that there is an open ball of radius $\epsilon$ such that $x_n \in A \cap B$ for infinitely many values of $n$.

It follows that we can construct inductively a sequence of open balls $B_1, B_2, \ldots$ such that $B_r$ has radius $2^{-r}$ and $x_n \in \bigcap_{s=1}^{r} B_s$ for infinitely many values of $n$ $[r = 1, 2, \ldots]$. Pick $n(1) < n(2) < n(3) < \ldots$ such that $x_{n(r)} \in \bigcap_{s=1}^{r} B_s$. If $p, q > r$, then $x_{n(p)}, x_{n(q)} \in B_r$, and so $d(x_{n(p)}, x_{n(q)}) < 2^{-r+1}$. Thus the sequence $x_{n(r)}$ is Cauchy and, since $X$ is complete, it converges.


### ex_11_24 [ex_11_24]

Show that the open interval $(0, 1)$ with the usual Euclidean metric is totally bounded but does not have the Bolzano-Weierstrass property.


### ex_11_25 [ex_11_25]

Use the completeness of the Euclidean norm on $\mathbb{R}^m$ and Theorem 11.23 to show that a closed bounded subset of $\mathbb{R}^m$ with the usual Euclidean norm has the Bolzano-Weierstrass property. (Thus we have an alternative proof of Theorem 4.16, provided we do not use Bolzano-Weierstrass to prove the completeness of $\mathbb{R}^m$.)


### ex_11_26 [ex_11_26]

Show that a metric space $(X, d)$ is totally bounded if and only if every sequence in $X$ has a Cauchy subsequence.

We shall not make a great deal of use of the concept of the Bolzano-Weierstrass property in the remainder of this book. Thus, although the results that follow are quite important, the reader should treat them merely as a revision exercise for some of the material of Section 4.3.

Our first result is a generalisation of Theorem 4.41.


### def_11_27 [def_11_27]

If $(X, d)$ is a metric space, we say that a subset $A$ has the Bolzano-Weierstrass property if the metric subspace $(A, d_A)$ (where $d_A$ is the restriction of the metric $d$ to $A$) has the Bolzano-Weierstrass property.


### ex_11_28 [ex_11_28]

(i) Let $(X, d)$ and $(Z, \rho)$ be metric spaces. Show that, if $K$ is a subset of $X$ with the Bolzano-Weierstrass property and $f: X \to Z$ is continuous, then $f(K)$ has the Bolzano-Weierstrass property.

(ii) Let $(X, d)$ be a metric space with the Bolzano-Weierstrass property and let $\mathbb{R}^p$ have the usual Euclidean norm. Show, by using part (i), or otherwise, that, if $\mathbf{f}: X \to \mathbb{R}^p$ is a continuous function, then $\mathbf{f}(K)$ is closed and bounded.


### ex_11_29 [ex_11_29]

State and prove the appropriate generalisation of Theorem 4.44.


### ex_11_30 [ex_11_30]

(This generalises Exercise 4.48.) Let $(X, d)$ be a metric space with the Bolzano-Weierstrass property. Show that if $K_1, K_2, \ldots$ are closed sets such that $K_1 \supseteq K_2 \supseteq \ldots$, then $\bigcap_{j=1}^{\infty} K_j \neq \emptyset$.

The following is a natural generalisation of Definition 4.60.


### def_11_31 [def_11_31]

Let $(X, d)$ and $(Z, \rho)$ be metric spaces. We say that a function $f: X \to Z$ is uniformly continuous if, given $\epsilon > 0$, we can find a $\delta(\epsilon) > 0$ such that, if $x, y \in X$ and $d(x, y) < \delta(\epsilon)$, we have
$$\rho(f(x), f(y)) < \epsilon.$$

The next exercise generalises Theorem 4.63.


### ex_11_32 [ex_11_32]

Let $(X, d)$ and $(Z, \rho)$ be metric spaces. If $(X, d)$ has the Bolzano-Weierstrass property, then any continuous function $f: X \to Z$ is uniformly continuous.

This section is devoted to one of the most important norms on functions. We shall write $\mathbb{F}$ to mean either $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$.


### def_11_33 [def_11_33]

If $E$ is a non-empty set, we write $\mathcal{B}(E)$ (or, more precisely, $\mathcal{B}_{\mathbb{F}}(E)$) for the set of bounded functions $f: E \to \mathbb{F}$. The uniform norm $\|\cdot\|_\infty$ on $\mathcal{B}(E)$ is defined by $\|f\|_\infty = \sup_{x \in E} |f(x)|$.


### ex_11_34 [ex_11_34]

If we use the standard operations, show that $\mathcal{B}(E)$ is a vector space over $\mathbb{F}$ and $\|\cdot\|_\infty$ is a norm.

Show also that, if $f, g \in \mathcal{B}(E)$ and we write $f \times g(x) = f(x)g(x)$, then $f \times g \in \mathcal{B}(E)$ and $\|f \times g\|_\infty \leq \|f\|_\infty \|g\|_\infty$.

The norm $\|\cdot\|_\infty$, just defined, is called the uniform norm, sup norm, or $\infty$-norm.


### thm_11_35 [thm_11_35]

The uniform norm on $\mathcal{B}(E)$ is complete.


### thm_11_35_proof [thm_11_35_proof]

Proof. This follows the model set up in Section 11.1. If the reader has done Exercise 11.13, the argument will be completely familiar.

Suppose $f_n$ is a Cauchy sequence in $(\mathcal{B}(E), \|\cdot\|_\infty)$. For each fixed $x \in E$,
$$|f_n(x) - f_m(x)| \leq \|f_n - f_m\|_\infty,$$
so $f_n(x)$ is a Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{F}$. The general principle of convergence tells us that $f_n(x)$ tends to a limit $f(x)$ as $n \to \infty$.

Since any Cauchy sequence is bounded, we can find a $K$ such that $\|f_n\|_\infty \leq K$ for all $n$. We observe that
$$|f(x)| \leq |f(x) - f_n(x)| + |f_n(x)| \leq |f(x) - f_n(x)| + \|f_n\|_\infty \leq |f(x) - f_n(x)| + K \to K$$
as $n \to \infty$. Thus $|f(x)| \leq K$ for all $x \in E$, and so $f \in \mathcal{B}(E)$.

Finally we need to show that $\|f - f_n\|_\infty \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. To do this we use the usual trick of the 'irrelevant $m$', observing that
$$|f(x) - f_n(x)| \leq |f(x) - f_m(x)| + |f_m(x) - f_n(x)| \leq |f(x) - f_m(x)| + \|f_m - f_n\|_\infty$$
$$\leq |f(x) - f_m(x)| + \sup_{p,q \geq M} \|f_p - f_q\|_\infty \to \sup_{p,q \geq M} \|f_p - f_q\|_\infty$$
as $m \to \infty$. Thus $|f(x) - f_n(x)| \leq \sup_{p,q \geq M} \|f_p - f_q\|_\infty$ for all $x \in E$, and so
$$\|f - f_n\|_\infty \leq \sup_{p,q \geq M} \|f_p - f_q\|_\infty$$
for all $n \geq M$. Recalling that the sequence $f_m$ is Cauchy, we see that $\|f - f_n\|_\infty \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, and we are done.

The space $\mathcal{B}(E)$ is not very interesting in itself, but, if $E$ is a metric space, it has a very interesting subspace.


### def_11_36 [def_11_36]

If $(E, d)$ is a non-empty metric space, we write $\mathcal{C}(E)$ (or, more precisely, $\mathcal{C}_{\mathbb{F}}(E)$) for the set of bounded continuous functions $f: E \to \mathbb{F}$.

The next remark merely restates what we already know.


### lem_11_37 [lem_11_37]

If $(E, d)$ is a non-empty metric space, then $\mathcal{C}(E)$ is a vector subspace of $\mathcal{B}(E)$. Further, if $f, g \in \mathcal{C}(E)$, the pointwise product $f \times g \in \mathcal{C}(E)$.

However, the next result is new and crucial.


### thm_11_38 [thm_11_38]

If $(E, d)$ is a non-empty metric space, then $\mathcal{C}(E)$ is a closed subset of $\mathcal{B}(E)$ under the uniform metric.

This has the famous $\epsilon/3$ proof.


### thm_11_38_proof [thm_11_38_proof]

Proof. Let $f_n \in \mathcal{C}(E)$, $f \in \mathcal{B}(E)$ and $\|f_n - f\|_\infty \to 0$. We wish to show that $f$ is continuous on $E$, and to do this it suffices to show that $f$ is continuous at any specified point $x \in E$.

Let $\epsilon > 0$ and $x \in E$ be given. Since $\|f_n - f\|_\infty \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, it follows, in particular, that there exists an $N$ with
$$\|f_N - f\|_\infty < \epsilon/3.$$

Since $f_N$ is continuous at $x$, we can find a $\delta > 0$ such that $|f_N(x) - f_N(t)| \leq \epsilon/3$ for all $t \in E$ with $d(x, t) < \delta$. It follows that
$$|f(x) - f(t)| = |(f(x) - f_N(x)) + (f_N(x) - f_N(t)) + (f_N(t) - f(t))|$$
$$\leq |f(x) - f_N(x)| + |f_N(x) - f_N(t)| + |f_N(t) - f(t)|$$
$$\leq \|f - f_N\|_\infty + |f_N(x) - f_N(t)| + \|f_N - f\|_\infty$$
$$< \epsilon/3 + \epsilon/3 + \epsilon/3 = \epsilon$$
for all $t \in E$ with $d(x, t) < \delta$.

The key to the argument above is illustrated in Figure 11.1. Suppose that $\|f - g\|_\infty < \eta$. Then $f$ is trapped in a snake of radius $\eta$ with $g$ as its backbone. In particular, if $g$ is continuous, $f$ cannot be 'terribly discontinuous'.

Since $\mathcal{C}(E)$ is a closed subset of $\mathcal{B}(E)$, Theorem 11.35 and Lemma 11.4 give us another important theorem.


### thm_11_39 [thm_11_39]

If $(E, d)$ is a non-empty metric space, then the uniform metric on $\mathcal{C}(E)$ is complete.

If $E$ is a closed bounded subset of $\mathbb{R}^m$ with the Euclidean metric (or, more generally, if $(E, d)$ is a metric space with the Bolzano-Weierstrass property), then, by Theorem 4.44 (or its easy generalisation to metric spaces with the Bolzano-Weierstrass property), all continuous functions $f: E \to \mathbb{F}$ are bounded. In these circumstances, we shall write $C(E) = \mathcal{C}(E)$ (or, more precisely, $C_{\mathbb{F}}(E) = \mathcal{C}_{\mathbb{F}}(E)$) and refer to the space $C(E)$ of continuous functions on $E$ equipped with the uniform norm.


### ex_11_40 [ex_11_40]

The question of which subsets of $C(E)$ have the Bolzano-Weierstrass property is quite hard and will not be tackled here. To get some understanding of the problem, show by considering $f_n(x) = \sin n\pi x$, or otherwise, that $\{f \in C([0,1]): \|f\|_\infty \leq 1\}$ does not have the Bolzano-Weierstrass property.

The reader has now met three different norms on $C([a, b])$ (two of them introduced in Lemma 11.8 and Exercise 11.9):
$$\|f\|_\infty = \sup_{t \in [a,b]} |f(t)|,$$
$$\|f\|_1 = \int_a^b |f(t)| \, dt,$$
$$\|f\|_2 = \left(\int_a^b |f(t)|^2 \, dt\right)^{1/2}.$$

They represent different answers to the question 'when do two continuous functions $f$ and $g$ resemble each other'. If we say that $f$ and $g$ are close only if $\|f - g\|_\infty$ is small, then, however small $|f(x) - g(x)|$ is over 'most of the range', if $|f(x) - g(x)|$ is large anywhere, we say that $f$ and $g$ are far apart. For many purposes this is too restrictive, and we would like to say that $f$ and $g$ are close if 'on average' $|f(x) - g(x)|$ is small, that is to say, $\|f - g\|_1$ is small. For a communications engineer, to whom
$$\|f\|_2^2 = \int_a^b |f(t)|^2 \, dt$$
is a measure of the power of a signal, the obvious measure of the closeness of $f$ and $g$ is $\|f - g\|_2$.

The problem of finding an appropriate measure of dissimilarity crops up in many different fields. Here are a few examples.

(a) In weather forecasting, how do we measure how close the forecast turns out to be to the true weather?

(b) In archaeology, ancient graves contain various 'grave goods'. Presumably graves which have many types of grave goods in common are close in time and those with few in common are distant in time. What is the correct measure of similarity between graves?

(c) In high definition TV and elsewhere, pictures are compressed for transmission and then reconstituted from the reduced information. How do we measure how close the final picture is to the initial one?

(d) Machines find it hard to read handwriting. People find it easy. How can we measure the difference between curves so that the same words written by different people give curves which are close but different words are far apart?

Since there are many different problems, there will be many measures of closeness. We should not be surprised that mathematicians use many different metrics and norms.

We conclude the section with a generalisation of Theorem 11.39. The proof provides an opportunity to review the contents of this section. In Exercise K.252 we use the result with $V = \mathbb{R}^2$.


### ex_11_41 [ex_11_41]

Let $(E, d)$ be a non-empty metric space and $(V, \|\cdot\|)$ a complete normed space. We write $\mathcal{C}_V(E)$ for the set of bounded continuous functions $\mathbf{f}: E \to V$, and set
$$\|\mathbf{f}\|_\infty = \sup_{x \in E} \|\mathbf{f}(x)\|_V$$
whenever $\mathbf{f} \in \mathcal{C}_V(E)$. Show that $(\mathcal{C}_V(E), \|\cdot\|_\infty)$ is a complete normed vector space.


## 11.4. Uniform convergence [sec_11_4]


Traditionally, the material of the previous section has been presented in a different but essentially equivalent manner.


### def_11_42 [def_11_42]

(Uniform convergence.) If $E$ is a non-empty set and $f_n: E \to \mathbb{F}$ and $f: E \to \mathbb{F}$ are functions, we say that $f_n$ converges uniformly to $f$ as $n \to \infty$ if, given any $\epsilon > 0$, we can find an $n_0(\epsilon)$ with the property that $|f_n(x) - f(x)| < \epsilon$ for all $x \in E$ and all $n \geq n_0(\epsilon)$.

**Remark 1:** For a generalisation see Exercise K.226.

**Remark 2:** On page 64 we placed the definition of uniform continuity in parallel with the definition of continuity. In the same way, the reader should compare the definition of uniform convergence with the notion of convergence that we have used so far and which we shall now call pointwise convergence.


### def_11_43 [def_11_43]

(Pointwise convergence.) If $E$ is a non-empty set and $f_n: E \to \mathbb{F}$ and $f: E \to \mathbb{F}$ are functions, we say that $f_n$ converges pointwise to $f$ as $n \to \infty$ if, given any $\epsilon > 0$ and any $x \in E$, we can find an $n_0(\epsilon, x)$ with the property that $|f_n(x) - f(x)| < \epsilon$ for all $n \geq n_0(\epsilon, x)$.

Once again, 'uniform' means independent of the choice of $x$.

Theorem 11.38 now takes the following form.


### thm_11_44 [thm_11_44]

If $(E, d)$ is a non-empty metric space and $f_n: E \to \mathbb{F}$ forms a sequence of continuous functions tending uniformly to $f$, then $f$ is continuous.

More briefly, the uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous.


### thm_11_44_proof [thm_11_44_proof]

Proof of Theorem 11.44 from Theorem 11.38. The problem we must face is that the $f_n$ need not be bounded.

To get round this, choose $N$ such that $|f_N(x) - f(x)| < 1$ for all $x \in E$ and all $n \geq N$. If we set $g_n = f_n - f_N$, then
$$|g_n(x)| \leq |f_n(x) - f(x)| + |f_N(x) - f(x)| < 2,$$
and so $g_n \in \mathcal{C}(E)$ for all $n \geq N$. If we set $g = f - f_N$, then $g \in \mathcal{B}(E)$, and
$$\|g_n - g\|_\infty = \sup_{x \in E} |g_n(x) - g(x)| = \sup_{x \in E} |f_n(x) - f(x)| \to 0.$$

By Theorem 11.38, $g \in \mathcal{C}(E)$, and so $f = g + f_N$ is continuous.

The same kind of simple argument applied to Theorem 11.39 gives the so-called 'general principle of uniform convergence'.


### thm_11_45 [thm_11_45]

(General principle of uniform convergence.) Suppose that $(E, d)$ is a non-empty metric space and $f_n: E \to \mathbb{F}$ is a continuous function $[n \geq 1]$. The sequence $f_n$ converges uniformly to a continuous function $f$ if and only if, given any $\epsilon > 0$, we can find an $n_0(\epsilon)$ such that $|f_n(x) - f_m(x)| < \epsilon$ for all $n$, $m \geq n_0(\epsilon)$ and all $x \in E$.

This theorem is also known as the GPUC by those who do not object to theorems which sound as though they were a branch of the secret police.


### ex_11_46 [ex_11_46]

Prove Theorem 11.45 from Theorem 11.39.


### ex_11_47 [ex_11_47]

(i) Prove Theorem 11.44 and Theorem 11.45 directly. (Of course, the proofs will be very similar to those of Theorem 11.38 and Theorem 11.35. If you prefer simply to look things up, proofs are given in practically every analysis textbook.)

(ii) Prove Theorem 11.38 from Theorem 11.44 and Theorem 11.39 from Theorem 11.45.

The next two examples are very important for understanding the difference between pointwise and uniform convergence.


### ex_11_48 [ex_11_48]

(The witch's hat.) Define $f_n: [0,2] \to \mathbb{R}$ by
$$f_n(x) = 1 - n|x - n^{-1}| \quad \text{for } |x - n^{-1}| \leq n^{-1},$$
$$f_n(x) = 0 \quad \text{otherwise.}$$

Then the $f_n$ are continuous functions such that $f_n(x) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ for each $x$ but $f_n \not\to 0$ uniformly.

More briefly, pointwise convergence does not imply uniform convergence.


### ex_11_48_proof [ex_11_48_proof]

Proof. Observe that, if $x \neq 0$, then $x \geq 2N^{-1}$ for some positive integer $N$, and so, provided $n \geq N$,
$$f_n(x) = 0 \to 0$$
as $n \to \infty$. On the other hand, if $x = 0$,
$$f_n(0) = 0 \to 0$$
as $n \to \infty$. Thus $f_n(x) \to 0$ for each $x \in [0, 2]$.

We observe that $\|f_n\|_\infty \geq f_n(n^{-1}) = 1 \not\to 0$, so $f_n \not\to 0$ uniformly as $n \to 0$.


### ex_11_49 [ex_11_49]

Define $f_n: [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ by $f_n(x) = x^n$. Then $f_n(x) \to f(x)$ as $n \to \infty$, where $f(x) = 0$ for $0 \leq x < 1$ but $f(1) = 1$.

Thus the pointwise limit of continuous functions need not be continuous.


### ex_11_49_proof [ex_11_49_proof]

We leave the verification to the reader.


### ex_11_50 [ex_11_50]

Draw a diagram to illustrate Example 11.49 and prove the result.

Uniform convergence is a very useful tool when dealing with integration.


### thm_11_51 [thm_11_51]

Let $f_n \in C([a, b])$. If $f_n \to f$ uniformly, then $f \in C([a, b])$ and
$$\int_a^b f_n(x) \, dx \to \int_a^b f(x) \, dx.$$

Students often miss the full force of this theorem because it is so easy to prove. Note, however, that we need to prove that the second integral actually exists. (You should look briefly at Exercise 9.1 before proceeding. If you want an example of a sequence of continuous functions whose pointwise limit is not Riemann integrable, consult the harder Exercise K.175.)


### thm_11_51_proof [thm_11_51_proof]

Proof. Since $f$ is the uniform limit of continuous functions, it is itself continuous and therefore Riemann integrable. By the sup $\times$ length inequality,
$$\left|\int_a^b f(t) \, dt - \int_a^b f_n(t) \, dt\right| = \left|\int_a^b (f(t) - f_n(t)) \, dt\right| \leq \int_a^b |f(t) - f_n(t)| \, dt$$
$$\leq (b-a) \|f - f_n\|_\infty \to 0$$
as $n \to \infty$.

Theorem 11.51 should be considered in the context of the following two examples.


### ex_11_52 [ex_11_52]

(The tall witch's hat.) Define $f_n: [0,2] \to \mathbb{R}$ by
$$f_n(x) = n(1 - n|x - n^{-1}|) \quad \text{for } |x - n^{-1}| \leq n^{-1},$$
$$f_n(x) = 0 \quad \text{otherwise.}$$

Then the $f_n$ are continuous functions such that $f_n(x) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ but
$$\int_0^2 f_n(x) \, dx \not\to 0$$
as $n \to \infty$.


### ex_11_53 [ex_11_53]

(Escape to infinity.) Define $f_n: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ by
$$f_n(x) = n^{-1}(1 - n^{-1}|x|) \quad \text{for } |x| \leq n,$$
$$f_n(x) = 0 \quad \text{otherwise.}$$

Then the $f_n$ are continuous functions such that $f_n(x) \to 0$ uniformly as $n \to \infty$ but
$$\int_{-\infty}^\infty f_n(x) \, dx \not\to 0$$
as $n \to \infty$.

[We gave a similar example of escape to infinity in Exercise 5.24.]


### ex_11_54 [ex_11_54]

(i) Draw a diagram to illustrate Example 11.52 and prove the result.

(ii) Draw a diagram to illustrate Example 11.53 and prove the result.

One way of contrasting Theorem 11.51 with Example 11.53 is to think of pushing a piston down a cylinder with water at the bottom. Eventually we must stop because the water has nowhere to escape to. However, if we place a glass sheet on top of another glass sheet, any water between them escapes outwards.

The following example, which requires some knowledge of probability theory, illustrates the importance of the phenomenon exhibited in Example 11.53.


### ex_11_55 [ex_11_55]

Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ be independent identically distributed random variables. Suppose further that $X_1$ has continuous density distribution $f$. We know that $X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n$ then has a continuous distribution $f_n$.

(i) Explain why $\int_{-\infty}^\infty f_n(x) \, dx = 1$.

(ii) In the particular case $f(x) = (2\pi)^{-1/2} \exp(-x^2/2)$, state the value of $f_n(x)$ and show that $f_n(x) \to 0$ everywhere. Thus
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \int_{-\infty}^\infty f_n(x) \, dx \neq \int_{-\infty}^\infty \lim_{n \to \infty} f_n(x) \, dx.$$

(iii) If $Y$ is a real-valued random variable with continuous density distribution $g_Y$ and $a > 0$, show that $aY$ has continuous density distribution $g_{aY}$ given by $g_{aY}(x) = a^{-1}g(a^{-1}x)$. What is the density for $-aY$?

(iv) In the particular case investigated in part (ii), show that

(a) If $1/2 > \alpha \geq 0$, then $n^\alpha f_n(n^\alpha x) \to 0$ uniformly on $\mathbb{R}$ as $n \to \infty$ and
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \int_{-\infty}^\infty n^\alpha f_n(n^\alpha x) \, dx \neq \int_{-\infty}^\infty \lim_{n \to \infty} n^\alpha f_n(n^\alpha x) \, dx.$$

(b) If $\alpha = 1/2$, then $n^\alpha f_n(n^\alpha x) = f(x)$ and
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \int_{-\infty}^\infty n^\alpha f_n(n^\alpha x) \, dx = \int_{-\infty}^\infty \lim_{n \to \infty} n^\alpha f_n(n^\alpha x) \, dx.$$

(c) If $\alpha > 1/2$, then $n^\alpha f_n(n^\alpha x) \to 0$ for each $x \neq 0$ as $n \to \infty$, but $n^\alpha f_n(n^\alpha \cdot 0) \to \infty$.

(v) Draw diagrams illustrating the three cases in (iv) and give a probabilistic interpretation in each case.

(vi) How much further you can go, for general $f$, depends on your knowledge of probability. If you know any of the terms Tchebychev inequality, central limit theorem, or Cauchy distribution, discuss how they apply here.

In any case, I hope I have demonstrated that when talking about things like
$$\frac{X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n}{n^\alpha}$$
we expect the interchange of limits only to work in exceptional (but therefore profoundly interesting) cases.


### ex_11_56 [ex_11_56]

As I have already noted, we gave a similar example to Example 11.53 in Exercise 5.24. We followed that by a dominated convergence theorem for sums (Lemma 5.25). Can you formulate a similar dominated convergence theorem for integrals? A possible version is given as Exercise K.245.

Traditionally, Theorem 11.51 is always paired with the following result which is proved by showing that it is really a disguised theorem on integration!


### thm_11_57 [thm_11_57]

Suppose that $f_n: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable on $[a, b]$ with continuous derivative $f_n'$ (we take the one-sided derivative at end points as usual). Suppose that $f_n(x) \to f(x)$ as $n \to \infty$ for each $x \in [a, b]$ and suppose that $f_n'$ converges uniformly to a limit $F$ on $[a, b]$. Then $f$ is differentiable with derivative $F$.


### thm_11_57_proof_1 [thm_11_57_proof_1]

First proof. Since $f_n'$ is continuous and $f_n' \to F$ uniformly, $F$ is continuous and Theorem 11.51 tells us that
$$\int_c^t f_n'(x) \, dx \to \int_c^t F(x) \, dx$$
as $n \to \infty$ for all $t, c \in [a, b]$. By the fundamental theorem of the calculus (in the form of Theorem 8.42), we know that $\int_c^t f_n'(x) \, dx = f_n(t) - f_n(c)$ and so
$$f(t) - f(c) = \int_c^t F(x) \, dx.$$

Since $F$ is continuous, another application of the fundamental theorem of the calculus (this time in the form of Theorem 8.37) tells us that $f$ is differentiable with
$$f'(t) = F(t),$$
as required.

This proof is easy but rather roundabout. We present a second proof which is harder but much more direct.


### thm_11_57_proof_2 [thm_11_57_proof_2]

Second proof of Theorem 11.57. Our object in this proof is to show that $|f(x + h) - f(x) - F(x)h|$ decreases faster than linearly as $h \to 0$ [when $x, x + h \in [a, b]$]. We start in an obvious way by observing that
$$|f(x + h) - f(x) - F(x)h| \leq |f_n(x + h) - f_n(x) - f_n'(x)h|$$
$$+ |(f(x + h) - f(x) - F(x)h) - (f_n(x + h) - f_n(x) - f_n'(x)h)|.$$

The first term in the inequality can be estimated by the mean value inequality:
$$|f_n(x + h) - f_n(x) - f_n'(x)h| \leq |h| \sup_{0 < \theta < 1} |f_n'(x + \theta h) - f_n'(x)|.$$

To estimate $\sup_{0 < \theta < 1} |f_n'(x + \theta h) - f_n'(x)|$, we reverse the argument of the '$\epsilon/3$ theorem' (Theorem 11.38). We know that $F$ is continuous because it is the uniform limit of continuous functions. Thus, given $\epsilon > 0$, we can find a
$\delta(\epsilon) > 0$ such that $|F(y) - F(x)| < \epsilon/3$ whenever $y \in [a, b]$ and $|x - y| < \delta(\epsilon)$. Choosing an $N(\epsilon)$ such that $\|f_n' - F\|_\infty < \epsilon/3$ for all $n \geq N(\epsilon)$, we have
$$|f_n'(y) - f_n'(x)| \leq |f_n'(y) - F(y)| + |F(y) - F(x)| + |F(x) - f_n'(x)|$$
$$\leq 2\|f_n' - F\|_\infty + |F(y) - F(x)| < 2\epsilon/3 + \epsilon/3 = \epsilon$$
for all $y \in [a, b]$ with $|x - y| < \delta(\epsilon)$ and all $n \geq N(\epsilon)$. Using this result, we see that the inequality gives
$$|f_n(x + h) - f_n(x) - f_n'(x)h| \leq \epsilon|h|,$$
so that
$$|f(x + h) - f(x) - F(x)h| \leq |(f(x + h) - f(x) - F(x)h) - (f_n(x + h) - f_n(x) - f_n'(x)h)| + \epsilon|h|$$
for all $x + h \in [a, b]$ with $|h| < \delta(\epsilon)$, and all $n \geq N(\epsilon)$. Allowing $n \to \infty$, we obtain
$$|f(x + h) - f(x) - F(x)h| \leq \epsilon|h|,$$
for all $x + h \in [a, b]$ with $|h| < \delta(\epsilon)$, and this is what we want.

One major advantage of the second proof is that it generalises easily to many dimensions.


### ex_11_58 [ex_11_58]

Let $\Omega$ be an open set in $\mathbb{R}^m$. Suppose that $\mathbf{f}_n: \Omega \to \mathbb{R}^p$ is a differentiable function on $\Omega$ with continuous derivative $D\mathbf{f}_n$. Suppose that $\mathbf{f}_n(\mathbf{x}) \to \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ as $n \to \infty$ for each $\mathbf{x} \in \Omega$ and suppose that there is a function $\Theta: \Omega \to \mathcal{L}(\mathbb{R}^m, \mathbb{R}^p)$ (that is, $\Theta$ is a function on $\Omega$ whose values are linear maps from $\mathbb{R}^m$ to $\mathbb{R}^p$) such that
$$\sup_{\mathbf{x} \in \Omega} \|\Theta(\mathbf{x}) - D\mathbf{f}_n(\mathbf{x})\| \to 0$$
(that is, $D\mathbf{f}_n$ converges uniformly to $\Theta$ in the operator norm) as $n \to \infty$. Then $\mathbf{f}$ is differentiable with derivative $\Theta$.

Although Exercise 11.58 is not hard, it provides a useful exercise in understanding notation. We note, for example, that $D\mathbf{f}_n$ is a function on $\Omega$ whose values are linear maps from $\mathbb{R}^m$ to $\mathbb{R}^p$. The statement that $D\mathbf{f}_n$ is continuous must therefore be interpreted as
$$\|D\mathbf{f}_n(\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{h}) - D\mathbf{f}_n(\mathbf{x})\| \to 0$$
as $\|\mathbf{h}\|_2 \to 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in \Omega$, where $\|\cdot\|_2$ is the Euclidean norm on $\mathbb{R}^m$ and $\|\cdot\|$ is the operator norm on $\mathcal{L}(\mathbb{R}^m, \mathbb{R}^p)$.


### ex_11_59 [ex_11_59]

(Easy but optional.) Rewrite Exercise 11.58 as a theorem on differentiation of functions between general normed vector spaces in accordance with Definition 10.58. Check that essentially the same proof works in this more general case.

The reader knows how to turn results on the limits of sequences into results on infinite sums and vice versa (see Exercise 4.72 if necessary). Applied to Theorems 11.51 and 11.57, the technique produces the following results.


### thm_11_60 [thm_11_60]

(Term by term integration.) Let $g_j: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. If $\sum_{j=1}^n g_j$ converges uniformly as $n \to \infty$, then
$$\int_a^b \sum_{j=1}^\infty g_j(x) \, dx = \sum_{j=1}^\infty \int_a^b g_j(x) \, dx.$$


### thm_11_61 [thm_11_61]

(Term by term differentiation.) Let $g_j: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable with continuous derivative. If $\sum_{j=1}^n g_j(x)$ converges for each $x$ and $\sum_{j=1}^n g_j'$ converges uniformly as $n \to \infty$, then $\sum_{j=1}^\infty g_j$ is differentiable and
$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\sum_{j=1}^\infty g_j(x)\right) = \sum_{j=1}^\infty g_j'(x).$$

As a typical example of the use of Theorem 11.57, we use it to extend Theorem 8.57 on differentiation under the integral to a much more useful result.


### thm_11_62 [thm_11_62]

(Differentiation under an infinite integral.) Let $(c', d') \supseteq [c, d]$. Suppose $g: [0, \infty) \times (c', d') \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and that the partial derivative $g_{,2}$ exists and is continuous. Suppose further, that there exists a continuous function $h: [0, \infty) \times (c, d) \to \mathbb{R}$ with $|g_{,2}(x, y)| \leq h(x)$ for all $(x, y)$ and such that $\int_0^\infty h(x) \, dx$ exists and is finite. Then, if $G(y) = \int_0^\infty g(x, y) \, dx$ exists for all $y \in (c, d)$, we have $G$ differentiable on $(c, d)$ with
$$G'(y) = \int_0^\infty g_{,2}(x, y) \, dx.$$


### thm_11_62_proof [thm_11_62_proof]

Proof. Note that $H(y) = \int_0^\infty g_{,2}(x, y) \, dx$ exists by comparison (Lemma 9.6). Set $G_n(y) = \int_0^n g(x, y) \, dx$. By Theorem 8.57, $G_n$ is differentiable with
$$G_n'(y) = \int_0^n g_{,2}(x, y) \, dx.$$

Since
$$|G_n'(y) - H(y)| = \left|\int_n^\infty g_{,2}(x, y) \, dx\right| \leq \int_n^\infty h(x) \, dx \to 0,$$
we see that $G_n'$ converges uniformly to $H$ on $(c, d)$. By hypothesis, $G_n(y) \to G(y)$ on $(c, d)$, so, by Theorem 11.57, $G$ is differentiable with derivative $H$ on $(c, d)$. This is the required result.

You should be careful when using this theorem to check that the hypotheses actually apply. Exercise K.243 illustrates what can go wrong if we do not prevent 'escape to infinity'.


## 11.5. Power series [sec_11_5]


The object of this section and the next is to show how the notion of uniform convergence is used in two topics of practical importance.

We make use of a result which is too trivial to constitute a theorem but too useful to leave unnamed.


### lem_11_63 [lem_11_63]

(The Weierstrass M-test.) Consider functions $f_n: E \to \mathbb{C}$. Suppose that we can find positive real numbers $M_n$ such that $|f_n(x)| \leq M_n$ for all $x \in E$ and all $n \geq 1$. If $\sum_1^\infty M_n$ converges, then $\sum_1^\infty f_n$ converges uniformly on $E$.


### lem_11_63_proof [lem_11_63_proof]

Proof. Let $\epsilon > 0$. By the easy part of the general principle of convergence we can find an $N_0(\epsilon)$ such that $\sum_{r=n}^m M_n \leq \epsilon$ for all $m \geq n \geq N_0(\epsilon)$. It follows that
$$\left|\sum_{r=n}^m f_n(x)\right| \leq \sum_{r=n}^m |f_n(x)| \leq \sum_{r=n}^m M_n \leq \epsilon$$
for all $m \geq n \geq N_0(\epsilon)$ and all $x \in E$. By the general principle of uniform convergence (Theorem 11.45), $\sum_1^\infty f_n$ converges uniformly on $E$.

This book has been mainly about real analysis. When we talked about functions from $\mathbb{R}^n$ to $\mathbb{R}^m$ we made a point of the fact that we cannot divide a vector by a vector. There are, however, two exceptions to this rule. The first is that $\mathbb{R}$, itself, is a vector space where division is permitted. The second is that, if we give $\mathbb{R}^2$ the algebraic structure of $\mathbb{C}$, we again obtain a system in which division is permitted. This enables us to develop a theory of differentiation for functions $f: \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C}$ running in parallel with the theory of one-dimensional real differentiation. Note that we use the usual metric $d(z_1, z_2) = |z_1 - z_2|$ throughout.


### def_11_64 [def_11_64]

Let $\Omega$ be an open set in $\mathbb{C}$ and let $z \in \Omega$. We say that $f: \Omega \to \mathbb{C}$ is differentiable at $z$ with derivative $f'(z)$ if
$$\left|\frac{f(z + h) - f(z)}{h} - f'(z)\right| \to 0$$
as $h \to 0$.


### ex_11_65 [ex_11_65]

Check that the definition is equivalent to the statement
$$f(z + h) = f(z) + f'(z)h + \epsilon(h)|h|$$
for $z + h \in \Omega$, where $\epsilon(h) \to 0$ as $h \to 0$.


### ex_11_66 [ex_11_66]

Convince yourself that the elementary theory of complex differentiation is the same as that of real differentiation. For example, you could run through the general results leading to the following result:

Let $a_r \in \mathbb{C}$ $[0 \leq r \leq N]$, $b_s \in \mathbb{C}$ $[0 \leq s \leq M - 1]$, $b_M = 1$, and let $P(z) = \sum_{r=0}^N a_r z^r$, $Q(z) = \sum_{s=0}^M b_s z^s$. Then $\Omega = \{z \in \mathbb{C} : Q(z) \neq 0\}$ is open and the quotient $P/Q: \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C}$ is everywhere differentiable.

Exercise 11.66 is routine and not meant to be taken very seriously. The next two results are important in their own right and provide an opportunity to review the content and proof of two important earlier results.


### ex_11_67 [ex_11_67]

(A mean value inequality.) Suppose that $\Omega$ is an open set in $\mathbb{C}$ and that $f: \Omega \to \mathbb{C}$ is differentiable at all points of $\Omega$. Suppose, further, that the straight line segment
$$L = \{(1 - t)z_1 + tz_2: 0 \leq t \leq 1\}$$
joining $z_1$ and $z_2$ lies in $\Omega$ and that
$$|f'(z)| \leq K$$
for all $z \in L$.

Explain why we can find a real $\theta$ such that $e^{i\theta}(f(z_2) - f(z_1))$ is real and positive. Show that the function $F: [0, 1] \to \mathbb{R}$ given by
$$F(t) = \Re\left(e^{i\theta}\left(f((1 - t)z_1 + tz_2) - f(z_2)\right)\right)$$
is differentiable on $(0, 1)$, and find its derivative. By applying some form of mean value theorem or mean value inequality (many versions will work) to $F$, show that
$$|f(z_2) - f(z_1)| \leq K|z_2 - z_1|.$$


### ex_11_68 [ex_11_68]

By using the ideas of the second proof of Theorem 11.57, prove the following result.

Let $\Omega$ be an open set in $\mathbb{C}$. Suppose that $f_n: \Omega \to \mathbb{C}$ is differentiable at all points of $\Omega$ with continuous derivative $f_n'$. Suppose that $f_n(z) \to f(z)$ as $n \to \infty$ for each $z \in \Omega$, and suppose that there is a function $g: \Omega \to \mathbb{C}$ such that
$$\sup_{z \in \Omega} |g(z) - f_n'(z)| \to 0$$
(that is, $f_n'$ converges uniformly to $g$). Then $f$ is differentiable at all points of $\Omega$ with derivative $g$.

To use this result we need to recall the definition of the radius of convergence of a power series and the proof that makes the definition possible. The reader can look these things up on page 70, but it would, I think, be helpful to redo the work as an exercise.


### ex_11_69 [ex_11_69]

(i) Suppose that $a_n \in \mathbb{C}$. Show that if $\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n z_0^n$ converges for some $z_0 \in \mathbb{C}$, then $\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n z^n$ converges for all $z \in \mathbb{C}$ with $|z| < |z_0|$.

(ii) Use (i) to show that either $\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n z^n$ converges for all $z$ (in which case we say the series has infinite radius of convergence) or there exists an $R \geq 0$ such that $\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n z^n$ converges for $|z| < R$ and diverges for $|z| > R$ (in which case we say that the series has radius of convergence $R$).

We can now improve this result slightly.


### lem_11_70 [lem_11_70]

Suppose that $a_n \in \mathbb{C}$ and $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z_0^n$ has radius of convergence $R$. If $0 \leq \rho < R$, then $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ converges uniformly for all $|z| \leq \rho$.


### lem_11_70_proof [lem_11_70_proof]

Choose $z_0$ with $\rho < |z_0| < R$. We know that $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z_0^n$ converges, and so $a_n z_0^n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. It follows that there exists an $M$ such that $|a_n z_0^n| \leq M$ for all $n \geq 1$. Thus, if $|z| \leq \rho$, we have

$$|a_n z^n| = |a_n z_0^n| \frac{|z|^n}{|z_0|^n} \leq M \left(\frac{\rho}{|z_0|}\right)^n,$$

and so, by the Weierstrass M-test, $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ converges uniformly for all $|z| \leq \rho$.


### ex_11_71 [ex_11_71]

Show that $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} z^n$ has radius of convergence 1 but that $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} z^n$ does not converge uniformly for $|z| < 1$.

Thus a power series converges uniformly in any disc with centre 0 of strictly smaller radius than the radius of convergence, but, in general, the condition strictly smaller cannot be dropped.

The next result is also easy to prove.


### lem_11_72 [lem_11_72]

Suppose that $a_n \in \mathbb{C}$ and $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ has radius of convergence $R$. Then $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n a_n z^{n-1}$ also has radius of convergence $R$.


### lem_11_72_proof [lem_11_72_proof]

Let $|w| < R$. Choose $w_0 \in \mathbb{C}$ with $|w| < |w_0| < R$ and $\rho \in \mathbb{R}$ with $|w| < \rho < |w_0|$. We know that $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n w_0^n$ converges, so, arguing as before, there exists an $M$ such that $|a_n w_0^n| \leq M$ for all $n \geq 0$. Thus

$$|n a_n \rho^{n-1}| = |w_0|^{-1} |a_n w_0^n| \frac{n\rho^{n-1}}{|w_0|^{n-1}} \leq |w_0|^{-1} M n \left(\frac{\rho}{|w_0|}\right)^{n-1} \to 0.$$

(We use the fact that, if $|x| < 1$, then $nx^n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. There are many proofs of this, including Exercise K.9.) Thus we can find an $M'$ such that $|na_n\rho^{n-1}| \leq M'$ for all $n \geq 1$. Our usual argument now shows that $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} na_n w^{n-1}$ converges.

We have shown that the radius of convergence of $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n a_n z^{n-1}$ is at least $R$. An easier version of the same argument shows that if $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n a_n z^{n-1}$ has radius of convergence $S$, then the radius of convergence of $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ is at least $S$. Thus the radius of convergence of $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n a_n z^{n-1}$ is exactly $R$.

We can now combine Exercise 11.68, Lemma 11.70 and Lemma 11.72 to obtain our main result on power series.


### thm_11_73 [thm_11_73]

Suppose that $a_n \in \mathbb{C}$ and $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ has radius of convergence $R > 0$. Set $\Omega = \{z : |z| < R\}$ (if $R = \infty$, then $\Omega = \mathbb{C}$) and define $f: \Omega \to \mathbb{C}$ by

$$f(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n.$$

Then $f$ is everywhere differentiable on $\Omega$, and

$$f'(z) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n a_n z^{n-1}.$$

More briefly, a power series can be differentiated term by term within its circle of convergence.


### thm_11_73_proof [thm_11_73_proof]

We wish to show that, if $|w| < R$, then $f$ is differentiable at $w$ with the appropriate derivative. To this end, choose a $\rho$ with $|w| < \rho < R$. By Lemma 11.70, we know that

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N} a_n z^n \to f(z)$$

uniformly for $|z| \leq \rho$. By Lemma 11.70, we know that there exists a function $g: \Omega \to \mathbb{C}$ such that $\sum_{n=1}^{N} na_n z^{n-1} \to g(z)$ as $N \to \infty$ for all $z \in \Omega$. Using Lemma 11.70 again, we have

$$\sum_{n=1}^{N} n a_n z^{n-1} \to g(z)$$

uniformly for $|z| \leq \rho$. Since

$$\frac{d}{dz}\left(\sum_{n=0}^{N} a_n z^n\right) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} n a_n z^{n-1},$$

Exercise 11.68 now tells us that $f$ is differentiable in $\{z : |z| < \rho\}$ with

$$f'(z) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n a_n z^{n-1}.$$

Since $|w| < \rho$, we are done.


### rem_11_73 [rem_11_73]

The proof above is more cunning than is at first apparent. Roughly speaking, it is often hard to prove directly that $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ has a certain property for all $|z| < R$, the radius of convergence, but relatively easy to show that $\sum_{n=0}^{N} a_n z^n$ has a certain property for all $|z| < R'$, whenever $R' < R$. However, if we choose $R_1 < R_2 < \ldots$ with $R_N \to R$, we then know that $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ will have the property for all

$$z \in \bigcup_{r=1}^{\infty} \{z : |z| < R_N\} = \{z : |z| < R\},$$

and we are done. (We give two alternative proofs of Theorem 11.73 in Exercise K.258 and Exercise K.260.)

Here are two useful corollaries.


### ex_11_74 [ex_11_74]

Suppose that $a_n \in \mathbb{C}$ and $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ has radius of convergence $R > 0$. Set $\Omega = \{z : |z| < R\}$ and define $f: \Omega \to \mathbb{C}$ by

$$f(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n.$$

Show that $f$ is infinitely differentiable on $\Omega$ and $a_n = f^{(n)}(0) / n!$.

In other words, if $f$ can be expanded in a power series about 0, then that power series must be the Taylor series.


### ex_11_75 [ex_11_75]

(Uniqueness of power series.) Suppose that $a_n \in \mathbb{C}$, and $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ has radius of convergence $R > 0$. Set $\Omega = \{z : |z| < R\}$ and define $f: \Omega \to \mathbb{C}$ by

$$f(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n.$$

If there exists a $\delta$ with $0 < \delta \leq R$ such that $f(z) = 0$ for all $|z| < \delta$, show, by using the preceding exercise, or otherwise, that $a_n = 0$ for all $n \geq 0$. [In Exercise K.268 we give a stronger result with a more direct proof.]

By restricting our attention to the real axis, we can obtain versions of all these results for real power series.


### lem_11_76 [lem_11_76]

Suppose that $a_n \in \mathbb{R}$.

(i) Either $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n x^n$ converges for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ (in which case we say the series has infinite radius of convergence), or there exists an $R \geq 0$ such that $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n x^n$ converges for $|x| < R$ and diverges for $|x| > R$ (in which case we say the series has radius of convergence $R$).

(ii) If $0 \leq \rho < R$, then $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n x^n$ converges uniformly on $[-\rho, \rho]$.

(iii) The sum $f(x) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n x^n$ is differentiable, term by term, on $(-R, R)$.

(iv) If $R > 0$, then $f$ is infinitely differentiable and $a_n = f^{(n)}(0) / n!$.

(v) If $f$ vanishes on $(-\delta, \delta)$ where $0 < \delta \leq R$, then $a_n = 0$ for all $n$.

Part (iv) should be read in conjunction with Cauchy's example of a well behaved function with no power series expansion round 0 (Example 7.5).

The fact that we can differentiate a power series term by term is important for two reasons. The first is that there is a very beautiful and useful theory of differentiable functions from $\mathbb{C}$ to $\mathbb{C}$ (called 'complex variable theory' or 'the theory of analytic functions'). In the initial development of the theory it is not entirely clear that there are any interesting functions for the theory to talk about. Power series provide such interesting functions.

The second reason is that it provides a rigorous justification for the use of power series in the solution of differential equations by methods of the type employed on page 89.


### ex_11_77 [ex_11_77]

(i) Show that the sum $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^n}{n!}$ has infinite radius of convergence.

(ii) Let us set

$$e(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^n}{n!}$$

for all $z \in \mathbb{C}$. Show that $e$ is everywhere differentiable and $e'(z) = e(z)$.

(iii) Use the mean value theorem of Exercise 11.67 to show that the function $f$ defined by $f(z) = e(a - z)e(z)$ is constant. Deduce also that $e(a - z)e(z) = e(a)$ for all $z \in \mathbb{C}$ and $a \in \mathbb{C}$ and conclude that

$$e(z) e(w) = e(z + w)$$

for all $z, w \in \mathbb{C}$.


### ex_11_78 [ex_11_78]

Let $\alpha \in \mathbb{C}$. Solve the differential equation

$$(1 + z) f'(z) = \alpha f(z)$$

subject to $f(0) = 1$.

Solution. We look for a solution of the form

$$f(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$$

with radius of convergence $R > 0$. We differentiate term by term within the radius of convergence to get

$$(1 + z) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n a_n z^{n-1} = \alpha \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n,$$

whence

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} ((\alpha - n) a_n - (n + 1) a_{n+1}) z^n = 0$$

for all $|z| < R$. By the uniqueness result of Exercise 11.75, this gives

$$(\alpha - n) a_n - (n + 1) a_{n+1} = 0,$$

so

$$a_{n+1} = \frac{\alpha - n}{n + 1} a_n,$$

and, by induction,

$$a_n = A \frac{1}{n!} \prod_{j=0}^{n-1} (\alpha - j),$$

for some constant $A$. Since $f(0) = 1$, we have $A = 1$ and

$$f(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!} \prod_{j=0}^{n-1} (\alpha - j) z^n.$$

If $\alpha$ is a positive integer $N$, say, then $a_j = 0$ for $j \geq N + 1$ and we get the unsurprising result

$$f(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N} \binom{N}{n} z^n = (1 + z)^N.$$

From now on we assume that $\alpha$ is not a positive integer. If $z \neq 0$,

$$\frac{|a_{n+1} z^{n+1}|}{|a_n z^n|} = \frac{|\alpha - n|}{n + 1} |z| = \frac{|1 - \alpha n^{-1}|}{1 + n^{-1}} |z| \to |z|$$

as $n \to \infty$, so, using the ratio test, $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ has radius of convergence 1.

We have shown that, if there is a power series solution, it must be

$$f(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!} \prod_{j=0}^{n-1} (\alpha - j) z^n.$$

Differentiating term by term, we see that, indeed, the $f$ given is a solution valid for $|z| < 1$.

We have left open the possibility that the differential equation of Example 11.78 might have other solutions (such solutions would not have Taylor expansions). The uniqueness of the solution follows from general results developed later in this book (see Section 12.2). However, there is a simple proof of uniqueness in this case.


### ex_11_79 [ex_11_79]

(i) Write $D = \{z : |z| < 1\}$. Let $\alpha \in \mathbb{C}$. Suppose that $f_\alpha : D \to \mathbb{C}$ satisfies

$$(1 + z) f_\alpha'(z) = \alpha f_\alpha(z)$$

and $f_\alpha(0) = 1$, whilst $g_{-\alpha} : D \to \mathbb{C}$ satisfies

$$(1 + z) g_{-\alpha}'(z) = -\alpha g_{-\alpha}(z)$$

and $g_{-\alpha}(0) = 1$. Use the mean value theorem of Exercise 11.67 to show that $f_\alpha(z) g_{-\alpha}(z) = 1$ for all $z \in D$, and deduce that the differential equation

$$(1 + z) f'(z) = \alpha f(z),$$

subject to $f(0) = 1$, has exactly one solution on $D$.

(ii) If $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{C}$, show, using the notation of part (i), that

$$f_{\alpha + \beta}(z) = f_\alpha(z) f_\beta(z)$$

for all $z \in D$. State and prove a similar result for $f_\alpha(f_\beta(z))$.

Restricting to the real axis, we obtain the following version of our results.


### lem_11_80 [lem_11_80]

Let $\alpha$ be a real number. Then the differential equation

$$(1 + x) f'(x) = \alpha f(x),$$

subject to $f(0) = 1$, has exactly one solution $f: (-1, 1) \to \mathbb{R}$, which is given by

$$f(x) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!} \prod_{j=0}^{n-1} (\alpha - j) x^n.$$

In Section 5.7 we developed the theory of the function $r_\alpha(x) = x^\alpha$ for $x > 0$ and $\alpha$ real. One of these properties is that

$$x r_\alpha'(x) = \alpha r_\alpha(x)$$

for all $x > 0$. We also have $r_\alpha(0) = 1$. Thus, if $g_\alpha(x) = r_\alpha(1 + x)$, we have

$$(1 + x) g_\alpha'(x) = \alpha g_\alpha(x)$$

for all $x \in (-1, 1)$ and $g_\alpha(0) = 1$. Lemma 11.80 thus gives the following well known binomial expansion.


### lem_11_81 [lem_11_81]

If $x \in (-1, 1)$, then

$$(1 + x)^\alpha = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!} \prod_{j=0}^{n-1} (\alpha - j) x^n.$$


### ex_11_82 [ex_11_82]

Use the same ideas to show that

$$\log(1 - x) = -\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{n}$$

for $x \in (-1, 1)$.


### ex_11_83 [ex_11_83]

(i) If you are unfamiliar with the general binomial expansion described in Lemma 11.81, write out the first few terms explicitly in the cases $\alpha = -1$, $\alpha = -2$, $\alpha = -3$, $\alpha = 1/2$ and $\alpha = -1/2$. Otherwise, go directly to part (ii).

(ii) Show that

$$1 + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2x}{1 + x^2}\right) + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{4}\left(\frac{2x}{1 + x^2}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{4} \times \frac{5}{6}\left(\frac{2x}{1 + x^2}\right)^3 + \dots$$
