However, you must remember that a closed bounded metric space need not have the Bolzano-Weierstrass property (see Exercise 11.20) and that, for topological spaces, the Bolzano-Weierstrass property is not equivalent to compactness.


## app_i_abuse_of_lang [app_i_abuse_of_lang]

**Abuse of language:** The language of mathematics is a product of history as well as logic, and sometimes the forces of history are stronger than those of logic. Logically, we need to talk about the value $\sin x$ that the function $\sin$ takes at the point $x$, but, traditionally, mathematicians have talked about the function $\sin x$. To avoid this problem mathematicians tend to use phrases like 'the function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ given by $f(x) = x^2$' or 'the map $x \mapsto x^2$'. In Section 13.3, when we wish to talk about the map $\mathbf{x} \mapsto L(\lambda, \mathbf{x}) = t(\mathbf{x}) - \lambda f(\mathbf{x})$ we write $L(\lambda, \cdot) = t - \lambda f$. (Many mathematicians dislike leaving a blank space in a formula and use a place holder '...' instead. They write $L(\lambda, \cdot) = t(\cdot) - \lambda f(\cdot)$.)

To a 19th century mathematician and to most of my readers this may appear unnecessary, but the advantage of the extra care appears when we need to talk about the map $x \mapsto 1$. However, from time to time, mathematicians revert to their traditional habits.

Bourbaki calls such reversions 'abuses of language without which any mathematical text runs the risk of pedantry, not to say unreadability.'

Perhaps the most blatant abuse of language in this book concerns sequences. Just as $f(x)$ is not a function $f$, but the value of $f$ at $x$, so $a_{n}$ is not the sequence

$$a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}, a_{4} \dots$$

but the $n$th term in such a sequence. Wherever I refer to 'the sequence $a_{n}$', I should have used some phrase like 'the sequence $(a_{n})_{n=1}^{\infty}$'. Perhaps future generations will write like this, but it seemed to me that the present generation would simply find it distracting.


## app_i_notation [app_i_notation]

**Non-uniform notation:** When Klein gave his lectures on Elementary Mathematics from an Advanced Standpoint, he complained:

> There are a great many symbols used for each of the vector operations and, so far, it has proved impossible to produce a generally accepted notation. A commission was set up for this purpose at a scientific meeting at Kassel (1903). However, its members were not even able to come to a complete agreement among themselves. None the less, since their intentions were good, each member was willing to meet the others part way and the result was to bring three new notations into existence[^2]! My experience in such things inclines me to the belief that real agreement is only possible if there are powerful economic interests behind such a move. ... But there are no such interests involved in vector calculus and so we must agree, for better or worse, to let every mathematician cling to the notation which he finds most convenient or—if he is dogmatically inclined—the only correct one.

Most mathematics students believe that there should be a unique notation for each mathematical concept. This is not entirely reasonable. Consider the derivative of a function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$. If I am interested in the slope of the curve $y = f(x)$ it is natural to use the suggestive Leibniz notation $\frac{dy}{dx}$. If I am interested in the function which is the derivative of $f$ it is more natural to consider $f'$, and if I am interested in the operation of differentiation rather than the functions it operates on I may well write $Df$. Different branches of mathematics may have genuine reasons for preferring different notations for the same thing.

Even if she disagrees, the student must accept that different notations exist and there is nothing that she can do about it. A quick visit to the library reveals the following notations for the same thing (here $f: \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}$ is a well behaved function):

$$\frac{\partial^{3} f}{\partial x^{2} \partial z}, \quad f_{xz z}, \quad f_{xxz}^{\prime \prime \prime}, \quad D_{xxz} f, \quad f_{113}, \quad f_{113}^{\prime \prime \prime}, \quad D_{113} f, \quad D_{(2, 0, 1)} f, \quad D^{(2, 0, 1)} f$$

and several others.

Here are three obvious pieces of advice.

(1) If you are writing a piece of mathematics and several notations exist, you must make it clear to the reader which one you are using.

(2) If you are reading a piece of mathematics which uses an unfamiliar notation, try to go along with it rather than translating it back into your favourite notation. The advantage of a familiar notation is that it carries you along without too much thought, the advantage of an unfamiliar notation is that it makes you think again—and, who knows, the new notation might turn out to be better than the old.

(3) Do not invent a new notation when a reasonably satisfactory one already exists.


## app_i_deriv [app_i_deriv]

**Left and right derivatives:** In many ways, the natural subsets of $\mathbb{R}^m$ to do analysis on are the open sets $U$, since, given a function $f: U \to \mathbb{R}^n$ and a point $\mathbf{x} \in U$, we can then examine the behaviour of $f$ in all directions round $\mathbf{x}$, on a ball

$$B(\mathbf{x}, \epsilon) = \left\{\mathbf{y}: \|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}\| < \epsilon\right\},$$

for some sufficiently small $\epsilon > 0$.

However, as we saw in Theorem 4.41 and Theorem 4.63, continuous functions behave particularly well on closed bounded sets. This creates a certain tension, as the next exercise illustrates.


### ex_i_1 [ex_i_1]

Exercise I.1.

(i) Show that the map $\mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}$ given by $\mathbf{x} \mapsto \|\mathbf{x}\|$ is continuous.

(ii) If $E$ is a closed bounded set, show that there exists an $\mathbf{e}_0 \in E$ such that $\|\mathbf{e}_0\| \geq \|\mathbf{x}\|$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in E$. Give an example to show that $\mathbf{e}_0$ need not be unique.

(iii) Show that the only subset of $\mathbb{R}^m$ which is both open and closed and bounded is the empty set.

This tension is often resolved by considering functions defined on an open set $U$, but working on closed bounded subsets of $U$. In the special case when we work in one dimension and deal with intervals, we can use a different trick.


### def_i_2 [def_i_2]

**Definition I.2.** Let $b > a$ and consider $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$. We say that $f$ has right derivative $f^{\prime}(a+)$ at $a$ if

$$\frac{f(a + h) - f(a)}{h} \rightarrow f^{\prime}(a +)$$

as $h \to 0$ through values $h > 0$.


### ex_i_3 [ex_i_3]

Exercise I.3. Define the left derivative $f^{\prime}(b - )$, if it exists.

When mathematicians write that $f$ is differentiable on $[a, b]$ with derivative $f'$, they are using the following convention.


### def_i_4 [def_i_4]

**Definition I.4.** If $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable at each point of $(a,b)$ and has right derivative $f'(a+)$ at $a$ and left derivative $f'(b-)$ at $b$, we say that $f$ is differentiable on $[a,b]$ and write $f'(a) = f'(a+)$, $f'(b) = f'(b-)$.

Observe that this is not radically different from our other suggested approach.


### lem_i_5 [lem_i_5]

**Lemma I.5.** If $f: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable on $[a, b]$, we can find an everywhere differentiable function $\tilde{f}: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ with $\tilde{f}(t) = f(t)$ for $t \in [a, b]$.


### lem_i_5_proof [lem_i_5_proof]


**Proof.** Set

$$\begin{array}{l}
\tilde{f}(t) = f(a) + f^{\prime}(a)(t - a) & \text{for } t < a, \\
\tilde{f}(t) = f(t) & \text{for } a \leq t \leq b, \\
\tilde{f}(t) = f(b) + f^{\prime}(b)(t - b) & \text{for } b < t.
\end{array}$$

It is easy to check that $\tilde{f}$ has the required properties.


### ex_i_6 [ex_i_6]

Exercise I.6. If $f: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable on $[a, b]$ with continuous derivative, show that we can find an everywhere differentiable function $\tilde{f}: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ with continuous derivative such that $\tilde{f}(t) = f(t)$ for $t \in [a, b]$.

If $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable on $[a,b]$ and $f':[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable on $[a,b]$, it is natural to say that $f$ is twice differentiable on $[a,b]$ with derivative $f'' = (f')'$, and so on.


### ex_i_7 [ex_i_7]

Exercise I.7. If $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is twice differentiable on $[a,b]$ with continuous second derivative, show that we can find an everywhere twice differentiable function $\tilde{f}:\mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ with continuous second derivative such that $\tilde{f}(t) = f(t)$ for $t \in [a,b]$. Generalise this result.


## app_i_piecewise [app_i_piecewise]

**Piecewise definitions:** Occasionally mathematicians define functions as piecewise continuous, piecewise continuously differentiable and so on. The underlying idea is that the graph of the function is made up of a finite number of well behaved pieces.


### def_i_8 [def_i_8]

**Definition I.8.** A function $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is piecewise continuous if we can find

$$a = x_{0} < x_{1} < \dots < x_{n} = b$$

and continuous functions $g_j : [x_{j-1}, x_j] \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $f(x) = g_j(x)$ for all $x \in (x_{j-1}, x_j)$ and all $1 \leq j \leq n$.


### def_i_9 [def_i_9]

**Definition I.9.** A function $f: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is piecewise linear (respectively differentiable, continuously differentiable, infinitely differentiable, etc.) if it is continuous and we can find

$$a = x_{0} < x_{1} < \dots < x_{n} = b$$

such that $f|_{[x_{j-1}, x_j]} : [x_{j-1}, x_j] \to \mathbb{R}$ is linear (respectively differentiable, continuously differentiable, infinitely differentiable, etc.) for all $1 \leq j \leq n$.

Notice that Definition I.8 does not follow the pattern of Definition I.9.


### ex_i_10 [ex_i_10]

Exercise I.10.

(i) Show by means of an example that a piecewise continuous function need not be continuous.

(ii) Show that a piecewise continuous function is bounded.


### ex_i_11 [ex_i_11]

Exercise I.11. (This is a commentary on Theorem 8.32.)

(i) Show that any piecewise continuous function $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is Riemann integrable.

(ii) Show that, if $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous on $(a,b)$ and bounded on $[a,b]$, then $f$ is Riemann integrable.

The definitions just considered are clearly rather ad hoc. Numerical analysts use a more subtle approach via the notion of a spline (see, for example, Chapters 18 and onwards in the relevant text).


## K Exercises [app_k]



### ex_k_3 [ex_k_3]

K.3. [1.3, P, S] Let $a_1 > b_1 > 0$. We define $a_{n+1} = \frac{a_n + b_n}{2}$ and $b_{n+1} = \sqrt{a_n b_n}$ (the arithmetic and geometric means). Show that 

$$a_{n} > a_{n + 1} > b_{n + 1} > b_{n}.$$

Prove that, as $n \to \infty$, $a_{n}$ and $b_{n}$ both tend to the limit $\sqrt{(a_1b_1)}$.

Use this result to give an example of an increasing sequence of rational numbers tending to a limit $l$ which is not rational.


### ex_k_4 [ex_k_4]

K.4. [1.3, P, S] Let $f, g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be functions such that the composition $g \circ f$ is continuous at $x$.

(i) Show, by means of examples, that the continuity of $f$ at $x$ is neither a necessary nor a sufficient condition for the continuity of $g$ at $f(x)$.

(ii) Show, by means of examples, that the continuity of $g$ at $f(x)$ is neither a necessary nor a sufficient condition for the continuity of $f$ at $x$.


### ex_k_5 [ex_k_5]

K.5. [1.3, P] The function $f:[0,1]\to [0,1]$ is defined by

$$f (x) = \begin{cases} 1 - x & \text{if } x \text{ is rational}, \\ x & \text{if } x \text{ is irrational}. \end{cases}$$

Consider the following statements, prove those which are true and demonstrate the falsity of the remainder.

(i) $f(f(x)) = x$ for all $x\in [0,1]$

(ii) $f(x) + f(1 - x) = 1$ for all $x\in [0,1]$

(iii) $f$ is bijective.

(iv) $f$ is everywhere discontinuous in $[0,1]$.

(v) $f(x + y) - f(x) - f(y)$ is rational for all $x, y \in [0,1]$ such that $x + y \in [0,1]$.


### ex_k_6 [ex_k_6]

K.6. [1.3, P] Enumerate the rationals in $[0, 1]$ as a sequence $q_1, q_2, \ldots$ and let $H$ be the Heaviside function given by $H(t) = 0$ for $t \leq 0$ and $H(t) = 1$ for $t > 0$. Show that the equation

$$f (x) = \lim _ {N \to \infty} \sum_ {n = 1} ^ {N} 2 ^ {- n} H (x - q _ {n})$$

gives a well defined increasing function $f:[0,1]\to \mathbb{R}$ which is discontinuous at rational points and continuous at irrational points.


### ex_k_7 [ex_k_7]

K.7. [1.4, P] A bounded sequence of real numbers $a_{n}$ satisfies the condition

$$a _ {n} \leq \frac {a _ {n - 1} + a _ {n + 1}}{2}$$

for all $n \geq 1$. By showing that the sequence $a_{n+1} - a_n$ is decreasing, or otherwise, show that the sequence $a_n$ converges.

What, if anything, can we deduce if the sequence is unbounded? What, if anything, can we deduce if the sequence $a_{n}$ is bounded but we have the reverse inequality

$$a _ {n} \geq \frac {a _ {n - 1} + a _ {n + 1}}{2}$$

for all $n \geq 1$? Prove your answers.


### ex_k_8 [ex_k_8]

K.8. [1.4, P] The function $f:(0,1)\to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous on the open interval $(0,1)$ and satisfies $0 < f(x) < x$. The sequence of functions $f_{n}:(0,1)\to \mathbb{R}$ is defined by

$$f _ {1} (x) = f (x), \quad f _ {n} (x) = f \left(f _ {n - 1} (x)\right) \text{ for } n \geq 2.$$

Prove that $f_{n}(x) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ for all $x \in (0,1)$.

Show, by means of a counterexample, that the result need not be true if $f$ is not continuous everywhere in $(0,1)$.

Enter any number on your calculator. Press the sine button repeatedly. What appears to happen? Prove your conjecture.


### ex_k_9 [ex_k_9]

K.9. [1.4, T] (i) If $\delta > 0$, use the binomial theorem to show that

$$(1 + \delta) ^ {n} \geq \binom {n} {2} \delta^ {2}.$$

Deduce that $n^{-1}(1 + \delta)^n \to \infty$ as $n \to \infty$. Show that, if $|x| < 1$, then $nx^n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

(ii) Show, more generally, that, if $\delta > 0$ and $k$ is any integer, then $n^{-k}(1 + \delta)^n \to \infty$ as $n \to \infty$. Show that, if $|x| < 1$, then $n^k x^n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.


### ex_k_10 [ex_k_10]

K.10. [1.4, P] Let $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots$ be a sequence of positive real numbers and suppose that

$$\frac {x _ {1} + x _ {2} + \cdots + x _ {n}}{n} \rightarrow 1$$

as $n\to \infty$. Choose $m(n)$ so that $1\leq m(n)\leq n$ and

$$x _ {m (n)} = \max  \left(x _ {1}, x _ {2}, \dots , x _ {n}\right).$$

Show that $x_{n} / n\to 0$ and deduce that $x_{m(n)} / n\to 0$ as $n\to \infty$.

By comparing $x_r^\alpha$ with $x_{r}x_{m(n)}^{\alpha -1}$, or otherwise, show that

$$\frac {x _ {1} ^ {\alpha} + x _ {2} ^ {\alpha} + \cdots + x _ {n} ^ {\alpha}}{n ^ {\alpha}} \rightarrow \begin{cases}0&\text{if } \alpha > 1,\\\infty&\text{if } 0 \leq \alpha <   1,\end{cases}$$

as $n\to \infty$.


### ex_k_11 [ex_k_11]

K.11. [1.5, P] Which of the following statements are true and which are false? Give a proof or counterexample.

(i) The sum of a rational number and an irrational number is irrational.

(ii) The product of a rational number and an irrational number is irrational.

(iii) If $x$ is a real number and $\epsilon > 0$, we can find an irrational number $y$ with $|x - y| < \epsilon$.

(iv) If each $x_{n}$ is irrational and $x_{n}\to x$, then $x$ is irrational.

(v) If $x$ and $y$ are irrational, then $x^{y}$ is irrational.

[Hint: This is an old chestnut. Consider $a = 2^{1/2}$ and observe that $(a^a)^a = 2$.]


### ex_k_12 [ex_k_12]

K.12. (Liouville's theorem.) [1.5, T] A real number $x$ is called algebraic if

$$\sum_ {r = 0} ^ {n} a _ {r} x ^ {r} = 0$$

for some $a_r \in \mathbb{Z}$ $[0 \leq r \leq n]$, $a_n \neq 0$ and $n \geq 1$ (in other words, $x$ is a root of a non-trivial polynomial with integer coefficients). Liouville proved that not all real numbers are algebraic. Here is a version of his proof.

(i) If $x$ and $y$ are real and $n$ is a strictly positive integer, show, by extracting the factor $x - y$ from $x^n - y^n$, that

$$\left| x ^ {n} - y ^ {n} \right| \leq n \left(\max  \left(\left| x \right|, \left| y \right|\right)\right) ^ {n - 1} \left| x - y \right|.$$

(ii) If $P$ is a polynomial and $R > 0$, show, using (i), that there exists a $K$, depending on $P$ and $R$, such that

$$| P (x) - P (y) | \leq K | x - y |$$

whenever $|x|, |y| \leq R$.

(iii) Suppose that

$$P (t) = \sum_ {r = 0} ^ {n} a _ {r} t ^ {r}$$

for some $a_r \in \mathbb{Z}$ $[0 \leq r \leq n]$, $a_n \neq 0$ and $n \geq 1$. Show that $N^n P(M / N)$ is an integer whenever $M$ is an integer and $N$ is a strictly positive integer. Conclude that, if $M$ and $N$ are as stated, either $P(M / N) = 0$ or $|P(M / N)| \geq N^{-n}$.

(iv) Let $P, R, K, M$ and $N$ be as in parts (ii) and (iii). Suppose that $\eta$ is a real number with $P(\eta) = 0$. If $\eta$ and $M / N$ lie in the interval $[-R, R]$, show that

$$N ^ {- n} \leq K | \eta - M / N |.$$

(v) Use (iv) to prove that, if $\eta$ is an algebraic number which is not a rational number, then we can find $n$ and $L$ (depending on $\eta$) such that

$$| \eta - M / N | \geq L N ^ {- n}$$

whenever $M$ is an integer and $N$ is a strictly positive integer.

(vi) Explain why $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} 10^{-k!}$ converges. Let us write $\lambda = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} 10^{-k!}$. Show that

$$\left| \lambda - \sum_ {k = 1} ^ {J} 10 ^ {- k!} \right| \leq \frac {10}{9} \times 10 ^ {- (J + 1)!}.$$

By considering $N = 10^{J!}$ and $M = \sum_{k=1}^{J} 10^{J! - k!}$, or otherwise, show that (a) $\lambda$ is irrational and (b) $\lambda$ is not an algebraic number.

Note that we have shown that $\lambda$ is too well approximable by rationals to be an algebraic number. We discuss approximation problems again in Exercises K.13 and K.14.

(vii) In the first part of the exercise I deliberately avoided using the mean value inequality (Theorem 1.7). Prove (ii) directly, using the mean value inequality.

(viii) Show that no real number of the form

$$\sum_ {k = 1} ^ {\infty} m _ {k} 10 ^ {- k!}$$

with $m_{k}$ taking the value 1 or 2 can be algebraic. If you know enough about countability, show that the collection of numbers of the form just given is uncountable.


### ex_k_13 [ex_k_13]

K.13. (Continued fractions.) [1.5, T] In this exercise we take a first glance at continued fractions.

Consider the following process. If $x = x_0 \in [0,1)$, either $x_0 = 0$ and we stop, or we set

$$r _ {1} = \left[ \frac {1}{x _ {0}} \right]$$

(that is, $r_1$ is the integer part of $1 / x_0$). We set

$$x _ {1} = \frac {1}{x _ {0}} - r _ {1},$$

so that $x_{1}$ is the fractional part of $1 / x_0$ and $x_{1} \in [0,1)$. We now repeat the process so that (unless the process terminates) we have $x_{n} \in [0,1)$,

$$r _ {n + 1} = \left[ \frac {1}{x _ {n}} \right], \quad x _ {n + 1} = \frac {1}{x _ {n}} - r _ {n + 1}.$$

Show that, if the process terminates, $x$ must be rational.

For the rest of the exercise we shall consider the case in which the process does not terminate, unless we explicitly state otherwise. Show, by induction, or otherwise, that

$$\star \quad x = (r _ {1} + (r _ {2} + (r _ {3} + (r _ {4} + \dots (r _ {n} + (r _ {n + 1} + x _ {n + 1}) ^ {- 1}) ^ {- 1} \dots) ^ {- 1}) ^ {- 1}) ^ {- 1}).$$

This formula is usually written out as

$$x = \frac {1}{r _ {1} + \frac {1}{r _ {2} + \frac {1}{r _ {3} + \frac {1}{r _ {4} + \cdots + \frac {1}{r _ {n} + \frac {1}{r _ {n + 1} + x _ {n + 1}}}}}},$$

but this works better in handwriting than in print so we shall use the compact (but less suggestive) notation

$$x = [ r _ {1}, r _ {2}, r _ {3}, r _ {4}, \dots , r _ {n}, r _ {n + 1} + x _ {n + 1} ].$$

We are interested in what happens when we consider the 'truncation'

$$y _ {n + 1} = [ r _ {1}, r _ {2}, r _ {3}, r _ {4}, \dots , r _ {n}, r _ {n + 1} ].$$

Write out the formula for $y_{n}$ in the style of $\star$. Show by induction, using the formula $x_{n + 1} = \frac{1}{x_n} - r_{n + 1}$, or otherwise, that

$$x = \frac {p _ {n + 1} + p _ {n} x _ {n + 1}}{q _ {n + 1} + q _ {n} x _ {n + 1}},$$

where

$$p _ {n + 1} = r _ {n + 1} p _ {n} + p _ {n - 1}, \quad q _ {n + 1} = r _ {n + 1} q _ {n} + q _ {n - 1},$$

and $p_0 = 0$, $q_0 = 1$, $p_1 = 1$, $q_1 = r_1$. If you have met Euclid's algorithm, you should note that the recurrence equations for $p_n$ and $q_n$ are precisely those which occur in that process. By considering the appropriate interpretation of the right hand side of equation $\star\star$ when $x_{n+1} = 0$, show that

$$y _ {n} = \frac {p _ {n}}{q _ {n}}$$

for $n\geq 1$.

By using the recurrence equations for $p_n$ and $q_n$, show that

$$p _ {n - 1} q _ {n} - p _ {n} q _ {n - 1} = (- 1) ^ {n}$$

for all $n \geq 1$. Use this fact together with equation $\star\star$ to show that

$$x - \frac {p _ {n + 1}}{q _ {n + 1}} = \frac {(- 1) ^ {n} x _ {n + 1}}{q _ {n + 1} \left(q _ {n + 1} + q _ {n} x _ {n + 1}\right)}$$

for all $n \geq 0$. Hence show that $x - p_n / q_n$ is alternately positive and negative, and that

$$\left| x - \frac {p _ {n}}{q _ {n}} \right| <   \frac {1}{q _ {n + 1} q _ {n}}$$

for all $n \geq 1$. By looking at the recurrence relations for $q_{n}$, show that $q_{n} \geq n$ and deduce that $p_{n} / q_{n} \to x$ as $n \to \infty$. It is thus natural to say that we have expressed $x$ as an infinite continued fraction

$$x = \frac {1}{r _ {1} + \frac {1}{r _ {2} + \frac {1}{r _ {3} + \frac {1}{r _ {4} + \ldots}}}}.$$

We know that the process of constructing successive $x_{n}$ cannot terminate if $x$ is irrational, but we did not discuss the case $x$ rational. Show that, if $r$, $s$, $p$, $q$ are integers with $s > r \geq 0$, $q > p \geq 0$, then either $r / s = p / q$ or

$$\left| \frac {r}{s} - \frac {p}{q} \right| \geq \frac {1}{q s}.$$

By considering equation $\star\star\star$, conclude that, if $x$ is rational, the process of forming $x_{n}$ will indeed terminate. It is easy to see (but I leave it up to the reader to decide whether to check the details) that we can then express any rational $x$ with $0 < x < 1$ as a continued fraction

$$x = \left[ r _ {1}, r _ {2}, r _ {3}, r _ {4}, \dots , r _ {N} \right]$$

for appropriate $r_j$ and $N$.

In this exercise we have shown how to express any real number $x$ with $0 < x < 1$ as a continued fraction. (So any real number can be written in the form $m$ or $m + y$, where $m$ is an integer and $y$ can be expressed as a continued fraction.) We have not discussed whether a general continued fraction

$$\frac {1}{s _ {1} + \frac {1}{s _ {2} + \frac {1}{s _ {3} + \frac {1}{s _ {4} + \ldots}}}},$$

with $s_n$ a strictly positive integer, converges. (A fairly simple adaptation of the argument above shows that it does. Of course, we need to use the fundamental axiom of analysis at some point.)


### ex_k_14 [ex_k_14]

K.14. [1.5, T, ↑] In this exercise we continue the discussion of Exercise K.13. We shall suppose, as before, that $0 < x < 1$ and $x$ is irrational. One major advantage of continued fractions is that they give very good rational approximations, as was shown in $\star\star\star\star$, which gave us the estimate

$$\left| x - \frac {p _ {n}}{q _ {n}} \right| <   \frac {1}{q _ {n + 1} q _ {n}} \leq \frac {1}{q _ {n} ^ {2}}. \tag {†}$$

Conclude that, if $x$ is a real number, we can find integers $p$ and $q$ with $q \geq 1$ such that

$$\left| x - \frac {p}{q} \right| <   \frac {1}{q ^ {2}}.$$

Since the larger $q_{n}$ is (for fixed $n$), the better the approximation $(†)$, it is natural to ask how small $q_{n}$ can be.

Use induction and the recurrence relation for $q_{n}$ obtained in Exercise K.13 to show that $q_{n} \geq F_{n}$, where

$$F _ {n + 1} = F _ {n} + F _ {n - 1}$$

and $F_0 = 1$, $F_1 = 1$. By directly solving the recurrence relation, if you know how, or by inductive verification, if you do not, show that

$$F _ {n} = \frac {\tau_ {1} ^ {n + 1} - \tau_ {2} ^ {n + 1}}{\sqrt {5}},$$

where $\tau_{1} = (1 + \sqrt{5}) / 2$ and $\tau_{2} = (1 - \sqrt{5}) / 2$. Explain why, if $n$ is sufficiently large, $F_{n}$ is the integer closest to $\tau_{1}^{n + 1} / \sqrt{5}$. (Actually, the result is true for all $n \geq 0$, and you might wish to prove this stronger statement.)

It is interesting to look for an example of a 'worst behaved' continued fraction. If $q_{n} = F_{n}$, then the associated recurrence relation tells us that $r_{n} = 1$ and suggests we look at

$$x = \frac {1}{1 + \frac {1}{1 + \frac {1}{1 + \frac {1}{1 + \ldots}}}}.$$

Since we have not proved the convergence of this continued fraction, our argument is now merely heuristic. Explain why

$$x = \frac {1}{1 + x}$$

and deduce that, if $1 > x > 0$, we must have $x = (\sqrt{5} - 1) / 2$. Returning to full rigour, show that, if $x = (\sqrt{5} - 1) / 2$, then the process of Exercise K.13 does, indeed, give

$$x = \frac {1}{1 + \frac {1}{1 + \frac {1}{1 + \frac {1}{1 + \dots}}}},$$

as required.

Hardy and Wright's delightful \emph{The Theory of Numbers} [26] contains a discussion of the problem of approximating real numbers by rationals, and shows that $(\sqrt{5} - 1)/2$ and its relatives are indeed the 'worst approximable' numbers. (Chapter X of [26] deals with continued fractions and Chapter XI with problems of approximation by rationals.) We discussed a related problem in Exercise K.12.


### ex_k_15 [ex_k_15]

K.15. (Lévy's universal chord theorem.) [1.6, P] Suppose $f: [0, 1] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $f(0) = f(1)$. By considering the function $g: [0, 1/2] \to \mathbb{R}$ defined by $g(x) = f(x + \frac{1}{2}) - f(x)$, show that there exist $a, b \in [0, 1]$ such that $b - a = \frac{1}{2}$ and $f(b) = f(a)$. Show, more generally, that there exist $\alpha_n, \beta_n \in [0, 1]$ such that $\beta_n - \alpha_n = \frac{1}{n}$ and $f(\beta_n) = f(\alpha_n)$. (This is Lévy's universal chord theorem. For more discussion, including a proof that you cannot replace $\frac{1}{n}$ by a more general $h$, see [8], page 109.)

Show that we can find $a_n, b_n \in [0,1]$ such that $a_n \leq a_{n+1} < b_{n+1} \leq b_n$, $b_n - a_n = 2^{-n}$ and $f(b_n) = f(a_n)$.

Now suppose that $\delta > 0$, that $f$ is defined on the larger interval $(-\delta, 1 + \delta)$ and that $f: (-\delta, 1 + \delta) \to \mathbb{R}$ is everywhere differentiable. Show that, if $f(0) = f(1)$, then there exists a $c \in [0, 1]$ such that $f'(c) = 0$. (A little extra thought gives the full Rolle's theorem (Theorem 4.52).)


### ex_k_16 [ex_k_16]

K.16. [1.6, P] Suppose that $f:[a,b]\to \mathbb{R}$ is increasing and $g:[a,b]\to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous. Show that, if $f(a)\geq g(a)$ and $f(b)\leq g(b)$, then there exists a $c\in [a,b]$ such that $f(c) = g(c)$.

Is the result of the first paragraph true if we replace $f(a) \geq g(a)$ and $f(b) \leq g(b)$ by $f(a) \leq g(a)$ and $f(b) \geq g(b)$? Is the result of the first paragraph true if we replace $g$ continuous' by $g$ decreasing'? Is the result of the first paragraph true if we replace $f$ increasing' by $f$ continuous'? Give proofs or counterexamples as appropriate.


### ex_k_17 [ex_k_17]

K.17. [3.1, P] Let $c \in (a,b)$. Suppose $f_{n}:(a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous at $c$ for each $n \geq 1$.

Show that, if $g:(a,b)\to \mathbb{R}$ is given by

$$g (x) = \max  \left(f _ {1} (x), f _ {2} (x), \dots , f _ {N} (x)\right),$$

then $g$ is continuous at $c$, but show, by means of an example, that, even if there exists a $K$ such that $|f_n(x)| \leq K$ for all $n$ and all $x \in (a,b)$, if we define $G:(a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ by

$$G (x) = \sup  \left(f _ {1} (x), f _ {2} (x), \dots\right),$$

then $G$ need not be continuous at $c$.


### ex_k_18 [ex_k_18]

K.18. [3.1, P] (This requires good command of the notion of countability)

(i) Suppose that $E$ is a bounded uncountable set of real numbers. Show that there exist real numbers $\alpha$ and $\beta$ with $\alpha < \beta$ such that both of the sets

$$\left\{e \in E: e <   \gamma \right\} \text{ and } \left\{e \in E: e > \gamma \right\}$$

are uncountable if and only if $\alpha <  \gamma <  \beta$.

(ii) State and prove the appropriate version of (ii) if we omit the condition that $E$ be bounded.

(iii) Give an example of a bounded infinite set $E$ of real numbers such that there does not exist a real $\gamma$ with the property that both

$$\{e \in E: e <   \gamma \} \text{ and } \{e \in E: e > \gamma \}$$

are infinite.


### ex_k_19 [ex_k_19]

K.19. [3.2, P, S] Consider a sequence of real numbers $x_{n}$. Which of the following statements are always true and which are sometimes true and sometimes false? Give proofs or examples.

(i) There is a strictly increasing subsequence. (That is, we can find $n(j)$ with $n(j + 1) > n(j)$ and $x_{n(j + 1)} > x_{n(j)}$ for all $j \geq 1$.)

(ii) There is an increasing subsequence. (That is, we can find $n(j)$ with $n(j + 1) > n(j)$ and $x_{n(j + 1)} \geq x_{n(j)}$ for all $j \geq 1$.)

(iii) If there is no increasing subsequence, there must be a strictly decreasing subsequence.

(iv) If there is no strictly increasing subsequence and no strictly decreasing subsequence, then we can find an $N$ such that $x_{n} = x_{N}$ for all $n \geq N$.


### ex_k_20 [ex_k_20]

K.20. [3.2, P] (i) Suppose that $a_{n}$ is a bounded sequence of real numbers. Show that, if $n(p) \to \infty$ as $p \to \infty$ and $a_{n(p)} \to a$, then

$$\limsup_{n\to \infty}a_{n}\geq a\geq \liminf_{n\to \infty}a_{n}.$$

(ii) Suppose that $\lim \sup_{n\to \infty}a_n = 1$ and $\lim \inf_{n\to \infty}a_n = 0$. Show, by means of examples, that it may happen that 1 and 0 are the only numbers which are the limits of convergent subsequences of the $a_{n}$, or it may happen that every $a$ with $0\leq a\leq 1$ is such a limit. [You may wish to investigate precisely what sets can occur, but you will need the notion of a closed set.]

(iii) Suppose that $a_{n}$ and $b_{n}$ are bounded sequences of real numbers. Show, by means of an example, that the equation

$$\lim  _ {n \rightarrow \infty} \sup  (a _ {n} + b _ {n}) \stackrel {?} {=} \lim  _ {n \rightarrow \infty} \sup  a _ {n} + \lim  _ {n \rightarrow \infty} \sup  b _ {n}$$

need not hold. By giving proofs or counterexamples, establish whether the following relations always hold:

$$\lim  _ {n \to \infty} \sup  (a _ {n} + b _ {n}) \stackrel {?} {\leq} \lim  _ {n \to \infty} \sup  a _ {n} + \lim  _ {n \to \infty} \sup  b _ {n},$$

$$\limsup_{n\to \infty}\left(a_{n} + b_{n}\right)\overset {?}{≥}\limsup_{n\to \infty}a_{n} + \liminf_{n\to \infty}b_{n}.$$


### ex_k_21 [ex_k_21]

K.21. (Ptolomey's inequality.) [4.1, S] We work in a vector space with inner product. By squaring $\left\| \frac{\mathbf{x}}{\|\mathbf{x}\|^2} - \frac{\mathbf{y}}{\|\mathbf{y}\|^2} \right\|$ and rewriting the result, or otherwise, prove Ptolomey's inequality

$$\left\| \mathbf {x} - \mathbf {y} \right\| \left\| \mathbf {z} \right\| \leq \left\| \mathbf {y} - \mathbf {z} \right\| \left\| \mathbf {x} \right\| + \left\| \mathbf {z} - \mathbf {x} \right\| \left\| \mathbf {y} \right\|.$$


### ex_k_22 [ex_k_22]

K.22. [4.2, P] We use the standard Euclidean norm on $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$. Which of the following statements are true and which are false? Give proofs or counterexamples as appropriate.

(i) If $E$ is a closed subset of $\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}$, then

$$\left\{\mathbf {x} \in \mathbb {R} ^ {n}: (0, \mathbf {x}) \in E \right\}$$

is closed in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

(ii) If $U$ is an open subset of $\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}$, then

$$\left\{\mathbf {x} \in \mathbb {R} ^ {n}: (0, \mathbf {x}) \in U \right\}$$

is open in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

(iii) If $E$ is a closed subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$, then

$$\{(0, \mathbf {x}): \mathbf {x} \in E \}$$

is closed in $\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}$.

(iv) If $U$ is an open subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$, then

$$\left\{\left(0, \mathbf {x}\right): \mathbf {x} \in U \right\}$$

is open in $\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}$.


### ex_k_23 [ex_k_23]

K.23. [4.2, T] We can extend Definition 4.34 as follows.

Let $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}^m$, $A \subseteq E$, $\mathbf{x} \in E$ and $\mathbf{l} \in \mathbb{R}^p$. Consider a function $\mathbf{f}: E \setminus \{\mathbf{x}\} \to \mathbb{R}^p$. We say that $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{y}) \to \mathbf{l}$ as $\mathbf{y} \to \mathbf{x}$ through values of $\mathbf{y} \in A$ if, given $\epsilon > 0$, we can find a $\delta(\epsilon, \mathbf{x}) > 0$ such that, whenever $\mathbf{y} \in A$ and $0 < \| \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \| < \delta(\epsilon, \mathbf{x})$, we have

$$\| \mathbf {f} (\mathbf {y}) - \mathbf {l} \| <   \epsilon .$$

(i) Let $A \subseteq E \subseteq \mathbb{R}^m$, $\mathbf{x} \in E$ and $\mathbf{l} \in \mathbb{R}^p$. Consider a function $\mathbf{f}: E \setminus \{\mathbf{x}\} \to \mathbb{R}^p$. Show that, if $A$ is finite (this includes the case $A = \varnothing$), then, automatically, $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{y}) \to \mathbf{l}$ as $\mathbf{y} \to \mathbf{x}$ through values of $\mathbf{y} \in A$.

(ii) Let $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}^m$, $A \subseteq E$, $\mathbf{x} \in E$ and $\mathbf{l} \in \mathbb{R}^p$. Consider a function $\mathbf{f}: E \setminus \{\mathbf{x}\} \to \mathbb{R}^p$ such that $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{y}) \to \mathbf{l}$ as $\mathbf{y} \to \mathbf{x}$ through values of $\mathbf{y} \in A$. Show that, if $B \subseteq A$, then $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{y}) \to \mathbf{l}$ as $\mathbf{y} \to \mathbf{x}$ through values of $\mathbf{y} \in A$.

(iii) Let $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}^m$, $A \subseteq E$, $B \subseteq E$, $\mathbf{x} \in E$ and $\mathbf{l} \in \mathbb{R}^p$. Consider a function $\mathbf{f}: E \setminus \{\mathbf{x}\} \to \mathbb{R}^p$ such that $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{y}) \to \mathbf{l}$ as $\mathbf{y} \to \mathbf{x}$ through values of $\mathbf{y} \in A$ and $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{y}) \to \mathbf{l}$ as $\mathbf{y} \to \mathbf{x}$ through values of $\mathbf{y} \in B$. Show that $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{y}) \to \mathbf{l}$ as $\mathbf{y} \to \mathbf{x}$ through values of $\mathbf{y} \in A \cup B$.


### ex_k_24 [ex_k_24]

K.24. [4.2, P] We work in $\mathbb{R}^2$. The projections $\pi_1, \pi_2: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ are defined by $\pi_1(x,y) = x$ and $\pi_2(x,y) = y$.

(i) Show that $E = \{(x, 1/x) : x > 0\}$ is a closed set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ but that $\pi_1(E)$ is not closed.

(ii) Find a closed set $E$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that $\pi_2(E)$ is closed but $\pi_1(E)$ is not closed.

(iii) Show, however, that, if $E$ is a closed set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $\pi_2(E)$ bounded, then $\pi_1(E)$ must be closed.


### ex_k_25 [ex_k_25]

K.25. [4.2, P] (This echoes Exercise K.24 but does not require it.) Let $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ and let $\mathbf{f}: E \to \mathbb{R}^m$ be a function. We call $G = \{(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})): \mathbf{x} \in E\}$ the graph of $\mathbf{f}$.

(i) Show that, if $G$ is bounded, then $E$ is.

(ii) Show that we can have $G$ closed and $\mathbf{f}$ continuous but $E$ not closed.

(iii) Show that, if $E$ is closed and $\mathbf{f}$ is continuous, then $G$ is closed.

(iv) Show that $G$ and $E$ may be closed but $\mathbf{f}$ discontinuous.

(v) Show that, if $G$ is closed and bounded, then $E$ is closed and $\mathbf{f}$ is continuous.


### ex_k_26 [ex_k_26]

K.26. [4.3, P] Let $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$. A function $f: E \to \mathbb{R}$ is said to be upper semicontinuous on $E$ if, given any $\mathbf{x} \in E$ and any $\epsilon > 0$, we can find a $\delta(\epsilon, \mathbf{x}) > 0$ such that $f(\mathbf{y}) < f(\mathbf{x}) + \epsilon$ for all $\mathbf{y} \in E$ with $\| \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x} \| < \delta(\epsilon, \mathbf{x})$. Give an example of a function $f: [-1, 1] \to \mathbb{R}$ which is upper semicontinuous on $[-1, 1]$ but not continuous. If $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ and $f: E \to \mathbb{R}$ is such that both $f$ and $-f$ are upper semicontinuous on $E$, show that $f$ is continuous on $E$.

If $K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is closed and bounded and $f: K \to \mathbb{R}$ is upper semicontinuous, show that $f$ is bounded above and attains its least upper bound. Is it necessarily true that $f$ is bounded below? Is it necessarily true that, if $f$ is bounded below, it attains its greatest lower bound? Give proofs or counterexamples as appropriate.


### ex_k_27 [ex_k_27]

K.27. [4.3, P] (i) If $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and periodic (that is to say, there exists an $a > 0$ with $f(x + a) = f(x)$ for all $x$), show that $f$ is bounded. Does it attain its bounds?

(ii) Give an example of an unbounded periodic function.

(iii) Suppose that $g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is bounded on every interval $[0, a]$ and $g(x + 1) - g(x) \to k$ as $x \to \infty$. Show that $g(x) / x \to k$ as $x \to \infty$.

(iv) Give an example of a function $G: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $G(x + 1) - G(x) \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$ but $G(x) / x \nrightarrow 0$ as $x \to \infty$.

(v) Suppose that $h(x) / x \to k$ as $x \to \infty$. Is it necessarily true that $h(x + 1) - h(x) \to k$ as $x \to \infty$? Give a proof or a counterexample.


### ex_k_28 [ex_k_28]

K.28. [4.3, H, S] Suppose that $f:(a,b)\to \mathbb{R}$ is a function with continuous derivative $f'$. The object of this exercise is to show, using the mean value inequality (Theorem 1.42), that, if $f'(t) > 0$ for all $t\in (a,b)$, then $f$ is strictly increasing on $(a,b)$. (We prove a stronger result in Lemma 4.50 using Theorem 4.49.)

To this end, suppose $a < x < y < b$.

(i) Using the fact that a continuous function on a closed bounded interval is bounded and attains its bounds, show that there exists an $m > 0$ such that $f'(t)\geq m$ for all $t\in [x,y]$.

(ii) Hence, using the mean value inequality or one of its corollaries, show that $f(y) - f(x) \geq m(y - x) > 0$.


### ex_k_29 [ex_k_29]

K.29. [4.3, T!] Suppose $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots$ are real numbers. Show that we can find closed intervals $K_{n} = [a_{n}, b_{n}]$ (where $b_{n} > a_{n}$) such that $x_{n} \notin K_{n}$ for all $n \geq 1$ but $K_{n} \subseteq K_{n-1}$ for $n \geq 2$. By applying the result of Exercise 4.48, show that there exists a real $y$ with $y \neq x_{n}$ for all $n$ (i.e., the reals are uncountable).

[The method of this exercise is quite close to that of Cantor's original proof.]


### ex_k_30 [ex_k_30]

K.30. [4.3, T] Suppose $\alpha : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^n$ is a linear map which is self-adjoint, that is to say, such that

$$\alpha ({\mathbf {x}}) \cdot \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {x} \cdot (\alpha \mathbf {y})$$

for all $\mathbf{x}$, $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^n$. (We use the standard inner product notation.)

(i) Show that the map $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ given by $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x} \cdot \alpha \mathbf{x}$ is continuous. Hence, stating carefully any theorem that you use, show that there is an $\mathbf{e} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ with $\| \mathbf{e} \| = 1$ such that

$$\mathbf {e} \cdot (\alpha \mathbf {e}) \geq \mathbf {x} \cdot (\alpha \mathbf {x}),$$

whenever $\| \mathbf{x}\| = 1$.

(ii) If $\mathbf{e}$ is as in (i), deduce that, if $\mathbf{h} \cdot \mathbf{e} = 0$ and $\|\mathbf{h}\| = 1$, then

$$(1 + \delta^ {2}) \mathbf {e} \cdot (\alpha \mathbf {e}) \geq (\mathbf {e} + \delta \mathbf {h}) \cdot (\alpha (\mathbf {e} + \delta \mathbf {h}))$$

for all real $\delta$. By considering what happens when $\delta$ is small, or otherwise, show that

$$\mathbf {e} \cdot (\alpha \mathbf {h}) + \mathbf {h} \cdot (\alpha \mathbf {e}) = 0,$$

and so $\mathbf{h}\cdot (\alpha \mathbf{e}) = 0$.

(iii) Part (ii) shows that $\mathbf{h} \cdot (\alpha \mathbf{e}) = 0$ whenever $\mathbf{h} \cdot \mathbf{e} = 0$. Explain why this tells us that $\alpha \mathbf{e} = \lambda \mathbf{e}$ for some real $\lambda$, and so the vector $\mathbf{e}$ found in (i) is an eigenvector.

(iv) Let $U = \{\mathbf{h} : \mathbf{e} \cdot \mathbf{h} = 0\}$. Explain why $U$ is an $n - 1$-dimensional subspace of $V$ and $\alpha(U) \subseteq U$. If $\beta = \alpha|_U$, the restriction of $\alpha$ to $U$, show that $\beta$ is selfadjoint. Hence use induction to show that $\mathbb{R}^n$ has an orthonormal basis $\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{e}_n$ of eigenvectors of $\alpha$.


### ex_k_31 [ex_k_31]

K.31. [4.3, T!, ↑] (i) Suppose that $E$ is a subspace of $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $\mathbf{y} \notin E$. Show that the map $g: E \to \mathbb{R}$ given by

$$g ({\mathbf {x}}) = \left\| \mathbf {x} - \mathbf {y} \right\|$$

is continuous. Hence, show carefully (note that, except in the trivial case $E = \{\mathbf{0}\}$, $E$ itself is not bounded) that there exists a $\mathbf{x}_0 \in E$ such that

$$\left\| \mathbf {x} - \mathbf {y} \right\| \geq \left\| \mathbf {x} _ {0} - \mathbf {y} \right\|$$

for all $\mathbf{x} \in E$.

(ii) Show that $\mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{y}$ is perpendicular to $E$ (that is, $(\mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{y})\cdot \mathbf{x} = 0$ for all $\mathbf{x}\in E$). (See Exercise K.30 (ii), if you need a hint.) Show that $\| \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y}\| >\| \mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{y}\|$ for all $\mathbf{x}\in E$ with $\mathbf{x}\neq \mathbf{x}_0$ (in other words, $\mathbf{x}_0$ is unique).

(iii) Suppose that $\alpha : \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}$ is linear and $\alpha \neq 0$. By setting

$$E = \{\mathbf {x}: \alpha \mathbf {x} = 0 \}$$

and choosing an appropriate $\mathbf{y}$, show that there exists a unit vector $\mathbf{b}$ perpendicular to $E$. By considering the effect of $\alpha$ on the vector $\mathbf{x} - (\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{x})\mathbf{b}$, show that there exists a vector $\mathbf{a}$ such that

$$\alpha \mathbf {x} = \mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {x}$$

for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^m$.


### ex_k_32 [ex_k_32]

K.32. (Hahn-Banach for $\mathbb{R}^n$.) [4.3, T!, ↑] This exercise extends some of the ideas of Exercise K.31. Recall that a set $E$ in $\mathbb{R}^m$ is called convex if, whenever $\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2 \in E$ and $\lambda \in [0,1]$, we have $\lambda \mathbf{e}_1 + (1 - \lambda) \mathbf{e}_2 \in E$ (in other words, if two points lie in $E$, so does the line segment joining them).

(i) Suppose that $E$ is a non-empty closed convex set in $\mathbb{R}^m$ and $\mathbf{y} \notin E$. Show that there exists an $\mathbf{x}_0 \in E$ such that

$$\left\| \mathbf {x} - \mathbf {y} \right\| \geq \left\| \mathbf {x} _ {0} - \mathbf {y} \right\|$$

for all $\mathbf{x} \in E$.

(ii) Suppose that $\mathbf{x}_0 = \mathbf{0}$. Show that $\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} \leq 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in E$.

(iii) Returning to the general case, when all we know is that $E$ is a nonempty closed convex set in $\mathbb{R}^m$ and $\mathbf{y} \notin E$, show that there exist an $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ and a real number $c$ such that $\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{a} \leq c$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in E$ but $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{y} > c$. (Results like this are called Hahn-Banach type theorems.)


### ex_k_33 [ex_k_33]

K.33. (Extreme points.) [4.3, T!, ↑] Here is an application of Exercise K.32.

(i) Throughout this exercise, $K$ is a non-empty closed bounded convex set in $\mathbb{R}^n$. We say that a point $\mathbf{z} \in K$ is an extreme point for $K$, if, whenever $\mathbf{x}$, $\mathbf{y} \in K$, $1 > \lambda > 0$ and $\lambda \mathbf{x} + (1 - \lambda) \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{z}$, then $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{z}$.

Show that the extreme points of the closed unit ball $\{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n : \| \mathbf{x} \| \leq 1\}$ are precisely those points $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ with $\| \mathbf{z} \| = 1$. Show that the extreme points of the closed cube $[-1, 1]^n$ are precisely the $2^n$ points $(z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_n)$ with $|z_j| = 1$.

(ii) Suppose $\mathbf{w} \notin K$. By using Exercise K.32, show that there exist an $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and a real number $c$ such that $\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{a} \leq c$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in K$ but $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{w} > c$. Quoting carefully any theorems that you use, show that there exists an $\mathbf{x}_0 \in K$ such that $\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{a} \leq \mathbf{x}_0 \cdot \mathbf{a}$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in K$.

Show that

$$K ^ {\prime} = \{\mathbf {u}: \mathbf {x} _ {0} + \mathbf {u} \in K \text{ and } \mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {a} = 0 \}$$

is a non-empty closed convex subset of an $(n - 1)$-dimensional subspace of $\mathbb{R}^n$. Show further that, if $\mathbf{u}_0$ is an extreme point of $K'$, then $\mathbf{x}_0 + \mathbf{u}_0$ is an extreme point of $K$.

(iii) By using induction on the dimension $n$, or otherwise, show that every non-empty closed bounded convex set in $\mathbb{R}^n$ contains an extreme point.

(iv) Suppose that $T: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$. Explain why there exists an $\mathbf{x}_0 \in K$ such that $T(\mathbf{x}_0) \geq T(\mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in K$. By considering extreme points of the set

$$\{\mathbf {x} \in K: T (\mathbf {x}) = T (\mathbf {x} _ {0}) \},$$

or otherwise, show that there exists an extreme point $\mathbf{e}$ of $K$ with $T(\mathbf{e}) = T(\mathbf{x}_0)$. Thus every linear map $T: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ attains its maximum on a closed bounded convex set at some extreme point of that set. This observation is the foundation of the theory of linear optimisation.

(v) Suppose that $L$ is a non-empty closed convex subset of $K$. If $L \neq K$ (so that there exists a $\mathbf{w} \in K$ with $\mathbf{w} \notin L$), use the ideas of (ii) to show that there exists an extreme point of $K$ which does not lie in $L$.


### ex_k_34 [ex_k_34]

K.34. (Krein-Milman for $\mathbb{R}^n$.) [4.3, T!, ↑] We continue with the ideas of Exercise K.33.

(i) If $\mathcal{L}$ is a collection of convex sets in $\mathbb{R}^n$, show that $\bigcap_{L\in \mathcal{L}}L$ is convex. If $\mathcal{M}$ is a collection of closed convex sets in $\mathbb{R}^n$, show that $\bigcap_{M\in \mathcal{M}}M$ is a closed convex set.

(ii) Explain why $\mathbb{R}^n$ is a closed convex set. If $A$ is a subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$, we write

$$\operatorname {hull} (A) = \bigcap \{L \supseteq A: L \text{ is closed and convex} \}$$

and call $\mathrm{hull}(A)$ the closed convex hull of $A$. Show that $\mathrm{hull}(A)$ is indeed closed and convex, and that $\mathrm{hull}(A) \supseteq A$. Show also that, if $B$ is a closed convex set with $B \supseteq A$, then $B \supseteq \mathrm{hull}(A)$.

[This argument parallels the standard approach to the closure and interior set out in Exercise K.198.]

(iii) Use (ii) and part (v) of Exercise K.33 to show that the closed convex hull of the set $E$ of extreme points of a non-empty bounded closed convex subset $K$ of $\mathbb{R}^n$ is the set itself. (More briefly, $\mathrm{hull}(E) = K$.) Results of this kind are known as Krein-Milman theorems.


### ex_k_35 [ex_k_35]

K.35. [4.3, T] This easy exercise introduces a concept that will be used in other exercises. We say that a non-empty collection $\mathcal{A}$ of subsets of some set $X$ has the finite intersection property if, whenever $n \geq 1$ and $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n \in \mathcal{A}$, we have $\bigcap_{j=1}^{n} A_j \neq \emptyset$.

(i) Fix $N$ and let $\mathcal{A}(1)$ be the collection of subsets $A$ of $\mathbb{Z}$ such that

$A\cap \{1,2,3,\dots ,N,N + 1\}$ has at least $N$ members.

If $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N \in \mathcal{A}(1)$, show that $\bigcap_{j=1}^{N} A_j \neq \emptyset$. Show, however, that $\mathcal{A}(1)$ does not have the finite intersection property.

(ii) Let $\mathcal{A}(2)$ be the collection of subsets $A$ of $\mathbb{Z}$ such that $\mathbb{N} \setminus A$ is finite. Show that $\mathcal{A}(2)$ has the finite intersection property but $\bigcap_{A \in \mathcal{A}(2)} A = \emptyset$.

(iii) (Requires countability.) Let $\mathcal{A}(3)$ be the collection of subsets $A$ of $\mathbb{R}$ such that $\mathbb{R} \setminus A$ is finite. Show that if $A_1, A_2, \dots \in \mathcal{A}(3)$ then $\bigcap_{j=1}^{\infty} A_j \neq \emptyset$ (so that, in particular, $\mathcal{A}(3)$ has the finite intersection property) but $\bigcap_{A \in \mathcal{A}(3)} A = \emptyset$.

(iv) Let $\mathcal{A}(4)$ be the collection of subsets $A$ of $\mathbb{Z}$ of the form

$$A = \{k r: r \geq 1 \}$$

for some $k \geq 1$. Show that $\mathcal{A}(4)$ has the finite intersection property but $\bigcap_{A \in \mathcal{A}(4)} A = \emptyset$.


### ex_k_36 [ex_k_36]

K.36. [4.3, T, ↑] We work in $\mathbb{R}$. Let $\mathcal{K}$ be a collection of closed sets lying in some closed interval $[a,b]$ and suppose that $\mathcal{K}$ has the finite intersection property (see Exercise K.35).

(i) If $a \leq c \leq b$ and we write

$$\mathcal {K} _ {1} = \left\{K \cap [ a, c ]: K \in \mathcal {K} \right\} \text{ and } \mathcal {K} _ {2} = \left\{K \cap [ c, b ]: K \in \mathcal {K} \right\},$$

show that at least one of $\mathcal{K}_{1}$ and $\mathcal{K}_{2}$ must have the finite intersection property.

(ii) Use lion hunting to show that $\bigcap_{K\in \mathcal{K}}K\neq \emptyset$.


### ex_k_37 [ex_k_37]

K.37. (The Heine-Borel theorem) [4.3, T, ↑] We work in $\mathbb{R}^m$. Consider the following two statements.

(A) Let $R > 0$. If a collection $\mathcal{K}$ of closed sets lying in $[-R, R]^m$ has the finite intersection property, then $\bigcap_{K \in \mathcal{K}} K \neq \emptyset$.

(B) Let $E$ be a bounded closed set. If a collection $\mathcal{U}$ of open sets is such that $\bigcup_{U \in \mathcal{U}} U \supseteq E$, then we can find an $n \geq 1$ and $U_1, U_2, \ldots, U_n \in \mathcal{U}$ such that $\bigcup_{j=1}^{n} U_n \supseteq E$.

(i) By considering sets of the form $K = E \setminus U$, deduce statement (B) from statement (A). Deduce statement (A) from statement (B).

(ii) Prove statement (A) by a lion hunting argument along the lines of Exercises K.36 and 4.14. It follows from (i) that (B) holds. This result is known as the theorem of Heine-Borel.

(iii) Explain why Exercise 4.48 is a special case of statement (A).

(iv) Suppose that $E$ is a set with the properties described in the second sentence of statement (B). Show that $E$ is closed and bounded.

[Hint: To show $E$ is closed, suppose $\mathbf{x} \notin E$ and consider sets $U$ of the form

$$U = \{\mathbf {y}: r > \| \mathbf {x} - \mathbf {y} \| > 1 / r \}$$

with $r > 1$.]


### ex_k_38 [ex_k_38]

K.38. [4.3, H, ↑] (This complements Exercise K.36.) Suppose that $\mathbb{F}$ is an ordered field with the following property.

(A) Whenever $\mathcal{K}$ is a collection of closed sets lying in some closed interval $[a,b]$ and $\mathcal{K}$ has the finite intersection property, it follows that $\bigcap_{K\in \mathcal{K}}K\neq \emptyset$.

Suppose that $E$ is a non-empty set in $\mathbb{F}$ which is bounded above. By considering

$$\mathcal {K} = \{[ a, b ]: a \in E \text{ and } b \geq e \text{ for all } e \in E \},$$

$$\mathcal{K} = \{[a, b] : a \in E \text{ and } b \geq e \text{ for all } e \in E\},$$

or otherwise, deduce that $E$ has a supremum. Conclude that statement (A) is equivalent to the fundamental axiom of analysis.


### ex_k_39 [ex_k_39]

K.39. [4.4, T!] In this exercise you may assume the standard properties of sin and cos, but not their power series expansion.

(i) By considering the sign of $f_1'(x)$, when $f_1(t) = t - \sin t$, show that

$$t \geq \sin t$$

for all $t \geq 0$

(ii) By considering the sign of $f_2'(x)$, when $f_2(t) = \cos t - 1 + t^2 / 2!$, show that

$$\cos t \geq 1 - \frac{t^2}{2!}$$

for all $t \geq 0$

(iii) By considering the sign of $f_3'(x)$, when $f_3(t) = \sin t - t + t^3 / 3!$, show that

$$\sin t \geq t - \frac{t^3}{3!}$$

for all $t \geq 0$

(iv) State general results suggested by parts (i) to (iii), and prove them by induction. State and prove corresponding results for $t < 0$.

(v) Using (iv), show that

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N} \frac{(-1)^n t^{2n+1}}{(2n+1)!} \to \sin t$$

as $N \to \infty$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$. State and prove a corresponding result for $\cos$.

[This exercise could be usefully illustrated by computer graphics.]


### ex_k_40 [ex_k_40]

K.40. (Jensen's inequality.) [4.4, T] Good inequalities are the diamonds of analysis, valuable but rare. Jensen earned his living in the telephone industry and pursued mathematics in his spare time. He has two marvelous inequalities named after him. This exercise discusses one of them.

We call a function $f:(a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ convex if, whenever $x_1, x_2 \in (a,b)$ and $1 \geq \lambda \geq 0$, we have

$$\lambda f(x_1) + (1 - \lambda) f(x_2) \geq f(\lambda x_1 + (1 - \lambda) x_2).$$

(The centre of mass of a particle of mass $\lambda$ at $(x_1, f(x_1))$ and a particle of mass $1 - \lambda$ at $(x_2, f(x_2))$ lies above the graph of the curve $y = f(x)$.)

(i) Suppose $g:(a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ is twice differentiable with $g''(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \in (a,b)$. Suppose further that $a < x_1 < x_2 < b$ and $g(x_1) = g(x_2) = 0$. Sketch $g$. By using the mean value theorem twice, or otherwise, show that $g(t) \leq 0$ for all $t \in [x_1, x_2]$.

(ii) Suppose $f:(a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ is twice differentiable with $f''(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \in (a,b)$. Sketch $f$. By using (i), or otherwise, show that $f$ is convex.

We have now obtained a good supply of convex functions, and proceed to discuss a form of Jensen's inequality.

(iii) Suppose $f:(a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ is convex. By writing

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n+1} \lambda_j x_j = \lambda_{n+1} x_{n+1} + (1 - \lambda_{n+1}) y_{n+1}$$

and using induction, or otherwise, show that if

$$x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \in (a, b), \quad \lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n \geq 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{j=1}^n \lambda_j = 1,$$

then

$$\sum_{j=1}^n \lambda_j f(x_j) \geq f\left(\sum_{j=1}^n \lambda_j x_j\right).$$

This is Jensen's inequality.

(iv) The importance of Jensen's inequality lies in the fact that it has many classical inequalities as special cases. By taking $f(t) = -\log t$ and $\lambda_j = 1/n$, obtain the arithmetic-geometric inequality

$$\frac{x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n}{n} \geq (x_1 x_2 \cdots x_n)^{1/n}$$

whenever the $x_j$ are strictly positive real numbers.

[We give a more sophisticated account of these matters in Exercise K.143.]


### ex_k_41 [ex_k_41]

K.41. [4.4, P, ↑] (This uses Exercise K.40.) The four vertices $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ of a quadrilateral lie in anti-clockwise order on a circle of radius $a$ and center $O$. We write $2\theta_1 = \angle AOB$, $2\theta_2 = \angle BOC$, $2\theta_3 = \angle COD$, $2\theta_4 = \angle DOA$. Find the area of the quadrilateral and state the relation that $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ and $\theta_4$ must satisfy.

Use Jensen's inequality to find the largest possible area of a quadrilateral inscribed in a circle of radius $a$.

Use Jensen's inequality to find the largest possible area of an $n$-gon inscribed in a circle of radius $a$ [$n \geq 3$].

Use Jensen's inequality to find the smallest possible area of an $n$-gon circumscribing a circle of radius $a$ [$n \geq 3$].

[Compare Exercise K.325.]


### ex_k_42 [ex_k_42]

K.42. [4.4, P] Suppose that $g:[0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ is a continuous function such that, for every $c \in (0,1)$, we can find a $k$ with

$$0 < c - k < c < c + k < 1 \quad \text{and} \quad g(c) = \frac{1}{2}(g(c+k) + g(c-k)).$$

Show that, if $g(0) = g(1) = 0$, then $g(t) = 0$ for all $t \in [0,1]$. What can you say if we drop the conditions on $g(0)$ and $g(1)$?

(In one dimension this is just a brain teaser, but it turns out that the generalisation to higher dimensions is a branch of mathematics in itself.)


### ex_k_43 [ex_k_43]

K.43. [4.4, P] Define $f(x) = x^2 \sin(x^{-4})$ for $x \neq 0$, $f(0) = 0$. Show that $(f(h) - f(0))/h \to 0$ as $h \to 0$ and deduce that $f$ is differentiable at 0. Use the chain rule to show that $f$ is differentiable at all $x \neq 0$. Show that, although $f$ is everywhere differentiable, its derivative is not continuous.

[This example is extended in Exercise K.183.]


### ex_k_44 [ex_k_44]

K.44. (Darboux's theorem.) [4.4, T] Exercise K.43 shows that the derivative of a differentiable function need not be continuous. Oddly enough, it still satisfies a form of the intermediate value theorem. Suppose that $(\alpha, \beta) \supset [a, b]$, and that $f: (\alpha, \beta) \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable. Darboux's theorem asserts that, if $k$ lies between $f'(a)$ and $f'(b)$, then there is a $c \in [a, b]$ with $f'(c) = k$.

Explain why there is no loss in generality in supposing that $f'(a) > k > f'(b)$. Set $g(x) = f(x) - kx$. By looking at $g'(a)$ and $g'(b)$, show that $g$ cannot have a maximum at $a$ or $b$. Use the idea of the proof of Rolle's theorem (Theorem 4.52) to show that there exists a $c \in (a, b)$ with $g'(c) = 0$, and deduce Darboux's theorem.

Use Darboux's theorem to show that, if $(\alpha, \beta) \supseteq [a, b]$ and $f: (\alpha, \beta) \to \mathbb{R}$ is twice differentiable with a local maximum at $a$ and a local minimum at $b$, then $f''(c) = 0$ for some $c \in [a, b]$.

Darboux's theorem shows that not all functions can be derivatives. It is a very hard problem to characterise those functions $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ for which there exists an $F: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ with $F' = f$.


### ex_k_45 [ex_k_45]

K.45. [4.4, P] (i) Suppose $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is such that, for each $n \geq 1$, we can find $x_n$ and $y_n$ with $|x_n|, |y_n| \leq 1$, $|x_n - y_n| < n^{-1}$ and $|f(x_n) - f(y_n)| \geq 1$. Consider the following argument.

Pick $z_n \in [-1, 1]$ lying between $x_n$ and $y_n$. By the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem, we can find a sequence $n(j) \to \infty$ and a $z \in [-1, 1]$ such that $z_{n(j)} \to z$ and so $f$ must jump by at least 1 at $z$. Thus $f$ cannot be continuous at $z$ and in particular cannot be everywhere continuous.

Which parts of the argument are correct and which incorrect? Is the conclusion correct? Give a proof or counterexample.

(ii) Suppose $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and for each $n \geq 1$ we can find $x_n$ and $y_n$ with $|x_n|, |y_n| \leq 1$ such that $|x_n - y_n| < n^{-2}$ and $|f(x_n) - f(y_n)| \geq n^{-1}$. Consider the following argument.

By the mean value theorem, we can find $z_n \in [-1, 1]$ with $|f'(z_n)| \geq n$. By the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem, we can find a sequence $n(j) \to \infty$ and a $z \in [-1, 1]$ such that $z_{n(j)} \to z$, and so $f'(z)$ (if it exists) must be arbitrarily large. Thus $f$ cannot be differentiable at $z$ and in particular cannot be everywhere differentiable.

Which parts of the argument are correct and which incorrect? Is the conclusion correct? Give a proof or counterexample.


### ex_k_46 [ex_k_46]

K.46. [4.4, H] In this exercise we examine our proof of Rolle's theorem (Theorem 4.52) in detail.

(i) Obtain Rolle's theorem as the consequence of the following three statements.

(a) If $g:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous, we can find $k_1, k_2 \in [a,b]$ such that $g(k_2) \leq g(x) \leq g(k_1)$ for all $x \in [a,b]$.

(b) If $g:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is a function with $g(a) = g(b)$ and we can find $k_1, k_2 \in [a,b]$ such that $g(k_2) \leq g(x) \leq g(k_1)$ for all $x \in [a,b]$, then at least one of the following three statements is true: $a < k_1 < b$, $a < k_2 < b$, or $g$ is constant.

(c) If $g:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable at a point $c$ with $a < c < b$ such that $g(x) \leq g(c)$ for all $x \in [a,b]$ (or such that $g(c) \leq g(x)$ for all $x \in [a,b]$), then $g'(c) = 0$.

(ii) Suppose we now work over $\mathbb{Q}$ as we did in Example 1.3. Prove that the following results still hold.

(b') If $b > a$ and $f: \mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{Q}$ is a function with $f(a) = f(b)$ with the property that we can find $a \leq k_1, k_2 \leq b$ such that $f(k_2) \leq f(x) \leq f(k_1)$ for all $a \leq x \leq b$, then at least one of the following three statements is true: $a < k_1 < b$, $a < k_2 < b$ or $f$ is constant.

(c') If $b > a$ and $f: \mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{Q}$ is differentiable at a point $c$ with $a < c < b$ such that $f(x) \leq f(c)$ for all $a \leq x \leq b$ (or such that $f(c) \leq f(x)$ for all $a \leq x \leq b$), then $f'(c) = 0$.

This shows that the key to our proof of the mean value theorem is the fact that, if we work with the reals, a continuous function on a closed bounded interval is bounded and attains its bounds. All the rest is mere algebra.


### ex_k_47 [ex_k_47]

K.47. [4.4, H, S] (This is more of a comment than an exercise.)

Occasionally even a respected mathematician may fall into the trap of believing that 'Theorem 4.52 is subtle only because we do not ask the derivative to be continuous and do not include the endpoints'. It is worth pointing out that, even in this case, there is no simple proof. If you ever find yourself believing that there is a simple proof, check it against the following example.

Consider the function $f: \mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{Q}$ given in Example 1.3. Set

$$g(t) = 1 - t + f(t).$$

Show that the function $g: \mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{Q}$ has continuous derivative $g'$ and that $g'$ satisfies the conclusions of the intermediate value theorem. (More formally, if $a, b \in \mathbb{Q}$ with $a < b$ and $\gamma \in \mathbb{Q}$ is such that either $g'(a) \leq \gamma \leq g'(b)$ or $g'(b) \leq \gamma \leq g'(a)$, then there exists a $c \in \mathbb{Q}$ with $a \leq c \leq b$ such that $g'(c) = \gamma$. The statement is complicated, but the verification is trivial.)

Show that $g(0) = g(2) = 0$, but that there does not exist a $c \in \mathbb{Q}$ with $g'(c) = 0$.


### ex_k_48 [ex_k_48]

K.48. (Tchebychev polynomials.) [4.4, G, P] Prove that

$$(\cos \theta + i \sin \theta)^n = \cos n\theta + i \sin n\theta,$$

whenever $n$ is a positive integer. (This is just to get you started, so, provided you make clear what you are assuming, you may make any assumptions you wish.)

By taking real and imaginary parts, show that there is a real polynomial of degree $n$ such that

$$T_n(\cos \theta) = \cos n\theta \quad \text{for all real } \theta.$$

Write down $T_0(t)$, $T_1(t)$, $T_2(t)$, and $T_3(t)$ explicitly.

Prove that, if $n \geq 1$:

(a) $T_{n+1}(t) = 2tT_n(t) - T_{n-1}(t)$ for all $|t| \leq 1$,

(b) $T_{n+1}(t) = 2tT_n(t) - T_{n-1}(t)$ for all $t$,

(c) the coefficient of $t^n$ in $T_n(t)$ is $2^{n-1}$,

(d) $|T_n(t)| \leq 1$ for all $|t| \leq 1$, and

(e) $T_n$ has $n$ distinct roots all in $(-1, 1)$.

Show also that, if $n$, $m \geq 1$, then

$$\int_{-1}^{1} \frac{T_n(x) T_m(x)}{(1 - x^2)^{1/2}} dx = \frac{\pi}{2} \delta_{nm},$$

where $\delta_{nm} = 0$ if $n \neq m$ and $\delta_{nn} = 1$. What can you say if $m = 0$ and $n \geq 0$?

The $T_n$ are called the Tchebychev polynomials, after their discoverer.


### ex_k_49 [ex_k_49]

K.49. [4.4, T, ↑] (This requires Exercises 4.58 and K.48.) Reread Exercise 4.58. Suppose now that $a = -1$, $b = 1$ and we choose the $x_j$ of Exercise 4.58 to be the $n$ roots of $T_n$ (see part (d) of Exercise 4.58). Using part (b) of Exercise 4.58, show that $\prod_{j=1}^{n}(t - x_j) = 2^{-n+1}T_n(t)$. Conclude that, if $|f^{(n)}(x)| \leq A$ for all $x \in [-1,1]$, then

$$|f(t) - P(t)| \leq \frac{A}{2^{n-1} n!}$$

for all $t \in [-1, 1]$.

Modify the ideas above to deal with a general interval $[a, b]$.

(Note that, whilst it is, indeed, true that, if you are going to interpolate a function by a polynomial, the zeros of the Tchebychev polynomial are good points of interpolation, it remains the case that it is rarely a good idea to interpolate a function by a polynomial of high degree. To see why the inequalities established in this question are less useful than they seem observe that $\sup_{x \in [a,b]} |f^{(n)}(x)|$ may grow very fast with $n$. For another example involving choosing appropriate points, see Exercise K.238.)


### ex_k_50 [ex_k_50]

K.50. (The $n$th mean value theorem.) [4.4, T] The object of this exercise is to prove a version of Taylor's theorem corresponding to Theorem 4.49 (the one-dimensional mean value theorem).

(i) Reread Exercise 4.58.

(ii) Suppose that $f:(u,v) \to \mathbb{R}$ is $n + 1$ times differentiable and that $[a,b] \subseteq (u,v)$. Show that we can find a polynomial $P$ of degree $n$ such that $(f - P)^{(r)}(a) = 0$ for $r = 0, 1, \ldots, n$. Give $P$ explicitly.

(iii) We are interested in the error

$$E(b) = f(b) - P(b).$$

To this end, we consider the function

$$F(x) = f(x) - P(x) - E(b) \left(\frac{x-a}{b-a}\right)^{n+1}.$$

Show that $F(a) = F'(a) = \cdots = F^{(n)}(a) = 0$ and $F(b) = 0$.

(iv) By using Rolle's theorem, show that $F'(c_1) = 0$ for some $c_1$ with $a < c_1 < b$. By repeating your argument $n$ times, show that $F^{(n+1)}(c) = 0$ for some $c$ with $a < c < b$.

(v) Deduce that

$$0 = f^{(n+1)}(c) - \frac{(n+1)!}{(b-a)^{n+1}} E(b),$$

and so

$$f(b) - \sum_{j=0}^{n} \frac{f^{(j)}(a)}{j!} (b-a)^j = \frac{f^{(n+1)}(c)}{(n+1)!} (b-a)^{n+1}$$

for some $c$ with $a < c < b$.

(vi) Show that

$$f(a) - \sum_{j=0}^{n} \frac{f^{(j)}(b)}{j!} (a-b)^j = \frac{f^{(n+1)}(c')}{(n+1)!} (a-b)^{n+1}$$

for some $c'$ with $a < c' < b$.

(vii) Suppose that $f, g: (u, v) \to \mathbb{R}$ are $n + 1$ times differentiable and that $a \in (u, v)$. Show that, if

$$f(a) = f'(a) = \cdots = f^{(n)}(a) = g(a) = g'(a) = \cdots = g^{(n)}(a) = 0$$

but $g^{(n+1)}(a) \neq 0$, then, if $f^{(n+1)}$ and $g^{(n+1)}$ are continuous at $a$, it follows that

$$\frac{f(t)}{g(t)} \to \frac{f^{(n+1)}(a)}{g^{(n+1)}(a)}$$

as $t \to a$.


### ex_k_51 [ex_k_51]

K.51. [4.4, P, ↑] Suppose that $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is $2n + 2$ times differentiable. By considering polynomials of the form $x^k(1 - x)^l$, or otherwise, show that there is a unique polynomial $P$ of degree $2n + 1$ such that

$$P^{(r)}(0) = f^{(r)}(0) \quad \text{and} \quad P^{(r)}(1) = f^{(r)}(1) \quad \text{for all } 0 \leq r \leq n.$$

Show that the error $E(y) = f(y) - P(y)$ at $y \in [0, 1]$ is given by

$$E(y) = \frac{f^{(2n+2)}(\xi)}{(2n+2)!} y^{n+1}(y-1)^{n+1},$$

for some $\xi \in (0, 1)$.


### ex_k_52 [ex_k_52]

K.52. (Cauchy's mean value theorem.) [4.4, P] Suppose that $f$, $g: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ are continuous functions with $f$ and $g$ differentiable on $(a, b)$ and $g'(x) \neq 0$ for $x \in (a, b)$.

(i) Use the mean value theorem (Theorem 4.49) to show that $g(a) \neq g(b)$.

(ii) Show that we can find a real number $A$ such that, setting

$$h(t) = f(t) - A g(t),$$

the function $h$ satisfies the conditions of Rolle's theorem (Theorem 4.52).

(iii) By applying Rolle's theorem to the function $h$ in (ii), show that there exists a $c \in (a, b)$ such that

$$\frac{f(b) - f(a)}{g(b) - g(a)} = \frac{f'(c)}{g'(c)}.$$

(This is Cauchy's mean value theorem.)

(iv) Suppose that $f(a) = g(a) = 0$ and $f'(t)/g'(t) \to L$ as $t \to a$ through values of $t > a$. Prove the version of L'Hôpital's rule which states that, under these conditions, $f(t)/g(t) \to L$ as $t \to a$ through values of $t > a$.

(v) Suppose that $f, g: (u, v) \to \mathbb{R}$ are differentiable, that $a \in (u, v)$, $f(a) = g(a) = 0$ and $f'(t)/g'(t) \to L$ as $t \to a$. Show that $f(t)/g(t) \to L$ as $t \to a$.

(vi) Suppose that $f, g: (u, v) \to \mathbb{R}$ are differentiable, that $a \in (u, v)$,

$$f(a) = f'(a) = \cdots = f^{(n-1)}(a) = g(a) = g'(a) = g''(a) = \cdots = g^{(n-1)} = 0$$

and $f^{(n)}(t)/g^{(n)}(t) \to L$ as $t \to a$. Show that $f(t)/g(t) \to L$ as $t \to a$.

(vii) Suppose that $F: (u, v) \to \mathbb{R}$ is $n$ times differentiable, that $a \in (u, v)$ and that $F^{(n)}$ is continuous at $a$. By setting

$$f(t) = F(t) - \sum_{j=0}^{n} \frac{F^{(j)}(a)}{j!} (t-a)^j$$

and $g(t) = (t - a)^n$ and applying (vi), show that

$$F(t) = \sum_{j=0}^{n} \frac{F^{(j)}(a)}{j!} (t-a)^j + \epsilon(t) |t - a|^n$$

where $\epsilon(t) \to 0$ as $t \to a$. (This is another version of the local Taylor's theorem, which we obtain by other arguments as Theorem 7.3.)


### ex_k_53 [ex_k_53]

K.53. [4.4, P, ↑↑] (i) Write down the result of Exercise K.50 (v) in the specific case $n = 2$, $b - a = h$.

(ii) Throughout the rest of this exercise, $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ will be an everywhere twice differentiable function. Suppose that $|f(t)| \leq M_0$ and $|f''(t)| \leq M_2$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$. Show that

$$|f'(t)| \leq \frac{2M_0}{h} + \frac{hM_2}{2}$$

for all $h > 0$, and deduce, by choosing $h$ carefully, that

$$|f'(t)| \leq \sqrt{M_0 M_2}$$

for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$.

(iii) Which of the following statements are true and which false? Give a proof or counterexample as appropriate.

(a) Let $a < b$. If $|f(t)| \leq M_0$ and $|f''(t)| \leq M_2$ for all $t \in [a, b]$, then $|f'(a)| \leq \sqrt{M_0 M_2}$.

(b) There exists an $L > 0$ such that, if $a + L \geq b$, $|f(t)| \leq M_0$ and $|f''(t)| \leq M_2$ for all $t \in [a, b]$, then $|f'(a)| \leq \sqrt{M_0 M_2}$.

(c) Given $M_0, M_2 > 0$, we can find an $L > 0$ such that, if $a + L \geq b$, $|f(t)| \leq M_0$ and $|f''(t)| \leq M_2$ for all $t \in [a, b]$, then $|f'(a)| \leq \sqrt{M_0 M_2}$.

(d) There exists an infinitely differentiable function $g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ with $|g(t)| \leq 1$, $|g''(t)| \leq 1$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ and $g'(0) = 1$.

(e) Given $M_0, M_2 > 0$, we can find an infinitely differentiable function $G: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ with $|G(t)| \leq M_0$ and $|G''(t)| \leq M_2$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ and $G'(0) = \sqrt{M_0 M_2}$.

(f) There exists a constant $A$ such that, if $|f(t)| \leq M_0$ and $|f''(t)| \leq M_2$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, then $|f'(t)| \leq A(M_0 M_2)^{1/4}$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$.


### ex_k_54 [ex_k_54]

K.54. [4.5, P] We consider functions $f:(0,1) \to \mathbb{R}$. Give a proof or a counterexample for each of these statements:

(i) If $f$ is uniformly continuous, then $f$ is bounded.

(ii) If $f$ is uniformly continuous, then $f$ is bounded and attains its bounds.

(iii) If $f$ is continuous and bounded and attains its bounds, then $f$ is uniformly continuous.


### ex_k_55 [ex_k_55]

K.55. [4.5, P] Give a proof or a counterexample for each of these statements.

(i) If $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $|f(x)|$ tends to a limit as $|x| \to \infty$, then $f$ is uniformly continuous.

(ii) If $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is uniformly continuous, then $|f(x)|$ tends to a limit as $|x| \to \infty$.

(iii) If $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and bounded, then $f$ is uniformly continuous.

(iv) If $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is uniformly continuous, then $f$ is bounded.

(v) If $f: \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C}$ is continuous and $|f(z)|$ tends to a limit as $|z| \to \infty$, then $f$ is uniformly continuous.

(vi) If $f: \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C}$ is uniformly continuous, then so is $|f|: \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{R}$.

(vii) If $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{C}$ is continuous and $|f|: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is uniformly continuous, then $f$ is uniformly continuous.

(viii) If $f, g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ are uniformly continuous, then so is their product $f \times g$.

(ix) If $f, g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ are uniformly continuous, then so is their composition $f \circ g$.


### ex_k_56 [ex_k_56]

K.56. [4.5, P] If $E$ is a non-empty subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$, we define $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ by

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \inf \{\|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y}\| : \mathbf{y} \in E\}.$$

Show that $f$ is uniformly continuous.

Show also that $E$ is closed if and only if, given $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, we can find $\mathbf{y} \in E$ such that $\|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y}\| = f(\mathbf{x})$.


### ex_k_57 [ex_k_57]

K.57. [4.5, P] Suppose that $f: \mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{R}$ is uniformly continuous on $\mathbb{Q}$. The object of this exercise is to show that $f$ has a unique continuous extension to $\mathbb{R}$.

(i) By using the general principle of convergence, show that, if $x \in \mathbb{R}$, $x_n \in \mathbb{Q}$ ($n \geq 1$) and $x_n \to x$ as $n \to \infty$, then $f(x_n)$ tends to a limit.

(ii) Show that, if $x \in \mathbb{R}$ and $x_n, y_n \in \mathbb{Q}$ [$n \geq 1$] are such that $x_n \to x$ and $y_n \to x$, then $f(x_n)$ and $f(y_n)$ tend to the same limit.

(iii) Conclude that there is a unique $F: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ defined by $f(x_n) \to F(x)$ whenever $x_n \in \mathbb{Q}$ and $x_n \to x$.

(iv) Explain why $F(x) = f(x)$ whenever $x \in \mathbb{Q}$.

(v) Show that $F$ is uniformly continuous.

[See also Exercise K.333.]


### ex_k_58 [ex_k_58]

K.58. [4.6, P] Suppose that the power series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ has radius of convergence $R$ and the power series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} b_n z^n$ has radius of convergence $S$.

(i) Show that, if $R \neq S$, then $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(a_n + b_n)z^n$ has radius of convergence $\min(R, S)$.

(ii) Show that, if $R = S$, then $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(a_n + b_n)z^n$ has radius of convergence $T \geq R$.

(iii) Continuing with the notation of (ii), show, by means of examples, that $T$ can take any value with $T \geq R$.

(iv) If $\lambda \neq 0$, find the radius of convergence of $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \lambda a_n z^n$. What happens if $\lambda = 0$?

(v) If $\infty > R > 0$, find the radius of convergence of $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^{n^2}$. (It may help to start by considering the special case $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 2^n z^{n^2}$.) What, if anything, can be said about the cases $R = 0$ and $R = \infty$?

(vi) Investigate what, if anything, we can say about the radius of convergence of $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} c_n z^n$ if $|c_n| = \max(|a_n|, |b_n|)$. Do the same if $|c_n| = \min(|a_n|, |b_n|)$.


### ex_k_59 [ex_k_59]

K.59. [4.6, P] Let $E$ be a subset of the positive integers and let $h(n)$ be the number of elements in the set $\{m \in E : m \leq n\}$. We define a sequence $a_n$ by taking $a_0 = 1$ and setting $a_{n+1} = a_n/2$ if $n \in E$, $a_{n+1} = 2a_n$ if $n \notin E$.

If $h(n)/n \to \gamma$, show that the radius of convergence of $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ depends only on $\gamma$, and find it. Show, by means of examples, that we can choose $E$ so that $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ diverges everywhere on its circle of convergence and that we can choose $E$ so that $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ converges everywhere on its circle of convergence.


### ex_k_60 [ex_k_60]

K.60. [4.6, T] Consider the power series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$. Show that, if the sequence $|a_n|^{1/n}$ is bounded, then $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n$ has infinite radius of convergence if $\limsup_{n \to \infty} |a_n|^{1/n} = 0$, and radius of convergence

$$R = \left(\limsup_{n \to \infty} |a_n|^{1/n}\right)^{-1}$$

otherwise. What can we say if the sequence $|a_n|^{1/n}$ is unbounded? Prove your statement.

This formula for the radius of convergence is of considerable theoretical importance but is hard to handle as a practical tool. It is usually best to use the definition directly.


### ex_k_61 [ex_k_61]

K.61. [4.6, T] Suppose that the sequence $a_n$ of strictly positive real numbers has the property that there exists a $K \geq 1$ with $K \geq a_{n+1}/a_n \geq K^{-1}$ for all $n \geq 1$. Prove that

$$\liminf_{n \to \infty}(a_{n+1}/a_n) \leq \liminf_{n \to \infty} a_n^{1/n} \leq \limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n^{1/n} \leq \limsup_{n \to \infty}(a_{n+1}/a_n).$$

Conclude that, if $a_{n+1}/a_n \to l$ as $n \to \infty$, then $a_n^{1/n} \to l$.

Give examples of sequences $a_n$ such that

(i) $a_n^{1/n} \to l$ for some $l$, but

$$\liminf_{n \to \infty}(a_{n+1}/a_n) < l < \limsup_{n \to \infty}(a_{n+1}/a_n).$$

(ii) $\liminf_{n \to \infty}(a_{n+1}/a_n) = \limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n^{1/n} < \limsup_{n \to \infty}(a_{n+1}/a_n)$.

In how many different ways can you replace the $\leq$ in the initial formula by combinations of $<$ and $=$? Can all these possibilities occur? Justify your answer.

Comment very briefly on the connection with formulae for the radius of convergence of a power series given in Exercise K.60.


### ex_k_62 [ex_k_62]

K.62. (Summation methods.) [4.6, T] If $b_j \in \mathbb{R}$, let us write

$$\mathcal{C}_n = \frac{b_0 + b_1 + \cdots + b_n}{n+1}.$$

(i) Let $\epsilon > 0$. Show that, if $|b_j| \leq \epsilon$ for all $j \geq 0$, then $|\mathcal{C}_n| \leq \epsilon$ for all $n \geq 0$.

(ii) Show that, if $N \geq 0$ and $b_j = 0$ for all $n \geq N$, then $\mathcal{C}_n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

(iii) Show that, if $b_j \to 0$ as $j \to \infty$, then $\mathcal{C}_n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

(iv) By considering $b_j - b$, or otherwise, show that, if $b_j \to b$ as $j \to \infty$, then $\mathcal{C}_n \to b$ as $n \to \infty$.

(v) Let $b_j = (-1)^j$. Show that $\mathcal{C}_n$ tends to a limit (to be found) but $b_j$ does not.

(vi) Let $b_{2^m + k} = (-1)^m$ for $0 \leq k \leq 2^m - 1$ and $m \geq 1$. Show that $\mathcal{C}_n$ does not tend to a limit as $n \to \infty$.

(vii) If $0 < r < 1$ and $b_j \in \mathbb{R}$, let us write

$$\mathcal{A}_r = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (1-r) r^n b_n.$$

Show that, if $b_j \to b$ as $j \to \infty$, then $\mathcal{A}_r \to b$ as $r \to 1$ through values of $r < 1$. Give an example where $\mathcal{A}_r$ tends to a limit but $b_j$ does not. Show that there exists a sequence $b_j$ with $|b_j| \leq 1$ where $\mathcal{A}_r$ does not tend to a limit as $r \to 1$ through values of $r < 1$.

(viii) Let $\mathbf{b}_j \in \mathbb{R}^m$. Show that, if $\mathbf{b}_j \to \mathbf{b}$ as $j \to \infty$, then

$$\frac{\mathbf{b}_0 + \mathbf{b}_1 + \cdots + \mathbf{b}_n}{n+1} \to \mathbf{b}$$

You should give two proofs.

(A) By looking at coordinates and using the result for $\mathbb{R}$.

(B) Directly, not using earlier results.

(ix) Explain briefly why (vii) can be generalised in the manner of (viii).


### ex_k_63 [ex_k_63]

K.63. [4.6, T, ↑] We continue with the notation of Exercise K.62. We call the limit of $\mathcal{C}_n$, if it exists, the Cesàro limit of the sequence $b_j$. We call the limit of $\mathcal{A}_r$, if it exists, the Abel limit of the sequence $b_j$. Now suppose $a_j \in \mathbb{R}$ and we set $b_j = \sum_{k=0}^j a_k$.

(i) Show that

$$\mathcal{C}_n = \frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{k=0}^n (n+1-k) a_k$$

and

$$\mathcal{A}_r = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r^k a_k.$$

If $\mathcal{C}_n$ tends to a limit, we say that the sequence $a_j$ has that limit as a Cesàro sum and that the sequence $a_j$ is Cesàro summable. If $\mathcal{A}_r$ tends to a limit, we say that the sequence $a_j$ has that limit as an Abel sum and that the sequence $a_j$ is Abel summable.

(ii) Explain very briefly why the results of Exercise K.62 (viii) and (ix) show that we can extend the definitions of (i) to the case $a_j \in \mathbb{C}$.

(iii) Let $a_j = z^j$ with $z \in \mathbb{C}$.

(a) Show that $\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} z^j$ converges if and only if $|z| < 1$, and find the sum when it exists.

(b) Show that the sequence $z^j$ is Cesàro summable if and only if $|z| \leq 1$ and $z \neq 1$. Find the Cesàro sum when it exists.

(c) Show that the sequence $z^j$ is Abel summable if and only if $|z| \leq 1$ and $z \neq 1$. Find the Abel sum when it exists.


### ex_k_64 [ex_k_64]

K.64. [4.6, T, ↑] (This generalises parts of Exercise K.62.) Suppose that $u_{jk} \in \mathbb{R}$ for $j, k \geq 0$. Suppose that:

(1) If $k$ is fixed, $u_{jk} \to 0$ as $j \to \infty$.

(2) $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} u_{jk}$ is absolutely convergent, and there exists an $M$ such that $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} |u_{jk}| \leq M$ for each $j \geq 0$.

(3) $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} u_{jk} \to 1$ as $j \to \infty$.

(i) Show that, if $b_j \in \mathbb{R}$ and $b_j \to b$ as $j \to \infty$, then

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} u_{jk} b_k \to b$$

as $j \to \infty$.

(ii) Explain why the result just proved gives part (iv) of Exercise K.62.

(iii) Let $0 < r_n < 1$ and $r_n \to 1$. Use (i) to show that, in the notation of Exercise K.62 (vii), if $b_j \to b$ as $j \to \infty$, then $\mathcal{A}_{r_n} \to b$ as $n \to \infty$. Deduce Exercise K.62 (vii).

(iv) State and prove an extension of part (i) along the lines of Exercise K.62 (viii).

(v) If $\lambda > 0$ and $b_j \in \mathbb{C}$, let us write

$$\mathcal{B}_{\lambda} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^n}{n!} e^{-\lambda} b_n.$$

Show that, if $b_j \to b$ as $j \to \infty$, then $\mathcal{B}_{\lambda} \to b$ as $\lambda \to \infty$.

(vi) Much as in the introduction to Exercise K.63, we suppose $a_j \in \mathbb{C}$ and set $b_j = \sum_{r=0}^j a_j$.
