K.141. [8.3, T] The object of this exercise is to define the logarithm and the real exponential function, so no properties of those functions should be used. You should quote all theorems that you use, paying particular attention to those on integration.

We set $l(x) = \int_1^x \frac{1}{t} dt$.

(i) Explain why $l:(0,\infty)\to \mathbb{R}$ is a well defined function.

(ii) Use the change of variable formula for integrals to show that

$$\int_x^{xy} \frac{1}{t} dt = l(y)$$

whenever $x, y > 0$. Deduce that $l(xy) = l(x) + l(y)$.

(iii) Show that $l$ is everywhere differentiable with $l'(x) = 1/x$.

(iv) Show that $l$ is a strictly increasing function.

(v) Show that $l(x)\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

(vi) Show that $l:(0,\infty)\to \mathbb{R}$ is a bijective function.

(vii) In Section 5.6 we defined the logarithm as the inverse of the exponential function $e: \mathbb{R} \to (0, \infty)$. Turn this procedure on its head by defining $e$ as the inverse of $l$. Derive the main properties of $e$, taking particular care to quote those theorems on inverse functions that you require. When you have finished, glance through Section 5.4 to see if you have proved all the properties of the (real) exponential given there.

(viii) Check that

$$\frac{1}{1 - t} = 1 + t + \cdots + t^n + \frac{t^{n+1}}{1 - t}$$

for all $t \neq 1$, and show that

$$\left| \int_0^x \frac{t^{n+1}}{1 - t} dt \right| \leq \frac{|x|^{n+2}}{1 - |x|}$$

for all $|x| < 1$. Use these observations to obtain a Taylor series for $\log(1 - x)$, giving the range over which your argument is valid.

(ix) Use (viii) to find the Taylor series of $\log((1 + x)/(1 - x))$, giving the range over which your argument is valid. Show that

$$\log y = 2\left(\frac{y - 1}{y + 1} + \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{y - 1}{y + 1}\right)^3 + \frac{1}{5}\left(\frac{y - 1}{y + 1}\right)^5 + \cdots\right)$$

for all $y > 0$.


## app_k_142 [app_k_142]


K.142. [8.3, P, S, ↑] Use the Taylor series for $\log(1 + x)$ and some result on products like Exercise 5.38 to obtain

$$(\log(1 + x))^2 = 2\sum_{n=2}^\infty \frac{(-1)^n}{n} S_{n-1} x^n$$

for all $|x| < 1$, where $S_m = \sum_{n=1}^m 1/n$.


## app_k_143 [app_k_143]


K.143. (Convex functions.) [8.3, T] Recall from Exercise K.40 that we call a function $f:(a,b)\to \mathbb{R}$ convex if, whenever $x_1, x_2 \in (a,b)$ and $1 \geq \lambda \geq 0$, we have

$$\lambda f(x_1) + (1 - \lambda)f(x_2) \geq f(\lambda x_1 + (1 - \lambda)x_2).$$

(i) Suppose that $a < x_1 < x_2 < x_3 < b$. Show algebraically that

$$\frac{f(x_2) - f(x_1)}{x_2 - x_1} \leq \frac{f(x_3) - f(x_2)}{x_3 - x_2},$$

and illustrate the result graphically.

(ii) If $c \in (a, b)$, show that

$$\sigma_f(c+) = \inf \left\{\frac{f(c+h) - f(c)}{h} : c < c + h < b \right\}$$

exists and

$$\frac{f(c+h) - f(c)}{h} \rightarrow \sigma_f(c+)$$

as $h \to 0$ through values $h > 0$. State and prove the appropriate result for $\sigma_f(c-)$. Show also that $\sigma_f(c+) \geq \sigma_f(c-)$.

(iii) Using (ii), or otherwise, show that $f$ is continuous at $c$. (Thus a convex function is automatically continuous.)

(iv) Using (ii), or otherwise, show that we can find a real $B$ such that

$$f(x) - f(c) \geq B(x - c)$$

for all $x \in (a, b)$. (Notice that, if $\sigma_f(c+) = \sigma_f(c-)$, then $f$ is differentiable at $c$, $B = f'(c)$ and the line $y - f(c) = B(x - c)$ is the tangent, but that if $\sigma_f(c+) \neq \sigma_f(c-)$, then $B$ is not unique and $f$ is not differentiable at $c$. Exercise K.144 is relevant here.)

(v) Suppose $a < \alpha < \beta < b$. Let $g: [\alpha, \beta] \to \mathbb{R}$ be a positive continuous function with $\int_\alpha^\beta g(t) dt = 1$. Show that $c = \int_\alpha^\beta t g(t) dt \in [\alpha, \beta]$ and prove, using (iv), or otherwise, that

$$\int_\alpha^\beta f(t)g(t) dt \geq f\left(\int_\alpha^\beta t g(t) dt\right).$$

(vi) Prove the result of Exercise K.40(iii) by the method of (iv). If you know a little probability, use the method of (iv) to show that if $X$ is a random variable taking values in $[\alpha, \beta]$ we have

$$\mathbb{E}f(X) \geq f(\mathbb{E}X).$$

(vii) If you have done Exercise K.32(iii), try and obtain part (iv) as a consequence.


## app_k_144 [app_k_144]


K.144. [8.3, T, ↑] (i) By considering the map $x \to |x|$, or otherwise, show that a convex function need not be differentiable everywhere.

(ii) We use the assumptions and notation of Exercise K.143(ii). Show that, if $a < \alpha \leq c_1 < c_2 < \cdots < c_N \leq \beta < b$, then

$$\sum_{j=1}^N (\sigma_f(c_j+) - \sigma_f(c_j-)) \leq \sigma_f(\beta+) - \sigma_f(\alpha-).$$

Deduce, by using a 'hamburger argument' (see page 362), that the set of points in $[\alpha, \beta]$ where $f$ is not differentiable is countable. Deduce that $f$ is differentiable on $(a, b)$ except at a countable set of points.

(iii) Show that, if $f, g: (a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ are convex, then so are $f + g$ and $\mu f$ when $\mu \geq 0$. If $f_n: (a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ is convex for each $n$ and $f: (a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ is such that $f_n(x) \to f(x)$ as $n \to \infty$ for each $x \in (a,b)$, show that $f$ is convex.

(iv) Construct an $f: (-1, 1) \to \mathbb{R}$ which is convex but is not differentiable at any rational point.


## app_k_145 [app_k_145]


K.145. [8.3, H, S] (i) Let $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. Let $a_n < c < b_n$ and $a_n, b_n \to c$ as $n \to \infty$. Write $I_n = [a_n, b_n]$, $|I_n| = b_n - a_n$ and $\int_{I_n} f(t) dt = \int_{a_n}^{b_n} f(t) dt$. Show, by using the method of proof of Theorem 8.37, but not the theorem itself, that

$$\frac{1}{|I_n|} \int_{I_n} f(t) dt \rightarrow f(c).$$

(ii) How might this result generalise to higher dimensions (so we consider $f: \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}$) and how might we prove the generalisation? (Without more work we cannot give a rigorous proof, but we can certainly see how the proof ought to run.)


### app_k_146 [app_k_146]


K.146. [8.3, H!] We saw in Exercise K.130 that there is no hope of a successful theory of integration for functions $f: \mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{Q}$. None the less, I think that looking at such functions can illuminate the role played by uniform continuity in the proof of Theorem 8.32.

(i) Suppose $f:[0,1]\cap \mathbb{Q}\to \mathbb{Q}$ is uniformly continuous. Show that, given any $\epsilon >0$, we can find a dissection $\mathcal{D}$ of $[0,1]\cap \mathbb{Q}$ such that

$$S (f, \mathcal {D}) - s (f, \mathcal {D}) < \epsilon .$$

In what follows we shall construct a function $g:[0,1]\cap \mathbb{Q}\to \mathbb{Q}$ which is bounded and continuous but has the property that

$$S (g, \mathcal {D}) - s (g, \mathcal {D}) \geq 1$$

for every dissection $\mathcal{D}$ of $[0,1]\cap \mathbb{Q}$.

(ii) We start by working in $\mathbb{R}$. We say that an interval $J$ is 'good' if

$$J = \left\{x \in [ 0, 1 ]: (x - q) ^ {2} \leq 2 ^ {- m} \right\}$$

with $q$ rational and $m$ a strictly positive integer. We enumerate the elements of $[0,1] \cap \mathbb{Q}$ as a sequence $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots$ of distinct elements.

Show that, setting $S_0 = \varnothing$, we can construct inductively finite collections $S_n$ of disjoint good sets such that:

(a) $\mathcal{S}_n = \mathcal{S}_{n - 1}\cup \mathcal{K}_n\cup \mathcal{L}_n$ with $\mathcal{S}_{n - 1}$, $\mathcal{K}_{n}$ and $\mathcal{L}_n$ disjoint.

(b) The total lengths of the intervals in $\mathcal{K}_n\cup \mathcal{L}_n$ is less than $2^{-n - 3}$.

(c) If $J$ is a subinterval of $[0,1]$ of length at least $2^{-n}$ with the property that $J \cap \bigcup_{I \in S_{n-1}} I = \emptyset$, then we can find a $K \in \mathcal{K}_n$ and an $L \in \mathcal{L}_n$ such that $J \supseteq K \cup L$.

(d) $x_{n}\in \bigcup_{I\in \mathcal{S}_{n}}I$

(iii) Show that the total length of the intervals making up $S_{n}$ is always less than $1/4$.

Suppose that

$$\mathcal {D} = \left\{a _ {0}, a _ {1}, \dots , a _ {p} \right\} \text { with } 0 = a _ {0} \leq a _ {1} \leq a _ {2} \leq \dots \leq a _ {p} = 1$$

is a dissection of $[0,1]$. Show that, provided that $n$ is sufficiently large, the total length of those intervals $[a_{j-1}, a_j]$ with $1 \leq j \leq m$ such that we can find a $K \in \mathcal{K}_n$ and an $L \in \mathcal{L}_n$ with $[a_{j-1}, a_j] \supseteq K \cup L$ will be at least $1/2$.

(iv) Show that, if $q \in \mathbb{Q} \cap [0,1]$, then there is a unique $n \geq 1$ such that

$$q \in \bigcup_ {I \in \mathcal {S} _ {n} \backslash \mathcal {S} _ {n - 1}} I.$$

Explain why $q$ lies in exactly one of $\bigcup_{K \in \mathcal{K}_n} K$ or $\bigcup_{L \in \mathcal{L}_n} L$. We define $g(q) = 1$ if $q \in \bigcup_{K \in \mathcal{K}_n} K$ and $g(q) = -1$ if $q \in \bigcup_{L \in \mathcal{L}_n} L$.

(v) We now work in $\mathbb{Q}$. Show that $g:[0,1]\cap \mathbb{Q}\to \mathbb{Q}$ is a bounded continuous function. By using (iii), or otherwise, show that

$$S (g, \mathcal {D}) - s (g, \mathcal {D}) \geq 1$$

for every dissection $\mathcal{D}$ of $[0,1]\cap \mathbb{Q}$.


### app_k_147 [app_k_147]


K.147. [8.4, P] Suppose that $g: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ is a differentiable function with continuous partial derivatives. Suppose that $g: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ is a differentiable function with continuous partial derivatives. Examiners often ask you to show that

$$G (x) = \int_ {0} ^ {x} g (x, t) d t$$

is differentiable and determine its derivatives.

(i) Why can you not just quote Theorem 8.57?

(ii) Set $F(x,y) = \int_0^y g(x,t)dt$. Show that $F$ has partial derivatives. Show further that these partial derivatives are continuous.

(iii) Use the chain rule to show that $G$ is differentiable and determine its derivatives.

(iv) Suppose that $h: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable. Show that

$$H (x) = \int_ {0} ^ {h (x)} g (x, t) d t$$

is differentiable, and determine its derivatives.


### app_k_148 [app_k_148]


K.148. [8.4, P] The following exercise is included because it uses several of our theorems in a rather neat manner. (It is actually a conservation of energy result.) Suppose that $u: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ has continuous partial second derivatives, that

$$\frac {\\partial^ {2} u}{\partial x ^ {2}} (x, t) = \frac {\partial^ {2} u}{\partial t ^ {2}} (x, t) \text { and that } \frac {\partial u}{\partial t} (0, t) = \frac {\partial u}{\partial t} (1, t) = 0.$$

Show that, if

$$E (t) = \int_ {0} ^ {1} \left(\frac {\partial u}{\partial t} (x, t)\right) ^ {2} + \left(\frac {\partial u}{\partial x} (x, t)\right) ^ {2} d x,$$

then $E$ is a constant.

Identify explicitly the major theorems that you use. (The author required four major theorems to do this exercise, but you may have done it another way or have a different view about what constitutes a major theorem.)


### app_k_149 [app_k_149]


K.149. [8.4, T] In Example 7.6 we constructed an infinitely differentiable function $E: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ with $E(t) = 0$ for $t \leq 0$ and $E(t) > 0$ for $t > 0$. If $\delta > 0$, sketch the function $H_{\delta}: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ given by

$$H _ {\delta} (t) = \int_ {0} ^ {t} E (\delta - x) E (x) d x.$$

By using functions constructed along these lines, or otherwise, prove the following mild improvement of Exercise 8.66.

If

$$I (f) = \int_ {0} ^ {1} \left(1 - \left(f ^ {\prime} (x)\right) ^ {4}\right) ^ {2} + f (x) ^ {2} d x$$

(as in Exercise 8.66), show that there exists a sequence of infinitely differentiable functions $f_{n}:[0,1]\to \mathbb{R}$ with $f_{n}(0) = f_{n}(1) = 0$ such that

$$I (f _ {n}) \rightarrow 0.$$


### app_k_150 [app_k_150]


K.150. [8.4, M] (This exercise should be treated as one in mathematical methods rather than in analysis.)

Show that the Euler-Lagrange equation for finding stationary values of an integral of the type

$$\int_ {a} ^ {b} g (x, y) d s,$$

where the integral is an arc length integral (informally, $ds$ is the element of arc length), may be written

$$g _ {,2} (x, y) - g _ {,1} (x, y) y ^ {\prime} (x) - \frac {y ^ {\prime \prime} (x) g (x , y)}{1 + y ^ {\prime} (x) ^ {2}} = 0.$$

Hence, or otherwise, show (under the assumption that the problem admits a well behaved solution) that the curve joining 2 given points (on one side of the $y$ axis) that minimises the surface area generated by rotating the curve about the $y$-axis is given by

$$x = c \cosh \frac {y + A}{C},$$

where $A$ and $C$ are constants.


### app_k_151 [app_k_151]


K.151. [8.4, M] (This exercise should be treated as one in mathematical methods rather than in analysis.)

We wish to find a well behaved function $y:[a,b]\to \mathbb{R}$ which makes the integral

$$I = \int_ {a} ^ {b} f (x, y, y ^ {\prime}, y ^ {\prime \prime}) d x$$

stationary subject to given values of $y$ and $y'$ at $a$ and $b$. Derive an analogue of the Euler-Lagrange equation for $y$ and solve the problem in the case where $a = 0$, $b = 1$, $y(0) = y'(0) = 0$, $y(1) = 0$, $y'(1) = 4$ and

$$f (x, y, y ^ {\prime}, y ^ {\prime \prime}) = \frac {1}{2} (y ^ {\prime \prime}) ^ {2} - 2 4 y.$$

Explain why your result cannot be a maximum.


### app_k_152 [app_k_152]


K.152. [8.5, T] Use the argument of Exercise K.137 (ii) to show that, if $\mathbf{f}:[a,b]\to \mathbb{R}^m$ is a continuous function and we write

$$\mathbf {S} _ {N} = \frac {b - a}{N} \sum_ {j = 0} ^ {N - 1} \mathbf {f} (a + j (b - a) / N),$$

then $\mathbf{S}_N$ tends to a limit as $N\to \infty$. We can define this limit to be $\int_{a}^{b}\mathbf{f}(t)dt$.

(The advantage of the procedure is that we define $\int_{a}^{b}\mathbf{f}(t)dt$ directly without using components. It is possible to define $\int_{a}^{b}\mathbf{f}(t)dt$ directly for all Riemann integrable $\mathbf{f}:[a,b]\to \mathbb{R}^m$ by mixing the ideas of this exercise with the ideas we used to define the one-dimensional Riemann integral, but it is not clear that the work involved is worth it.)


### app_k_153 [app_k_153]


K.153. [9.1, T] (i) Define $g_m : [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ by $g_m(x) = 1 - m\left|\frac{1}{2} - x\right|$ for $\left|\frac{1}{2} - x\right| \leq m^{-1}$, $g_m(x) = 0$ otherwise. Show that there exists a $g : [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$, which you should define explicitly, such that $g_m(x) \to g(x)$ as $m \to \infty$ for each $x \in [0,1]$.

(ii) If $f_{n}$ is as in Exercise 9.1, show that there exists a sequence of continuous functions $g_{nm} : [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $g_{nm}(x) \to f_n(x)$ as $m \to \infty$ for each $x \in [0,1]$.

(Thus repeated taking of limits may take us out of the class of Riemann integrable functions. In fact, the later and more difficult Exercise K.175 shows that it is possible to find a sequence of bounded continuous functions $h_n:[0,1]\to \mathbb{R}$ and a function $h:[0,1]\to \mathbb{R}$ which is not Riemann integrable such that $h_n(x)\rightarrow h(x)$ as $n\to \infty$ for each $x\in [0,1]$.)


### app_k_154 [app_k_154]


K.154. [9.2, P] Suppose that $f:[0,\infty) \to \mathbb{R}$ is a non-negative, non-increasing function. If $h > 0$, show that $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f(nh)$ converges if and only if $\int_0^\infty f(t)dt$ converges. If $\int_0^\infty f(t)dt$ converges, show that

$$h \sum_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} f (n h) \rightarrow \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} f (t) d t$$

as $h\to 0$ with $h > 0$.

Show that, given any integer $N \geq 1$ and any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a continuous function $g: [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $0 \leq g(x) \leq 1$ for all $x \in [0,1]$ and

$$\int_ {0} ^ {1} g (x) d x < \epsilon , \text{ but } \sum_ {n = 1} ^ {N} g (n N ^ {- 1}) = 1.$$

Show that there exists a continuous function $G:[0,\infty)\to \mathbb{R}$ such that $G(x)\geq 0$ for all $x\geq 0$ and $G(x)\rightarrow 0$ as $x\to \infty$ but

$$h \sum_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} G (n h) \not \to \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} G (t) d t$$

as $h\to 0$ with $h > 0$.


### app_k_155 [app_k_155]


K.155. [9.2, P] (i) Suppose that $g:(0,\infty)\to \mathbb{R}$ is everywhere differentiable with continuous derivative and $\int_1^\infty |g'(t)|dt$ converges. Show that $\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty}g(n)$ converges if and only if $\int_1^\infty g(t)dt$ converges.

(ii) Deduce Lemma 9.6 for the case when $f$ is differentiable.

(iii) Suppose $g$ satisfies the hypotheses of the first sentence of part (i). By giving a proof or a counterexample, establish whether, if the sum $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} g(n)$ is convergent, then it is automatically absolutely convergent.


### app_k_156 [app_k_156]


K.156. (A weak Stirling's formula.) [9.2, T] Show that

$$\int_ {n - 1 / 2} ^ {n + 1 / 2} \log x d x - \log n = \int_ {0} ^ {1 / 2} \left(\log \left(1 + \frac {t}{n}\right) + \log \left(1 - \frac {t}{n}\right)\right) d t.$$

By using the mean value theorem, or otherwise, deduce that

$$\left| \int_ {n - 1 / 2} ^ {n + 1 / 2} \log x d x - \log n \right| \leq \frac {4}{3 n ^ {2}}.$$

(You may replace $4 / (3n^2)$ by $A n^{-2}$ with $A$ another constant, if you wish.) Deduce that $\int_{1/2}^{N+1/2} \log x \, dx - \log N!$ converges to a limit. Conclude that there is a constant $C$ such that

$$\frac {n !}{(n + 1 / 2) ^ {(n + 1 / 2)}} e ^ {- n} \rightarrow C \text { as } n \rightarrow \infty .$$


### app_k_157 [app_k_157]


K.157. [9.2, P, ↑] By using Exercise K.156 and Abel's test, or otherwise, determine the values of $z \in \mathbb{C}$ for which $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \binom{2n}{n} z^n$ converges and prove your answer.

Answer the same question for $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n^{\alpha}\binom{2n}{n}z^{n}$, where $\alpha$ is real. (The answer will depend on the value of $\alpha$.)


### app_k_158 [app_k_158]


K.158. [9.2, T] (i) Show that the change of variable theorem works for infinite integrals of the form $\int_0^\infty f(x)dx$. More specifically, prove the following theorem.

Suppose that $f:[0,\infty) \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $g:[0,\infty) \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable with continuous derivative. Suppose, further, that $g(t) \geq 0$ for all $t$, and that $g(0) = 0$ and $g(t) \to \infty$ as $t \to \infty$. Then if either side of the following equation exists, both do and they are equal:

$$\int_ {0} ^ {\infty} f (s) d s = \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} f (g (x)) g ^ {\prime} (x) d x.$$

Explain why this result is consistent with Example 9.18.

(ii) Explain why the function $f:[0,\infty)\to \mathbb{R}$ defined by $f(x) = (\sin x) / x$ for $x\neq 0$, $f(0) = 1$ is continuous at 0. It is traditional to write $f(x) = (\sin x) / x$ and ignore the fact that, strictly speaking, $(\sin 0) / 0$ is meaningless. Sketch $f$.

If $I_{n} = \int_{0}^{n\pi}\frac{\sin x}{x} dx$, show, by using the alternating series test, that $I_{n}$ tends to a strictly positive limit $L$, say. Deduce carefully that $\int_0^\infty \frac{\sin x}{x} dx$ exists with value $L$.

(iii) Let $I(t) = \int_0^\infty \frac{\sin tx}{x} dx$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$. Show using (i), or otherwise, that $I(t) = L$ for all $t > 0$, $I(0) = 0$, and $I(t) = -L$ for $t < 0$.

(iv) Find a continuous function $g:[0,\pi ]\to \mathbb{R}$ such that $g(t)\geq 0$ for all $t\in [0,\pi ]$, $g(\pi /2) > 0$ and

$$\left| \frac {\sin x}{x} \right| \geq \frac {g (x - n \pi)}{n}$$

for all $n\pi \leq x \leq (n + 1)\pi$ and all integers $n \geq 1$. Hence, or otherwise, show that $\int_0^\infty |(\sin x) / x|dx$ fails to converge.

(v) For which values of real $\alpha$ does $\int_0^\infty \frac{\sin x}{x^\alpha} dx$ converge? Prove your answer.

[There are many methods for finding the constant $L$ of part (ii). The best known uses complex analysis. I give a rather crude but direct method in the next exercise. Hardy found the matter sufficiently interesting to justify writing two articles (pages 528-533 and 615-618 of [23]) comparing various methods.]


### app_k_159 [app_k_159]


K.159. [9.2, T, ↑] Here is a way of evaluating

$$\int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \frac {\sin x}{x} d x.$$

(We adopt the convention that, if we write $f(t) = \frac{\sin\lambda t}{t}$, then $f(0) = \lambda$.)

(i) Show, by using the formula for the sum of a geometric series, or otherwise, that

$$1 + 2 \sum_ {r = 1} ^ {n} \cos r x = \frac {\sin \left((n + \frac {1}{2}) x\right)}{\sin \frac {x}{2}}$$

for all $|x|\leq \pi$, and deduce that

$$2 \pi = \int_ {- \pi} ^ {\pi} \frac {\sin \left(\left(n + \frac {1}{2}\right) x\right)}{\sin \frac {x}{2}} d x.$$

(ii) If $\epsilon > 0$, show that

$$\int_ {- \epsilon} ^ {\epsilon} \frac {\sin \lambda x}{x} d x \rightarrow \int_ {- \infty} ^ {\infty} \frac {\sin x}{x} d x$$

as $\lambda \to \infty$.

(iii) If $\pi \geq \epsilon > 0$, show, by using the estimates from the alternating series test, or otherwise, that

$$\int_ {- \epsilon} ^ {\epsilon} \frac {\sin \left((n + \frac {1}{2}) x\right)}{\sin \frac {x}{2}} d x \rightarrow \int_ {- \pi} ^ {\pi} \frac {\sin \left((n + \frac {1}{2}) x\right)}{\sin \frac {x}{2}} d x = 2 \pi$$

as $n\to \infty$.

(iv) Show that

$$\left| \frac {2}{x} - \frac {1}{\sin \frac {1}{2} x} \right|\rightarrow 0$$

as $x\to 0$.

(v) Combine the results above to show that

$$\int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \frac {\sin x}{x} d x = \frac {\pi}{2}.$$


### app_k_160 [app_k_160]


K.160. (Big Oh and little oh.) [9.2, T] The following notations are much used in branches of mathematics like number theory, algorithmics and combinatorics. Consider functions $f, g: \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$.

We say $f(n) = O(g(n))$ as $n \to \infty$ if there exist a constant $A > 0$ and an integer $n_0$ such that $|f(n)| \leq A |g(n)|$ for $n \geq n_0$.

We say $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ as $n \to \infty$ if there exist a constant $A > 0$ and an integer $n_0$ such that $|f(n)| \geq A |g(n)|$ for $n \geq n_0$.

We say $f(n) = o(g(n))$ if, given any $\epsilon > 0$, we can find an integer $n_1(\epsilon)$ such that $|f(n)| \leq \epsilon |g(n)|$ for $n \geq n_1(\epsilon)$.

We say $f(n) \sim g(n)$ as $n \to \infty$ if $f(n) / g(n) \to 1$ as $n \to \infty$.

(i) If $f(n) = O(g(n))$, does it follow that $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$? Give a proof or counterexample. If $f(n) = O(g(n))$, must it necessarily be false that $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$? Give a proof or counterexample.

(ii) Formulate the 11 other possible questions along the lines of (i) and resolve them. (This is not as tedious as it might seem, since they all have two-sentence answers.)

(iii) In more traditional terms, what does it mean to say that $f(n) = o(1)$ as $n \to \infty$? What does it mean to say that $f(n) = O(1)$ as $n \to \infty$?

(iv) Give an example of a pair of functions $f, g: \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $f(n), g(n) > 0$ for all $n$ and neither of the two relations $f(n) = O(g(n))$, $g(n) = O(f(n))$ holds.

(v) Consider $f, g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$. Give definitions along the lines given above for the statement '$f(x) = O(g(x))$ as $x \to \infty$' and for the statement '$f(x) = o(g(x))$ as $x \to 0'$.

(vi) Recall or do exercise K.94, and write the result in the notation of this exercise.

Although the notations introduced in this exercise are very useful for stating results, they are sharp-edged tools which can do as much harm as good in the hands of the inexperienced. I strongly recommend translating any statements involving such notations back into classical form before trying to work with them.


### app_k_161 [app_k_161]


K.161. [9.2, P, ↑] Are the following statements true or false? Give reasons.

(i) If $f_j, g_j : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ are positive functions such that $f_1(n) = O(g_1(n))$ and $f_2(n) = O(g_2(n))$, then $f_1(n) + f_2(n) = O(g_1(n) + g_2(n))$.

(ii) If $f_j, g_j : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ are functions such that $f_1(n) = O(g_1(n))$ and $f_2(n) = O(g_2(n))$, then $f_1(n) + f_2(n) = O(g_1(n) + g_2(n))$.

(iii) If $f_j, g_j : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ are positive functions such that $f_1(n) = O(g_1(n))$ and $f_2(n) = O(g_2(n))$, then $f_1(n) + f_2(n) = O\big(\max(g_1(n), g_2(n))\big)$.

(iv) $n! = O(2^{n^2})$ as $n\to \infty$.

(v) $\cos x - 1 + \frac{(\sin x)^2}{2!} = O(x^4)$ as $x\to 0$.

(vi) $\cos x - 1 + \frac{(\sin x)^2}{2!} -\frac{(\sin x)^4}{4!} = O(x^6)$ as $x\to 0$.


### app_k_162 [app_k_162]


K.162. [9.2, P, ↑] (i) If $\alpha > -1$, show that

$$\sum_ {r = 1} ^ {n} r ^ {\alpha} \sim \frac {n ^ {\alpha + 1}}{\alpha + 1}.$$

(ii) State and prove a result similar to (i) for $\alpha = -1$.

(iii) If $\alpha < -1$, show that

$$\sum_ {r = n} ^ {\infty} r ^ {\alpha} \sim \frac {n ^ {\alpha + 1}}{(- \alpha) - 1}.$$

(iv) Does there exist a modification of (iii) similar to (ii) for $\alpha = -1$? Give brief reasons.


### app_k_163 [app_k_163]


K.163. [9.2, P, ↑] Suppose that $g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is twice differentiable and

$$g ^ {\prime \prime} (x) = O \big (x ^ {- \lambda} \big) \text { as } x \to \infty$$

for some $\lambda > 0$. Prove that

$$\int_ {n} ^ {n + 1} \left(g (x) - g (n + \frac {1}{2})\right) d x = O (n ^ {- \lambda})$$

as $n\to \infty$. Deduce that, if $\lambda >1$ and $|\int_1^X g(x)dx|\to \infty$ as $n\to \infty$, then

$$\sum_ {r = 1} ^ {n} g (r + \frac {1}{2}) \sim \int_ {1} ^ {n + 1} g (x) d x.$$

Deduce the result of Exercise K.156 as a special case.


### app_k_164 [app_k_164]


K.164. [9.2, P] Show that $\int_0^{\pi /2}\log (\sin x)dx$ (defined to be the limit of $\int_{\epsilon}^{\pi /2}\log (\sin x)dx$ as $\epsilon \to 0$ through positive values of $\epsilon$) exists. By considering $\int_0^{\pi /2}\log (\sin 2x)dx$ and exploiting various symmetries, show that

$$\int_ {0} ^ {\pi / 2} \log (\sin x) d x = - \frac {\pi}{2} \log 2.$$


### app_k_165 [app_k_165]


K.165. [9.2, P] (i) Suppose that $f:(0,a]\to \mathbb{R}$ is a positive, monotonic decreasing, continuous function. Show that $\int_0^a f(x)dx$ (defined as the limit of $\int_{\epsilon}^{a}f(x)dx$ as $\epsilon \rightarrow 0$ through positive values of $\epsilon$) exists if and only if $(a / N)\sum_{r = 1}^{N}f(ra / N)$ tends to a limit. If these limits exist, show that

$$\frac {a}{N} \sum_ {1} ^ {N} f \left(\frac {r a}{N}\right)\rightarrow \int_ {0} ^ {a} f (x) d x \text { as } N \rightarrow \infty .$$

(ii) Show that the result of part (i) is false if we omit the condition that $f$ is monotonic decreasing.

(iii) Let $\omega = 2\pi / N$. By multiplying both sides of the equation by $z - 1$, or otherwise, show that

$$\sum_ {r = 0} ^ {N - 1} z ^ {r} = \prod_ {r = 1} ^ {N - 1} \left(z - \omega^ {r}\right)$$

for all $z \neq 1$. Explain why this continues to hold when $z = 1$, and deduce that

$$\prod_ {r = 1} ^ {N - 1} \sin \frac {r \pi}{N} = \frac {N}{2 ^ {N - 1}}.$$

(iv) Use the result of (i) to show that $\int_0^{\pi /2}\log (\sin x)dx$ exists and has value $-\frac{\pi}{2}\log 2$.


### app_k_166 [app_k_166]


K.166. [9.2, P] The function $f:[0,1]\to \mathbb{R}\cup \{\infty \}$ is defined as follows. Each $0 < x < 1$ is expressed as a decimal $x = .a_{1}a_{2}\dots a_{k}\dots$ with $a_{j}$ an integer $0\leq a_{j}\leq 9$. In ambiguous cases we use the non-terminating form. We put $f(x) = k$ if the $k$ th digit $a_{k}$ is the first digit 7 to occur in the expansion, if there is any digit 7. If there is none (and also at $x = 0$ and $x = 1$) we put $f(x) = \infty$. Prove that the function $f_{X}:[0,1]\to \mathbb{R}$ defined by $f_{X}(x) = \min (X,f(x))$ is integrable for all $X\geq 0$ and that

$$\int_ {0} ^ {1} f _ {X} (x) d x \rightarrow 1 0$$

as $X\to \infty$.

Does it make any difference if we redefine $f$ so at those points $x$ where we previously had $f(x) = \infty$ we now have $f(x) = 0$?


### app_k_167 [app_k_167]


K.167. [9.2, H] This exercise investigates another way of defining improper integrals.

(i) If $f:[a,b]\to \mathbb{R}$ and $R,S\geq 0$, we write

$$f _ {R, S} (x) = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} R & \text {if } f (x) \geq R, \\ f (x) & \text {if } R > f (x) > - S, \\ - S & \text {if } - S \geq f (x). \end{array} \right.$$

If $f|_{R,S} \in \mathcal{R}[a,b]$ for all $R, S \geq 0$ and there exists an $L$ such that, given any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists an $R_0 > 0$ with

$$\left| \int_ {a} ^ {b} f _ {R, S} (x) d x - L \right| < \epsilon$$

for all $R, S \geq R_0$, then we say that $\mathcal{LV}\int_{a}^{b}f(x)dx$ exists with value $L$.

Show that, if $f|_{R,S} \in \mathcal{R}[a,b]$ for all $R, S \geq 0$, then $\mathcal{LV}\int_{a}^{\infty}f(x)dx$ exists if and only if $\mathcal{LV}\int_{a}^{\infty}|f(x)|dx$ exists.

(ii) Suppose that $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous except at $b$ and $f(x) \to \infty$ as $x \to b$. Show that the following two statements are equivalent.

(A) There exists an $L$ such that, given any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists an $R_0 > 0$ with

$$\left| \int_ {a} ^ {b} f _ {R, S} (x) d x - L \right| < \epsilon$$

for all $R, S > R_0$.

(B) There exists an $L'$ such that, given any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a $\delta_0 > 0$ with

$$\left| \int_ {a} ^ {b - \eta} f (x) d x - L ^ {\prime} \right| < \epsilon$$

for all $0 < \eta < \delta_0$.

Show further that, if (A) and (B) hold, then $L = L^{\prime}$.

(iii) Suppose that $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous except at possibly at $b$. Show that statement (A) implies statement (B) but that the converse is false. (Think about Exercise K.158 and change of variable if you need a hint.)

[Warning: If you ever need to take the contents of this question seriously, you should switch to the Lebesgue integral.]


### app_k_168 [app_k_168]


K.168. [9.3, P, S] By considering both orders of integration in

$$\int_ {0} ^ {X} \int_ {a} ^ {b} e ^ {- t x} d t d x,$$

where $b > a > 0$ and $X > 0$, show that

$$\int_ {0} ^ {X} \frac {e ^ {- a x} - e ^ {- b x}}{x} d x = \int_ {a} ^ {b} \frac {1 - e ^ {- t X}}{t} d t.$$

Show that

$$\int_ {a} ^ {b} \frac {e ^ {- t X}}{t} d t \leq e ^ {- a X} \log \left(\frac {b}{a}\right),$$

and hence find

$$\int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \frac {e ^ {- a x} - e ^ {- b x}}{x} d x.$$


### app_k_169 [app_k_169]


K.169. [9.3, T] Suppose that $f: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous. By using results on the differentiation of integrals, which should be quoted exactly, show that

$$\frac {d}{d t} \int_ {a} ^ {t} \left(\int_ {c} ^ {d} f (u, v) d v\right) d u = \frac {d}{d t} \int_ {c} ^ {d} \left(\int_ {a} ^ {t} f (u, v) d u\right) d v.$$

Deduce that

$$\int_ {a} ^ {b} \left(\int_ {c} ^ {d} f (u, v) d v\right) d u = \int_ {c} ^ {d} \left(\int_ {a} ^ {b} f (u, v) d u\right) d v.$$

In what way is this result weaker than that of Theorem 9.20?


### app_k_170 [app_k_170]


K.170. [9.3, H] (i) Reread Exercise 5.32.

(ii) Let us define $f:[0,1]\times [0,1]\to \mathbb{R}$ by

$$\begin{array}{l} f (x, y) = 2 ^ {2 n} \quad \text {if } 2 ^ {- n} < x < 2 ^ {- n + 1}, 2 ^ {- n} < y < 2 ^ {- n + 1} \text { and } n \geq 1, n \in \mathbb {Z}, \\ f (x, y) = - 2 ^ {2 n + 1} \text { if } 2 ^ {- n} < x < 2 ^ {- n + 1}, 2 ^ {- n - 1} < y < 2 ^ {- n} \text { and } n \geq 1, n \in \mathbb {Z}, \\ f (x, y) = 0 \quad \text {otherwise}. \\ \end{array}$$

Sketch the sets where $f(x,y)$ takes various values. Show that the integral $\int_0^1 f(x,y)dx$ is well defined and calculate it for all values of $y$. Show that the integral $\int_0^1 f(x,y)dy$ is well defined, and calculate it for all values of $x$. Show that the two integrals

$$\int_ {0} ^ {1} \int_ {0} ^ {1} f (x, y) d x d y \text { and } \int_ {0} ^ {1} \int_ {0} ^ {1} f (x, y) d y d x$$

are well defined, and calculate them. Show that the two integrals are not equal.

(iii) Suppose that $u: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous function with $u(t) = 0$ for $t < 1/4$ and $t > 3/4$, $u(t) \geq 0$ for all $t$ and $\int_0^1 u(t) dt = 1$. Show that the function $g: [0,1]^2 \to \mathbb{R}$

$$g (x, y) = \sum_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} \left(2 ^ {2 n} u (2 ^ {n} x - 1) u (2 ^ {n} y - 1) - 2 ^ {2 n + 1} u (2 ^ {n} x - 1) u (2 ^ {n + 1} y - 1)\right)$$

is well defined. Show that the integral $\int_0^1 f(x,y)dx$ is well defined, and calculate it for all values of $y$. Show that the integral $\int_0^1 f(x,y)dy$ is well defined, and calculate it for all values of $x$. Show that the two integrals

$$\int_ {0} ^ {1} \int_ {0} ^ {1} g (x, y) d x d y \text { and } \int_ {0} ^ {1} \int_ {0} ^ {1} g (x, y) d y d x$$

are well defined but not equal. Show that $g$ is continuous except at $(0,0)$, that the function $x \mapsto g(x,y)$ is an everywhere continuous function for all $y$, and that the function $y \mapsto g(x,y)$ is an everywhere continuous function for all $x$. Why does Theorem 9.20 fail?

(iv) The traditional counterexample is the following. Define the function $h:[0,1]^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ by $h(0,0) = 0$ and

$$h (x, y) = \frac {x y (x ^ {2} - y ^ {2})}{(x ^ {2} + y ^ {2}) ^ {3}}$$

otherwise. Show that the integral $\int_0^1 h(x,y)dx$ is well defined, and calculate it for all values of $y$ (you may find the substitution $w = x^{2} + y^{2}$ useful). Show that the integral $\int_0^1 f(x,y)dy$ is well defined, and calculate it for all values of $x$. Show that the two integrals

$$\int_ {0} ^ {1} \int_ {0} ^ {1} h (x, y) d x d y \text { and } \int_ {0} ^ {1} \int_ {0} ^ {1} h (x, y) d y d x$$

are well defined but not equal. Show that $g$ is continuous except at $(0,0)$, that the function $x \mapsto g(x,y)$ is an everywhere continuous function for all $y$, and that the function $y \mapsto g(x,y)$ is an everywhere continuous function for all $x$.

(v) Which of examples (ii) and (iv) do you consider 'more natural'. Which do you feel you 'understand better'? Which is 'better'. Why? (Of course you can refuse to answer questions like this on the grounds that they are not mathematical, but I think you will lose something.)


### app_k_171 [app_k_171]


K.171. (Interchange of infinite integrals.) [9.3, T] (i) Suppose that $g:[0,\infty)\to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $|g(x)|\leq A(1 + x^2)^{-1}$ for all $x$. Show that $\int_0^\infty g(x)dx$ exists, that

$$\left| \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} g (x) d x \right| \leq 2 A,$$

and

$$\left| \int_ {0} ^ {R} g (x) d x - \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} g (x) d x \right| \leq A R ^ {- 1},$$

for $R \geq 1$. [If you do not wish to use knowledge of $\tan^{-1}$, simply observe that $(1 + x^2)^{-1} \leq 1$ for $0 \leq x \leq 1$ and $(1 + x^2)^{-1} \leq x^{-2}$ for $x \geq 1$.]

(ii) Suppose that $f:[0,\infty)^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and satisfies the condition $|f(x,y)| \leq A(1 + x^2)^{-1}(1 + y^2)^{-1}$ for all $(x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$. Show that $\int_0^\infty \int_0^\infty f(x,y) \, dx \, dy$ and $\int_0^\infty \int_0^\infty f(x,y) \, dy \, dx$ exist and that

$$\int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} f (x, y) d x d y = \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} f (x, y) d y d x.$$

(iii) (Parts (iii) and (iv) run along similar lines to parts (i) and (ii) of Exercise K.170. You do not need to have done Exercise K.170 to do this exercise, but if you have, it is worth noting the similarities and the differences.) Reread Exercise 5.32. Let $b_{rs}$ be as in part (ii) of that exercise. Define $F:[0,\infty)^{2}\to \mathbb{R}$ by

$$F (x, y) = b _ {r s} \text { for } r - 1 \leq x < r \text { and } s - 1 \leq y < s.$$

Show that $\int_0^\infty \int_0^\infty F(x,y)dxdy$ and $\int_0^\infty \int_0^\infty F(x,y)dydx$ exist and that

$$\int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} F (x, y) d x d y \neq \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} F (x, y) d y d x.$$

(iv) By modifying the construction in part (iii), or otherwise, find a continuous function $G:[0,\infty)^2\to \mathbb{R}$ such that $\sup_{x^2 +y^2\geq R}|G(x,y)|\to 0$ as $R\to \infty$ but

$$\int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} G (x, y) d x d y \neq \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} G (x, y) d y d x.$$


### app_k_172 [app_k_172]


K.172. [9.3, H, S] It is a weakness of the Riemann integral that there is no Fubini theorem for general Riemann integrable functions.

Let $A = [0,1] \times [0,1]$ and define $f: A \to \mathbb{R}$ by $f(x,0) = 1$ if $x$ is rational, $f(x,y) = 0$ otherwise. By finding appropriate dissections, show that $f$ is Riemann integrable. Explain why $F_{2}(y) = \int_{0}^{1} f(t,y) dt$ is not defined for all $y$.

[See also the next exercise.]


### app_k_173 [app_k_173]


K.173. [9.3, H] We say that a sequence $\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2, \ldots$ of points in $A = [0,1] \times [0,1]$ is dense in $A$ if, given $\mathbf{x} \in A$, we can find a $j \geq 1$ with $\|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{e}_j\| < \epsilon$.

(i) By considering $\mathbb{Q} \times \mathbb{Q}$, or by direct construction, or otherwise, show that we can find a sequence $\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2, \ldots$ of points which is dense in $A$.

(ii) By induction, or otherwise, show that we can find a sequence $\mathbf{w}_1 = (u_1,v_1)$, $\mathbf{w}_2 = (u_2,v_2)$, \ldots of points in $A$ such that $\| \mathbf{w}_k - \mathbf{e}_k\| < 1 / k$ and $u_i\neq u_j$, $v_{i}\neq v_{j}$ whenever $i\neq j$. Show that the sequence $\mathbf{w}_1$, $\mathbf{w}_2$, \ldots is dense in $A$.

(iii) Define $f: A \to \mathbb{R}$ by $f(\mathbf{x}) = 1$ if $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{w}_j$ for some $j$, $f(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ otherwise. Show that, if $x$ is fixed, $f(x,y) \neq 1$ for at most one value of $y$. Conclude that $F_1(x) = \int_0^1 f(x,s)ds$ exists and takes the value 0 for all $y$. Thus $\int_0^1\left(\int_0^1f(x,y)dy\right)dx$ exists. Similarly, $\int_0^1\left(\int_0^1f(x,y)dx\right)dy$ exists. Show, however, that $f$ is not Riemann integrable.


### app_k_174 [app_k_174]


K.174. [9.3, H] The object of this exercise is to construct a bounded open set in $\mathbb{R}$ which does not have Riemann length. Our construction will take place within the closed interval $[-2, 2]$, and all references to dissections and upper sums and so forth will refer to this interval.

(i) Enumerate the rationals in $[0,1]$ as a sequence $y_{1},y_{2},\ldots$. Let $U_{j} = \bigcup_{k = 1}^{j}(y_{k} - 2^{-k - 4},y_{k} + 2^{-k - 4})$ and $U = \bigcup_{k = 1}^{\infty}(y_k - 2^{-k - 4},y_k + 2^{-k - 4})$. Explain why $U$ is open and $I^{*}(\mathbb{I}_{U})\geq 1$.

(ii) We wish to show that $I_{*}(\mathbb{I}_{U}) < 1$, so that $\mathbb{I}_U$ is not Riemann integrable and $U$ has no Riemann length. To this end, suppose, if possible, that $I_{*}(\mathbb{I}_{U}) \geq 1$. Explain why this means that we can find disjoint closed intervals $I_r = [a_r, b_r]$ $[1 \leq r \leq N]$ lying inside $[-2, 2]$ such that

$$\sum_ {r = 1} ^ {N} \left(b _ {r} - a _ {r}\right) \geq 15 / 16 \text { yet } \bigcup_ {r = 1} ^ {N} \left[ a _ {r}, b _ {r} \right] \subset U.$$

(iii) Let $K_{j} = ([-2,2]\setminus U_{j})\cap \bigcup_{r = 1}^{N}[a_{r},b_{r}]$. Explain why $K_{j}$ is a closed bounded set. By considering the total length of the intervals making up $U_{j}$, show that $K_{j}\neq \emptyset$. Use Exercise 4.48 to show that

$$\left([-2, 2] \setminus U\right) \cap \bigcup_ {r = 1} ^ {N} \left[ a _ {r}, b _ {r} \right] \neq \varnothing .$$

Deduce, by reductio ad absurdum, that $I_{*}(\mathbb{I}_{U}) < 1$ and $U$ has no Riemann length.

(iv) Show that there is a closed bounded set in $\mathbb{R}$ which does not have Riemann length. (This is a one-line argument.)

(v) Show that there are bounded closed sets and bounded open sets in $\mathbb{R}^2$ which do not have Riemann area.

[The first example of this type was found by Henry Smith. Smith was a major pure mathematician at a time and place (19th century Oxford) not particularly propitious for such a talent. He seems to have been valued more as a good College and University man than for anything else.]


### app_k_175 [app_k_175]


K.175. [9.3, H, ↑] (This continues with the ideas of Exercise K.174 above.)

(i) If $(a,b)\subseteq [-2,2]$, find a sequence of continuous functions $f_{n} : [-2,2]\to \mathbb{R}$ such that $0\leq f_n(x)\leq \mathbb{I}_{(a,b)}(x)$ for all $n$ and $f_{n}(x)\rightarrow \mathbb{I}_{(a,b)}(x)$ as $n\to \infty$ for all $x\in [a,b]$.

(ii) If $U_{j}$ and $U$ are as in Exercise K.174, find a sequence of continuous functions $f_{n}:[-2,2]\to \mathbb{R}$ such that $0\leq f_n(x)\leq \mathbb{I}_{U_n}(x)$ for all $n$ and $f_{n}(x)\rightarrow \mathbb{I}_{U}(x)$ as $n\to \infty$ for all $x\in [a,b]$.

(iii) Show that it is possible to find a sequence of bounded continuous functions $h_n: [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ and a function $h: [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ which is not Riemann integrable such that $h_n(x) \to h(x)$ as $n \to \infty$, for each $x \in [0,1]$.


### app_k_176 [app_k_176]


K.176. [9.4, T] Let $f:[a,b]\to \mathbb{R}$ be an increasing function.

(i) If $t_j \in [a, b]$ and $t_1 \leq t_2 \leq t_3 \leq \ldots$, explain why $f(t_j)$ tends to a limit.

(ii) Suppose $x\in [a,b]$. If $t_j , s_j\in [a,b]$,

$$t _ {1} \leq t _ {2} \leq t _ {3} \leq \dots , \text { and } s _ {1} \leq s _ {2} \leq s _ {3} \leq \dots ,$$

$t_j \to x$, $s_j \to x$ and $t_j, s_j < x$ for all $j$, show that $\lim_{j \to \infty} f(t_j) = \lim_{j \to \infty} f(s_j)$. Show that the condition $t_j, s_j < x$ for all $j$ cannot be omitted.

(iii) Show that, if $x \in (a,b]$ and $t \to x$ through values of $t$ with $a \leq t < x$, then $f(t)$ tends to a 'left limit' $f(x-)$.

(iv) State and prove the appropriate result on 'right limits'.

(v) If we write $J(x) = f(x+) - f(x-)$ for the 'jump' at $x$, show that $J(x) \geq 0$. Show that, if $a < x_1 < x_2 < \dots < x_{N-1} < x_N < b$, then

$$\sum_ {r = 1} ^ {N} J (x _ {r}) \leq f (b) - f (a).$$

(vi) Let us write $E_{k} = \{x\in (a,b):J(x)\geq 1 / k\}$. Use (v) to show that $E_{k}$ is finite. Deduce that $E = \bigcup_{k = 1}^{\infty}E_{k}$ is countable, and conclude that an increasing function is continuous except at a countable set of points.

(vii) If we define $\tilde{f}(x) = f(x+)$ when $x \in E \cup \{a\}$, $\tilde{f}(x) = f(x)$ otherwise, show that $\tilde{f} : [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is a right continuous increasing function. If we define $\bar{f}(x) = f(x+)$ for all $x \in [a, b)$, $\bar{f}(b) = f(b)$, state and prove the relation between $\tilde{f}$ and $\bar{f}$.

(viii) If $g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is an increasing function, show that $g$ is continuous except at a countable set of points $E$, say. If we define $\tilde{g}(x) = f(x+)$ when $x \in E$, $\tilde{g}(x) = g(x)$ otherwise, show that $\tilde{g}: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a right continuous increasing function.

(ix) Not all discontinuities are as well behaved as those of increasing functions. If $g:[-1,1]\to \mathbb{R}$ is defined by $g(x) = \sin (1 / x)$ for $x\neq 0$, show that there is no choice of value for $g(0)$ which will make $g$ left or right continuous at 0.


### app_k_177 [app_k_177]


K.177. [9.4, P] Define $H: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ by $H(t) = 0$ if $t < 0$, $H(t) = 1$ if $t \geq 0$.

(i) If $f(t) = \sin(1/t)$ for $t > 0$, and $f(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, show that, whatever value we assign to $f(0)$, the function $f$ is not Riemann-Stieltjes integrable with respect to $H$.

(ii) If $f(t) = \sin(1/t)$ for $t < 0$, and $f(t) = 0$ for $t > 0$, show that $f$ is Riemann-Stieltjes integrable with respect to $H$ if and only if $f(0) = 0$.

(iii) By reflecting on parts (i) and (ii), or otherwise, formulate and prove a necessary and sufficient condition for a bounded function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ to be Riemann-Stieltjes integrable with respect to $H$.


### app_k_178 [app_k_178]


K.178. [9.4, P, ↑] (i) Show that, if $u: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is bounded, increasing and right continuous, then we can write

$$u (x) = U (x) + \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {\infty} \lambda_ {j} H (x - x _ {j}),$$

where $U: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is bounded, increasing and continuous, $H$ is the right continuous version of the Heaviside function, $\lambda_j \geq 0$ for all $j$ and $\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} \lambda_j$ converges.

(ii) Show that, if $G: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is bounded, increasing and right continuous and $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is bounded, increasing and left continuous, then $f$ is Riemann-Stieltjes integrable with respect to $G$.

(iii) Suppose $G: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is bounded, increasing and right continuous and $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is bounded, increasing and right continuous. Find necessary and sufficient conditions for $f$ to be Riemann-Stieltjes integrable with respect to $G$, and prove that your statement is correct.


### app_k_179 [app_k_179]


K.179. [9.4, P, ↑] (i) Let $H$ be the Heaviside function discussed in the previous exercises. Give an example of a continuous function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $f(t) \geq 0$ for all $t$ and $f$ is not identically zero but

$$\int_ {\mathbb {R}} f (x) d H (x) = 0.$$

(ii) For the remainder of the exercise $G: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ will always be a bounded, increasing and right continuous function. By reflecting on part (i) and the proof of Exercise 8.34, formulate a necessary and sufficient condition on $G$ so that the following theorem holds.

If $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and bounded, $f(t) \geq 0$ for all $t$ and

$$\int_ {\mathbb {R}} f (x) d G (x) = 0,$$

then $f(t) = 0$ for all $t$.

Prove that your condition is, indeed, necessary and sufficient.

(iii) By reflecting on part (ii), formulate a necessary and sufficient condition on $G$ so that the following theorem holds.

If $f:\mathbb{R}\to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and bounded and

$$\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x) g(x) dG(x) = 0,$$

whenever $g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and bounded, it follows that $f(t) = 0$ for all $t$.

Prove that your condition is, indeed, necessary and sufficient.


### app_k_180 [app_k_180]


K.180. (Bounded variation.) [9.4, T] We say that a function $F: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is of bounded variation if there exists a constant $K$ such that, whenever $a = x_0 \leq x_1 \leq \dots \leq x_n = b$, we have

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} |f(x_j) - f(x_{j-1})| \leq K.$$

(i) Show that any bounded increasing function $G:[a,b]\to \mathbb{R}$ is of bounded variation.

(ii) Show that the sum of two functions of bounded variation is of bounded variation. Deduce, in particular, that the difference $F - G$ of two increasing functions $F, G : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is of bounded variation.

(iii) Show that any function of bounded variation is bounded.

(iv) Let $f:[-1,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ be defined by $f(x) = x\sin(1/x)$ for $x \neq 0$, $f(0) = 0$. Show that $f$ is continuous everywhere on $[-1,1]$, but not of bounded variation. [For variations on this theme, consult Exercise K.183.]


### app_k_181 [app_k_181]


K.181. [9.4, T, ↑] (This continues the previous exercise.) In this exercise it will be useful to remember that, to prove $A \leq B$, it is sufficient to prove that $A \leq B + \epsilon$ for all $\epsilon > 0$. Suppose $F: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is of bounded variation.

(i) Explain why we can define $V_F : [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ by taking $V_F(t)$ to be the supremum of all sums

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} |F(x_j) - F(x_{j-1})|,$$

where $a = x_0 \leq x_1 \leq \dots \leq x_n = t$ and $n \geq 1$.

Explain why we can define $F_{+}:[a,b]\to \mathbb{R}$ by taking $F_{+}(t)$ to be the supremum of all sums

$$\sum_{j=0}^{n} (F(y_j) - F(x_j)),$$

where $a = x_0 \leq y_0 \leq x_1 \leq y_1 \leq x_2 \leq y_2 \leq \dots \leq x_n \leq y_n = t$ and $n \geq 0$.

We define $F_{-}:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ by taking $F_{-}(t)$ to be the supremum of all sums

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} (F(y_{j-1}) - F(x_j)),$$

where $a = x_0 \leq y_0 \leq x_1 \leq y_1 \leq x_2 \leq y_2 \leq \dots \leq x_n \leq y_n = t$ and $n \geq 1$.

(ii) Show that $F_{+}$ and $F_{-}$ are increasing functions and

$$V_F = F_+ + F_-, \quad F = F(a) + F_+ - F_-.$$

In particular, we have shown that every function of bounded variation on $[a,b]$ is the difference of two increasing functions. We call the function $V_{F}(t)$ the total variation of $F$ on $[a,t]$.

(iii) Suppose that we have two increasing functions $G_{+}, G_{-} : [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $G_{+}(a) = G_{-}(a) = 0$ and $F = F(a) + G_{+} - G_{-}$. Show that $G_{+}(t) \geq F_{+}(t)$ and $G_{-}(t) \geq F_{-}(t)$ for all $t \in [a, b]$.

Show, more precisely, that $G_{+} - F_{+}$ and $G_{-} - F_{-}$ are increasing functions.


### app_k_182 [app_k_182]


K.182. [9.4, T, ↑] We use the notation and hypotheses of the previous exercise. We need the results of Exercise K.176, which the reader should either do or reread before continuing.

(i) By Exercise K.176 there is a countable (possibly finite or empty) set $E_{+}$ such that

$$\lim_{t \to e, t > e} F_{+}(t) - \lim_{t \to e, t < e} F_{+}(t) > 0$$

for $e \in E_{+}$, and $f$ is continuous at each $x \notin E_{+}$. The same result holds with $F_{+}$ and $E_{+}$ replaced by $F_{-}$ and $E_{-}$. By using Exercise K.181(iii), or otherwise, show that $E_{+} \cap E_{-} = \emptyset$.

(ii) Use part (i) to show that $F_{+}$ and $F_{-}$ are right continuous if $F$ is. Show that $F_{+}$ and $F_{-}$ are continuous if $F$ is.

(iii) Suppose that $F$ is continuously differentiable. Show that

$$F_+(t) = \int_a^t \max(F'(x), 0) \, dx, \quad F_-(t) = -\int_a^t \min(F'(x), 0) \, dx.$$

[In setting out your proof, remember that a continuous function may change sign infinitely often in an interval.]

Conclude that $F_{+}$ and $F_{-}$ are continuously differentiable if $F$ is.


### app_k_183 [app_k_183]


K.183. [9.4, P, ↑] (This exercise is not required for later parts of the sequence.) If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are real, let $f_{\alpha\beta}:[-1,1]\to \mathbb{R}$ be defined by $f_{\alpha\beta}(x) = |x|^{\alpha}\sin(|x|^{\beta})$ for $x\neq 0$, $f_{\alpha\beta}(0) = 0$.

(i) For which values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ is $f_{\alpha\beta}$ of bounded variation?

(ii) For which values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ is $f_{\alpha\beta}$ everywhere continuous?

(iii) For which values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ is $f_{\alpha\beta}$ everywhere differentiable?

(iv) For which values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ is $f_{\alpha\beta}$ everywhere differentiable with continuous derivative?


### app_k_184 [app_k_184]


K.184. [9.4, T, ↑] (This exercise closely echoes Exercise K.181.) We say that a function $G: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is of bounded variation if there exists a constant $K$ such that, whenever $x_0 < x_1 < \dots < x_n$, we have

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} |G(x_j) - G(x_{j-1})| \leq K.$$

(i) Show that a function $G: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is of bounded variation if and only if it is the difference of two bounded increasing functions.

(ii) Consider a function $G: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$. Show that its restriction $G|_{[a,b]}$ is of bounded variation for every closed interval $[a,b]$ if and only if it is the difference of two increasing functions.

(iii) Show that, if a function $G: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is of bounded variation with $G(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$, then there exist unique bounded increasing functions $G_{+}, G_{-}: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $G_{+}(t), G_{-}(t) \to 0$ as $t \to -\infty$ and $G = G_{+} - G_{-}$ with the property that, if $F_{+}, F_{-}: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ are increasing functions with $G = F_{+} - F_{-}$, then $F_{+} - G_{+}$ and $F_{-} - G_{-}$ are increasing.

(iv) Suppose that $G$, $G_{+}$, $G_{-}$ are as in (iii). Show that $G_{+}$ and $G_{-}$ are right continuous if $G$ is. Show that $G_{+}$ and $G_{-}$ are continuous if $G$ is. Show that $G_{+}$ and $G_{-}$ are continuously differentiable if $G$ is.

(v) We write $G_V = G_+ + G_-$. Identify $G_V$ as the supremum of a certain set of sums, and prove that your identification is correct.


### app_k_185 [app_k_185]


K.185. [9.4, T, ↑] (i) Suppose that $F, G: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ are bounded increasing right continuous functions. Show that a bounded function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is Riemann-Stieltjes integrable with respect to both $F$ and $G$ if and only if it is Riemann-Stieltjes integrable with respect to $F + G$. If $f$ is Riemann-Stieltjes integrable with respect to both $F$ and $G$, show that

$$\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x) \, d(F + G)(x) = \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x) \, dF(x) + \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x) \, dG(x).$$

(ii) Let $F_{1}, F_{2}, G_{1}, G_{2}: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be bounded increasing right continuous functions with $F_{1} - G_{1} = F_{2} - G_{2}$. If a bounded function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is Riemann-Stieltjes integrable with respect to $F_{1}, F_{2}, G_{1}$ and $G_{2}$, show that

$$\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x) \, dF_1(x) - \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x) \, dG_1(x) = \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x) \, dF_2(x) - \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x) \, dG_2(x).$$


### app_k_186 [app_k_186]


K.186. [9.4, T, ↑] We use the notation and results of Exercise K.184. Although we do not use the results of Exercise K.185 directly, they show that the path chosen is a reasonable one.

If $G: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is right continuous of bounded variation, then we know that $G_{+}$ and $G_{-}$ are increasing bounded right continuous functions. We say that a bounded function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is Riemann-Stieltjes integrable with respect to $G$ if it is Riemann-Stieltjes integrable with respect to both $G_{+}$ and $G_{-}$. We write

$$\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x) \, dG(x) = \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x) \, dG_+(x) - \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x) \, dG_-(x).$$

Develop the theory of this extended integral along the lines of Section 9.4, starting at Exercise 9.38(ii) and ending at Exercise 9.44. Note that, though this is easy, you must be careful to make the right adjustments. Thus, for example, the conclusion of the result corresponding to Exercise 9.38(iii) is

$$\left|\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x) \, dG(x)\right| \leq K \lim_{t \to \infty} G_V(t),$$

and, in Exercise 9.44(ii), we can choose $\lambda_{j}$ positive or negative.


### app_k_187 [app_k_187]


K.187. (The trigonometric functions via arc length.) [9.5, T] In Section 5.5 we developed the theory of the trigonometric functions via their differential equations. In that development, the trigonometric functions come first and angle is defined in terms of those functions (see Exercise 5.56). Here we reverse the process.

(i) Consider the map $\gamma : [-1, 1] \to \mathbb{R}^2$ given by

$$\gamma(y) = ((1 - y^2)^{1/2}, y),$$

where we take the positive square root. Convince yourself that this represents an arc of the unit circle. If $1 > y \geq 0$, we define the angle $\theta(y)$ subtended at the origin by $\gamma(0)$ and $\gamma(y)$ to be the length of the curve $\gamma$ between those two points. If $0 \geq y > -1$, we define the angle $\theta(y)$ subtended at the origin by $\gamma(0)$ and $\gamma(y)$ to be minus the length of the curve $\gamma$ between those two points.

Show that

$$\theta(y) = \int_0^y \frac{1}{(1-t^2)^{1/2}} \, dt$$

for $-1 < y < 1$. Show that there is a real number $\omega$ such that $\theta(y) \to \omega$ as $y \to 1$ through values of $y < 1$, and $\theta(y) \to -\omega$ as $y \to -1$ through values of $y > -1$. We define $\theta(1) = \omega$ and $\theta(-1) = -\omega$.

(ii) Show that $\theta$ is a strictly increasing continuous function on $[-\omega, \omega]$. We may thus define a function $\sin : [-\omega, \omega] \to \mathbb{R}$ by

$$\sin t = \theta^{-1}(t).$$

Show that $\sin$ is once differentiable on $(-\omega, \omega)$ with $\sin't = (1 - (\sin t)^2)^{1/2}$. Show that $\sin$ is twice differentiable on $(-\omega, \omega)$ with $\sin''t = -\sin t$.

(iii) We now define $\sin t = \sin(2\omega - t)$ for $t \in [\omega, 3\omega]$. Show (paying particular attention to behaviour at $\omega$) that $\sin :[-\omega, 3\omega]\to \mathbb{R}$ is a well defined continuous function and that $\sin$ is twice differentiable on $(-\omega, 3\omega)$ with $\sin''t = -\sin t$.

(iv) Show that we can extend the definition of $\sin$ to obtain a twice differentiable function $\sin : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ with $\sin''t = -\sin t$.

(v) Show that, if we define $\cos t = \sin(t - \omega)$, then $\cos^2 t + \sin^2 t = 1$ for all $t$ and $0 \leq \cos s \leq 1$ for all $0 \leq s \leq \omega$. Explain why this gives the 'right geometrical meaning' to $\cos t$ for $0 \leq t \leq \omega$. Show also that, if we set $\pi = 2\omega$, this gives the right geometrical meaning for $\pi$ in terms of the circumference formula for the circle.


### app_k_188 [app_k_188]


K.188. (The trigonometric functions via area.) [9.5, T, ↑] We might argue that it is better to base our definition of angle on area rather than the more delicate concept of length. Consider the part of the unit circle shown in Figure K.3 with $A$ the point $(1,0)$, $B$ the point $(x,(1-x^2)^{1/2})$ and $C$ the point $(x,0)$. Explain why the area of the sector $OAB$ is

$$\theta(x) = \frac{x(1-x^2)^{1/2}}{2} + \int_x^1 (1-t^2)^{1/2} \, dt.$$

Use the definition of $\theta$ just given to obtain an appropriate definition of $\cos t$ in an appropriate range $[0, \omega]$. Show that $\cos$ has the properties we should expect. Extend your definition so that $\cos$ is defined on the whole real line and has the properties we expect.


### app_k_189 [app_k_189]


K.189. [9.5, G, M!] (You should treat this exercise informally.) Here are some simple examples of a phenomenon which has to be accepted in any reasonably advanced account of length, area and volume.

(i) We work in $\mathbb{R}^2$. Let

$$D_n = \{(x, y): (x-n)^2 + y^2 \leq (n+1)^{-2}\}.$$

Sketch $E = \bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} D_n$ and convince yourself that the area of $E$ ought to be $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \text{area } D_n = \pi \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (n+1)^{-2}$, which is finite, but that the length of the boundary of $E$ ought to be $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \text{circumference } D_n = 2\pi \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (n+1)^{-1}$, which is infinite.

(ii) Construct a similar example in $\mathbb{R}^3$ involving volume and area.

(iii) We work in $\mathbb{C}$. Consider

$$E = \{0\} \cup \bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} \bigcup_{r=1}^{n} \{z: |z - 2^{-n} \exp(2\pi i r/n)| \leq 2^{-n-3}\}.$$

Convince yourself that it is reasonable to say that $E$ has finite area but that $E$ has boundary of infinite length.

(iv) (Torricelli's trumpet, often called Gabriel's horn.) This example goes back historically to the very beginning of the calculus. Consider the volume of revolution $E$ obtained by revolving the curve $y = 1/x$ for $x \geq 1$ around the $x$ axis. Thus

$$E = \{(x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}^3: y^2 + z^2 \leq x^{-2}, x \geq 1\}.$$

We write $f(x) = 1/x$. Convince yourself that the volume of $E$ is given by $\pi \int_{1}^{\infty} f(x)^2 \, dx$, which is finite, but the curved surface of $E$ has area $2\pi \int_{1}^{\infty} f(x)(1 + f'(x)^2)^{1/2} \, dx$, which is infinite.

Thus, if we have a trumpet in the form of the curved surface of $E$, we would 'clearly' require an infinite amount of paint to paint its inside (since this has infinite area). Equally 'clearly', since $E$ has finite volume, we can fill the trumpet up with a finite amount of paint and empty it again leaving the inside nicely painted!

Note that by 'winding our trumpet like a ball of knitting' we can keep it within a bounded set (compare parts (i) and (ii)).

(v) (Thor's drinking horn.) As a variation on these themes, consider the volumes of revolution $K$ obtained by revolving the curve $y = x^{-1/2}$ for $x \geq 1$ around the $x$ axis and $K'$ obtained by revolving the curve $y = x^{-1/2} + x^{-1}$ for $x \geq 1$ around the $x$ axis. Thus

$$K = \{(x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}^3: y^2 + z^2 \leq x^{-1}, x \geq 1\}$$
$$K' = \{(x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}^3: y^2 + z^2 \leq (x^{-1/2} + x^{-1})^2, x \geq 1\}.$$

Show that $K$ has infinite volume but that $K' \setminus K$ has finite volume. Sluse, who was the first to discover such an object, wrote to Huygens that he could design a 'glass of small weight which the hardest drinker could not empty'.


### app_k_190 [app_k_190]


K.190. [9.5, T] In Section 9.5 we discussed how to define the line integral

$$\int_\gamma f(\mathbf{x}) \, ds.$$

In this exercise we discuss the vector line integral

$$\int_\gamma f(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{s}.$$

(i) In some sense, we should have

$$\int_\gamma f(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{s} \approx \sum_{j=1}^{N} f(\mathbf{x}_j) (\mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{x}_{j-1}).$$

Discuss informally how this leads to the following variation of the definition for $\int_{\gamma} f(\mathbf{x}) \, ds$ found on page 217.

Let $x, y: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}^2$ have continuous derivatives (with the usual conventions about left and right derivatives at end points) and consider the curve $\gamma: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}^2$ given by $\gamma(t) = (x(t), y(t))$. If $f: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous, then we define

$$\int_\gamma f(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{s} = \int_a^b f(x(t), y(t)) \boldsymbol{\gamma}'(t) \, dt.$$

(Here, as usual, $\gamma'(t) = (x'(t), y'(t))$.)

(ii) Compute $\int_{\gamma_k} f(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{s}$ for each of the six curves $\gamma_k$ on page 213 and the function $f$ given by $f(x,y) = x$.

(iii) (What follows requires Exercise K.186.) We can extend our definitions to general rectifiable curves as follows. Suppose that $\gamma : [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}^2$ is rectifiable. Show that, if we set $\gamma(t) = (X(t), Y(t))$, then the functions $X, Y : [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$ are of bounded variation, and so we may define

$$\int_\gamma f(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{s} = \left(\int_a^b f(X(t), Y(t)) \, dX(t), \int_a^b f(X(t), Y(t)) \, dY(t)\right).$$

Show that, if $\gamma$ is sufficiently well behaved that the definition given in part (i) applies, then the definitions in (i) and (iii) give the same answer.


### app_k_191 [app_k_191]


K.191. [10.2, H, G] Consider the system described in the first few paragraphs of Section 10.2, where $n$ bits are transmitted and each bit has a probability $p$ of being transmitted wrongly, independently of what happens to any other bit.

(i) Explain why the probability that there are $k$ errors in all is given by

$$u_n(k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}.$$

By looking at $u_n(k+1) / u_n(k)$, or otherwise, show that there is an integer $k_0$ with $u_n(k+1) \geq u_n(k)$ for $k \leq k_0 - 1$ and $u_n(k+1) \leq u_n(k)$ for $k_0 \leq k$. Show that $np - 1 < k_0 < np + 1$.

(ii) Let $\epsilon > 0$. Use the ideas of Lemma 10.5 to show that

$$\sum_{|k-np| \geq n\epsilon} u_n(k) \to 0$$

as $n \to \infty$. Deduce that, if we write $N_n$ for the number of errors in a message of length $n$, then

$$\Pr\{|N_n - np| \geq \epsilon n\} \to 0,$$

and so, in particular,

$$\Pr\{N_n \geq (p + \epsilon)n\} \to 0,$$

as $n \to \infty$.

(iii) If we are prepared to use a little more probability theory, then, as the reader almost certainly knows, there is a general method for obtaining the result of (ii). (If the notation is unfamiliar, do not proceed further.) Write $X_j = 0$ if the $j$th bit is transmitted correctly and $X_j = 1$ if not. Show that $\mathbb{E}X_j = p$ and $\operatorname{var}X_j = p(1-p)$. Explain why $N_n = \sum_{j=1}^{n} X_j$, and deduce carefully that $\mathbb{E}N_n = pn$ and $\operatorname{var}N_n = np(1-p)$. Now use Tchebychev's inequality (Lemma 9.47 with $X = N_n - np$) to obtain the equation in (ii).


### app_k_192 [app_k_192]


K.192. [10.3, P, S] This exercise and the next deal with modifications to the axioms for a metric space. Recall that the standard axioms are

(A) $d(x,y) = 0$ if and only if $x = y$

(B) $d(x,y) = d(y,x)$ for all $x, y \in X$

(C) $d(x,y) + d(y,z) \geq d(x,z)$ for all $x,y,z \in X$

(i) Suppose that $d: X^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ satisfies axiom (A) and

$(C)'$ $d(x,y) + d(z,y) \geq d(x,z)$ for all $x,y,z \in X$

Show that $(X,d)$ is a metric space.

(ii) Say everything that there is to say about 'anti-triangle' spaces $(X,d)$ satisfying axioms (A) and (B) together with

(D) $d(x,y) + d(y,z) \leq d(x,z)$ for all $x,y,z \in X$

What can you say if, in addition, we demand $d(x,y) \geq 0$ for all $x, y \in X$?

(iii) Suppose $(X,d)$ satisfies axioms (A) and (C). Show that, if we set $\rho(x,y) = d(x,y) + d(y,x)$, then $(X,\rho)$ is a metric space.


### app_k_193 [app_k_193]


K.193. [10.3, T, S] This exercise is more interesting than the previous one because it deals with a fairly frequent situation. It requires knowledge of the notions of equivalence relation and equivalence class.

Suppose $(X,d)$ satisfies axioms (B) and (C) together with

(A)' $d(x,x) = 0$ for all $x \in X$

Show that the relation $x \sim y$ if $d(x, y) = 0$ is an equivalence relation. If we write $[x]$ for the equivalence class of $x$ and $\tilde{X} = X / \sim$ for the set of equivalence classes, show that $\tilde{d}([x], [y]) = d(x, y)$ gives a well defined function on $\tilde{X}^2$, and that $(\tilde{X}, \tilde{d})$ is a metric space.


### app_k_194 [app_k_194]


K.194. [10.3, T, G] This is easy, but requires enough group theory to understand the statement of the exercise.

Let $G$ be a finite group with identity $e$ generated by a subset $X$. We write $X^{-1} = \{x^{-1} : x \in X\}$. We set $\rho(e) = 0$ and, if $g \neq e$, we set

$$\rho(g) = \min\{N : \text{we can find } x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N \in X \cup X^{-1} \text{ such that } g = x_1 x_2 \ldots x_N\}.$$

Show that $d(g, h) = \rho(gh^{-1})$ defines a metric on $G$.

Show also that $d$ is left invariant, that is to say, $d(gk, hk) = d(g, h)$ for all $g, h, k \in G$. Is it necessarily true that $d$ is right invariant (i.e. $d(kg, kh) = d(g, h)$ for all $g, h, k \in G$)? [Hint, if required: Think about the dihedral group.]

We say that $G$ has diameter $\max_{g \in G} d(e, g)$ (with respect to the generating set $X$).

(i) Show that $S_n$, the permutation group on $n$ elements, is generated by the set $X_1$ of elements of the form $(ij)$. Show that $S_n$ has diameter less than $A_1 n$ (for some constant $A_1$) with respect to $X_1$. Interpret this result in terms of card shuffling.

(ii) Show that $S_n$ is generated by the set $X_2$ of elements of the form $(1j)$. Show that $S_n$ has diameter less than $A_2 n$ (for some constant $A_2$) with respect to $X_2$.

(iii) Show that $S_n$ is generated by the set $X_3$ consisting of the two elements $(12)$ and $(123\ldots n)$. Show that $S_n$ has diameter less than $A_3 n^3$ (for some constant $A_3$) with respect to $X_3$.

(iv) Let $n \geq 2$. Consider the dihedral group $D_n$ generated by $a$ and $b$ subject only to the relations $a^n = e$, $b^2 = e$ and $bab = a^{-1}$. Show that there exists an $A_4 > 0$ such that $D_n$ has diameter at least $A_4 n$ with respect to $X_4 = \{a, b\}$.

(v) How many elements does $S_n$ have? How many does $D_n$ have? Comment very briefly on the relationship (or lack of relationship) between size and diameter shown by parts (i) to (iv).


### app_k_195 [app_k_195]


K.195. [10.3, T, G] We work on $\mathbb{R}$.

(i) Suppose that $|x| > 4^{-k}$ for some integer $k$. Show, by induction on $N$, or otherwise, that if

$$x = \sum_{j=1}^{N} x_j \quad \text{with} \quad |x_j| \leq 4^{-n(j)} \text{ for some integer } n(j) \geq 0 \, [1 \leq j \leq N],$$

then $\sum_{j=1}^{N} 2^{-n(j)} \geq 2^{-k}$.

(ii) Show that

$$d(x, y) = \inf\left\{\sum_{j=1}^{N} 2^{-n(j)}: x - y = \sum_{j=1}^{N} x_j \text{ with } |x_j| \leq 4^{-n(j)} \text{ for some integer } n(j) \geq 0 \, [1 \leq j \leq N]\right\}$$

defines a metric on $\mathbb{R}$ such that $d(x + a, y + a) = d(x, y)$ for all $x, y, a \in \mathbb{R}$.

(iii) Show that we can find a constant $K > 1$ such that

$$K|x-y|^{1/2} \geq d(x, y) \geq K^{-1}|x-y|^{1/2}$$

for all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$. Deduce that $d(x_n, y_n) \to 0$ if and only if $|x_n - y_n| \to 0$.

(iv) Sketch the graph of the function $f(x) = d(0, x)$. (This requires thought.)


### app_k_196 [app_k_196]


K.196. [10.3, P] Let $(X,d)$ and $(Y,\rho)$ be metric spaces and let $X_{1}$ and $X_{2}$ be subsets of $X$ such that $X_{1} \cup X_{2} = X$. Suppose $f: X \to Y$ is such that $f|_{X_j}$ is continuous as a function from $X_{j}$ to $Y$. Which, if any, of the following statements are true and which may be false? (Give proofs or counterexamples as appropriate.)

(i) The function $f$ is automatically continuous.

(ii) If $X_{1}$ and $X_{2}$ are closed, then $f$ is continuous.

(iii) If $X_{1}$ and $X_{2}$ are open, then $f$ is continuous.

(iv) If $X_{1}$ is closed and $X_{2}$ is open, then $f$ is continuous.

Give $\mathbb{R}^n$ its usual Euclidean norm. Suppose $\mathbf{g}: X \to \mathbb{R}^n$ is continuous. Show that there is a continuous function $\mathbf{f}: X \to \mathbb{R}^n$ such that $\mathbf{f}(x) = \mathbf{g}(x)$ when $\|\mathbf{g}(x)\| < 1$ and $\|\mathbf{f}(x)\| = 1$ when $\|\mathbf{g}(x)\| \geq 1$.


### app_k_197 [app_k_197]


K.197. [10.3, P] (i) Give an example of a metric space $(X,d)$ which contains a set $A$ which is both open and closed but $A \neq X$ and $A \neq \emptyset$.

(ii) Suppose $(X,d)$ is a metric space, that $A$ and $B$ are open subsets of $X$ with $A \cup B = X$, and that we are given a function $f:X \to \mathbb{R}$. Show that, if the restrictions $f|_{A}:A \to \mathbb{R}$ and $f|_{B}:B \to \mathbb{R}$ are continuous, then $f$ is continuous. (This repeats part of Exercise K.196.)

(ii) Consider the interval $[0,1]$ with the usual metric. Suppose that $A$ and $B$ are open subsets of $[0,1]$ with $A \cup B = [0,1]$ and $A \cap B = \emptyset$. Define $f(x) = 1$ if $x \in A$ and $f(x) = 0$ if $x \in B$. By considering the result of (ii), show that $A = \emptyset$ or $B = \emptyset$. Deduce that the only subsets of $[0,1]$ which are both open and closed are $[0,1]$ and $\emptyset$.

(iv) Suppose that $A$ is an open subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $\mathbf{a} \in A$, $\mathbf{b} \notin A$. By considering $f: [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ given by $f(t) = 1$ if $(1-t)\mathbf{a} + t\mathbf{b} \in A$ and $f(t) = 0$ otherwise, show that $A$ is not closed. Thus the only subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$ which are both open and closed are $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $\varnothing$.


### app_k_198 [app_k_198]


K.198. [10.3, T] Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space. If $A$ is a subset of $X$, show that the following definitions are equivalent.

(i) The point $x \in \bar{A}$ if and only if we can find $x_n \in A$ such that $d(x_n, x) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

(ii) Let $\mathcal{F}$ be the collection of closed sets $F$ such that $F \supseteq A$. Then $\bar{A} = \bigcap_{F \in \mathcal{F}} F$.

(iii) $\bar{A}$ is a closed set with $\bar{A} \supseteq A$ such that, if $F$ is closed and $F \supseteq A$, then $F \supseteq \bar{A}$.

(It is worth noting that condition (iii) has the disadvantage that it is not clear that such a set always exists.) We call $\bar{A}$ the closure of $A$. We also write $\bar{A} = \operatorname{Cl} A$.

If $B$ is a subset of $X$, show that the following definitions are equivalent.

(i)' The point $x \in B^{\circ}$ if and only if we can find $\epsilon > 0$ such that $y \in B$ whenever $d(x, y) < \epsilon$.

(ii)' Let $\mathcal{U}$ be the collection of open sets $U$ such that $U \subseteq B$. Then $B^{\circ} = \bigcup_{U \in \mathcal{U}} U$.

State a condition (iii)' along the lines of (iii), and show that it is equivalent to (i)' and (ii)'.

We call $B^{\circ}$ the interior of $B$. We also write $B^{\circ} = \operatorname{Int} B$.

Show that $\operatorname{Cl} A = X \setminus \operatorname{Int}(X \setminus A)$ and $\operatorname{Int} A = X \setminus \operatorname{Cl}(X \setminus A)$. Show that $A = \operatorname{Cl} A$ if and only if $A$ is closed and $A = \operatorname{Int} A$ if and only if $A$ is open.

Suppose we work in $\mathbb{R}$ with the usual distance. Find the interior and closure of the sets $\mathbb{Q}$, $\mathbb{Z}$, $\{1/n : n \in \mathbb{Z}, n \geq 1\}$, $[a,b]$, $(a,b)$ and $[a,b)$ where $b > a$.

Let $(X,d)$ and $(Y,\rho)$ be metric spaces. Show that $f:X \to Y$ is continuous if and only if $f(\bar{A}) \subseteq \overline{f(A)}$ for all subsets $A$ of $X$.

(An unimportant warning.) Find a metric space $(X,d)$ and an $x \in X$ such that

$$\operatorname{Cl}\{y: d(x,y) < 1\} \neq \{y: d(x,y) \leq 1\}$$

and

$$\operatorname{Int}\{y: d(x,y) \leq 1\} \neq \{y: d(x,y) < 1\}.$$


### app_k_199 [app_k_199]


K.199. [10.3, P, ↑] We use the notation of Exercise K.198. Show that, if $U$ is open,

$$\operatorname{Cl}(\operatorname{Int}(\operatorname{Cl} U)) = \operatorname{Cl} U.$$

Show that, starting from a given $A$, it is not possible to produce more than 7 distinct sets by repeated application of the operators Cl and Int.

Give an example of a set $A$ in $\mathbb{R}$ with the usual metric which gives rise to 7 distinct sets in this way.


### app_k_200 [app_k_200]


K.200. [10.4, T] The results of this exercise are simple but give some insight into the nature of the norm.

(i) Let $\|\cdot\|$ be a norm on a real vector space. Show that the closed unit ball

$$\Gamma = \bar{B}(\mathbf{0}, 1) = \{\mathbf{x} \in V: \|\mathbf{x}\| \leq 1\}$$

has the following properties.

(A) $\Gamma$ is convex, that is to say, if $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \Gamma$ and $1 \geq \lambda \geq 0$, then $\lambda\mathbf{x} + (1-\lambda)\mathbf{y} \in \Gamma$.

(B) $\Gamma$ is centrally symmetric, that is to say, if $\mathbf{x} \in \Gamma$, then $-\mathbf{x} \in \Gamma$.

(C) $\Gamma$ is absorbing, that is to say, if $\mathbf{x} \in V$, then we can find a strictly positive real number $\lambda$ with $\lambda\mathbf{x} \in \Gamma$.

(D) If $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$, then there exists a $t > 0$ such that (when $s \geq 0$) $s\mathbf{x} \in \Gamma$ if and only if $t \geq s$.

(ii) Show, conversely, that if $\Gamma$ is a convex, centrally symmetric, absorbing set obeying condition (D), then $\Gamma$ is the closed unit ball of a norm.

(iii) Suppose that $\Gamma$ is a convex, centrally symmetric, absorbing set obeying the following condition.

$(D)'$ If $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$, then there exists a $\mu > 0$ such that $\mu\mathbf{x} \notin \Gamma$.

Show that there exists a norm $\|\cdot\|$ such that $\|\mathbf{x}\| < 1$ implies $\mathbf{x} \in \Gamma$ and $\|\mathbf{x}\| > 1$ implies $\mathbf{x} \notin \Gamma$.

(iv) If $\|\cdot\|_A$ and $\|\cdot\|_B$ are norms on $V$ and $K > 0$, state and prove a necessary and sufficient condition in terms of the closed unit balls of the two norms for the inequality

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_A \leq K\|\mathbf{x}\|_B$$

to hold for all $\mathbf{x} \in V$.


### app_k_201 [app_k_201]


K.201. [10.4, P] Consider the real vector space $l^{\infty}$ of real sequences $\mathbf{a} = (a_1, a_2, \ldots)$ with norm $\|\mathbf{a}\|_{\infty} = \sup_{n \geq 1} |a_n|$. Show that the set

$$E = \{\mathbf{a}: \text{there exists an } N \text{ with } a_n = 0 \text{ for all } n \geq N\}$$

is a subspace of $l^{\infty}$ but not a closed subset.

Show that, if we set $F = \{\mathbf{a} : a_{2n} = 0 \text{ for all } n \geq 1\}$, then $F$ is a closed infinite-dimensional subspace of $l^{\infty}$.

Show that any finite-dimensional subspace of any normed vector space is closed.


### app_k_202 [app_k_202]


K.202. [10.4, T] Let $(E,\|\cdot\|)$ be a real normed space and let $\mathbb{R}$ have its usual norm. Let $T:E \to \mathbb{R}$ be a linear map.

(i) Show that, if $T$ is not the zero map, the null space

$$N = T^{-1}(0) = \{\mathbf{e} \in E: T\mathbf{e} = 0\}$$

is a subspace of $E$ such that, if $\mathbf{y} \notin N$, then every $\mathbf{x} \in E$ can be written in exactly one way as $\mathbf{x} = \lambda\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{e}$ with $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbf{e} \in N$. Comment very briefly on what happens when $T$ is the zero map.

(ii) Suppose that $T$ is not the zero map and $N$ is as in (i). Show that $N$ is closed if and only if, whenever $\mathbf{y} \notin N$, there exists a $\delta(\mathbf{y}) > 0$ such that $\|\lambda\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{e}\| \geq |\lambda|\delta(\mathbf{y})$ for all $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbf{e} \in N$.

(iii) Show that $T$ is continuous if and only if its null space $T^{-1}(0)$ is closed.

(iv) Find $T^{-1}(0)$, and check directly that the conclusions of (iii) are satisfied if we take $E = s_{00}$ and take $T$ as in part (ii) of Exercise 10.53. (There are five norms to check.)


### app_k_203 [app_k_203]


K.203. [10.4, H] This exercise and the one that follows are included more for the benefit of the author than the reader. I was worried whether the proof of Theorem 10.45 actually required the use of results from analysis. In this exercise I outline a simple example, supplied by Imre Leader, which should convince all but the most stubborn that the proof should indeed use analysis.

Consider the map $N:\mathbb{Q}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ given by $N(x,y) = |x + y\sqrt{2}|$. Show that:

(1) $N(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Q}^2$

(2) If $N(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, then $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$.

(3) If $\lambda \in \mathbb{Q}$ and $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Q}^2$, then $N(\lambda\mathbf{x}) = |\lambda| N(\mathbf{x})$.

(4) (The triangle inequality) If $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{Q}^2$, then $N(\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}) \leq N(\mathbf{x}) + N(\mathbf{y})$.

Let $\|(\,x,y)\| = \max(|x|, |y|)$. Show that, given any $\delta > 0$, we can find a $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Q}^2$ such that

$$N(\mathbf{x}) < \delta\|\mathbf{x}\|.$$

Explain why, if $N: \mathbb{Q}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ is any function satisfying conditions (1) to (4), there exists a $K > 0$ such that

$$N(\mathbf{x}) < K\|\mathbf{x}\|$$

for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Q}^2$.


### app_k_204 [app_k_204]


K.204. [10.4, H!, ↑↑] In this exercise we give another example of Imre Leader which proves conclusively that Theorem 10.45 is indeed a result of analysis. It is quite complicated, and I include it to provide pleasure for the expert rather than enlightenment for the beginner. Our object is to prove the following result.

There is a map $N:\mathbb{Q}^3 \to \mathbb{Q}$ such that:

(1) $N(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Q}^3$

(2) If $N(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, then $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$.

(3) If $\lambda \in \mathbb{Q}$ and $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Q}^3$, then $N(\lambda\mathbf{x}) = |\lambda| N(\mathbf{x})$.

(4) (The triangle inequality) If $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{Q}^3$, then $N(\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}) \leq N(\mathbf{x}) + N(\mathbf{y})$

(5) If we write $\|\mathbf{x}\|_{\infty} = \max_{1 \leq j \leq 3} |x_j|$, then, given any $\delta > 0$, we can find a $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Q}^3$ such that

$$N(\mathbf{x}) < \delta\|\mathbf{x}\|_{\infty}.$$

(i) Why does this result show that Theorem 10.45 is a result of analysis?

(ii) (The proof begins here. It will be helpful to recall the ideas and notation of Exercise K.200.) We work in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with the usual Euclidean norm $\|\cdot\|$. Suppose $\mathbf{u}_1, \mathbf{u}_2, \ldots$ are elements of $\mathbb{R}^2$ none of which are scalar multiples of any other and such that $1/2 \leq \|\mathbf{u}_j\| \leq 2$ for all $j \geq 1$. Show, by using induction, or otherwise, that we can find an $a_n \in \mathbb{Q}$ such that, writing $\mathbf{v}_n = a_n\mathbf{u}_n$, we have

(a) $1/2 < \|\mathbf{v}_n\| < 2$, and

(b) $\mathbf{v}_n \notin \Gamma_{n-1}$

where $\Gamma_0$ is the closed disc $\bar{B}(\mathbf{0}, 1/2)$ of radius $1/2$ and centre $\mathbf{0}$ and, if $n \geq 1$

$$\Gamma_n = \{\lambda\mathbf{x} + \mu\mathbf{v}_n : \mathbf{x} \in \Gamma_{n-1}, |\lambda| + |\mu| = 1, \lambda, \mu \in \mathbb{R}\}.$$

(For those who know the jargon, $\Gamma_n$ is the convex hull of $\Gamma_{n-1} \cup \{\mathbf{v}_n, -\mathbf{v}_n\}$.)

(iii) Continuing with the ideas of (ii), write $\Gamma$ for the closure of $\bigcup_{n=0}^{\infty} \Gamma_n$. Show that $\Gamma$ is a closed centrally symmetric convex set with

$$\bar{B}(\mathbf{0}, 1/2) \subseteq \Gamma \subseteq \bar{B}(\mathbf{0}, 2).$$

Show further that, if $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$, then $\mu\mathbf{v}_n \in \Gamma$ if and only if $|\mu| \leq 1$. Use the results of Exercise K.200 to deduce the existence of a norm $\|\cdot\|_*$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that $\|\mathbf{u}_n\|_* \in \mathbb{Q}$ for all $n$ and

$$2\|\mathbf{x}\| \geq \|\mathbf{x}\|_* \geq \|\mathbf{x}\|/2$$

for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^2$.

(iv) Let

$$E = \{(a + b\sqrt{2}, c + b\sqrt{3}) : a, b, c \in \mathbb{Q}\}.$$

By considering a suitable sequence $\mathbf{u}_n \in E$, show that there exists a norm $\|\cdot\|_*$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that $\|\mathbf{e}\|_* \in \mathbb{Q}$ for all $\mathbf{e} \in E$, and

$$2\|\mathbf{x}\| \geq \|\mathbf{x}\|_* \geq \|\mathbf{x}\|/2$$

for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^2$.
