(i) Suppose that $a_{n} \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_{n} x^{n}$ has radius of convergence $R > 0$. Set $f(x) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_{n} x^{n}$ for $|x| < R$. Show that, if there exist $x_{m} \to 0$ with $f(x_{m}) = 0$ and $x_{m} \neq 0$, then $a_{n} = 0$ for all $n$, and so $f = 0$.

(ii) Explain why a polynomial of degree at most $n$ with $n + 1$ zeros must be zero.

(iii) Give an example of a power series with radius of convergence $\infty$ which has infinitely many zeros but is not identically zero.

(iv) Let $F$ be as in Example 7.5. Set $G(x) = F(x)\sin x^{-1}$ for $x \neq 0$ and $G(0) = 0$. Show that $G$ is infinitely differentiable everywhere (this will probably require you to go through much the same argument as we used for $F$). Show that there exist $x_{m} \to 0$ with $G(x_{m}) = 0$ and $x_{m} \neq 0$, but $G$ is not identically zero.


### app_k_270 [app_k_270]


K.270. [11.5, P] This exercise prepares the way for Exercise K.271. The first two parts will probably be familiar, but are intended to provide a hint for the third part.

(i) If $n \geq r \geq 1$, show that $\binom{n}{r-1} + \binom{n}{r} = \binom{n+1}{r}$.

(ii) Use induction to show that

$$(x+y)^{n} = \sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{n}{r} x^{r} y^{n-r}$$

whenever $n$ is a positive integer and $x$ and $y$ are real.

(iii) Suppose that $n$ is a positive integer and $x$ and $y$ are real. Verify the next formula directly for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$ (we take $\prod_{j=0}^{-1}(x - j) = 1$), and then prove it for all $n$.

$$\sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{n}{r} \prod_{j=0}^{r-1}(x-j) \prod_{k=0}^{n-r-1}(y-k) = \prod_{q=0}^{n-1}(x+y-q).$$


# app_k_271 [app_k_271]


K.271. [11.5, T, ↑] The following is a modification of Cauchy's proof of the binomial theorem. We shall work in $\mathbb{R}$. We take $w$ as a fixed real number with $|w| < 1$.

(i) Use the ratio test to show that $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!} \prod_{j=0}^{n-1} (x-j) w^n$ converges. By thinking about your proof and the Weierstrass M-test, improve this result to show that $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!} \prod_{j=0}^{n-1} (x-j) w^n$ converges uniformly for $|x| \leq M$ whenever $M$ is fixed. We write

$$f (x) = 1 + \sum_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} \frac {1}{n !} \prod_ {j = 0} ^ {n - 1} (x - j) w ^ {n}.$$

Explain why $f$ is continuous.

(ii) Use Exercise 5.38 (first proved by Cauchy) and Exercise K.270 to show that

$$f (x) f (y) = f (x + y)$$

for all $x$ and $y$.

(iii) Show that $f(0) = 1$ and deduce that $f(x) \neq 0$ for all $x$. Deduce, quoting any theorem you need, that $f(x) > 0$ for all $x$.

(iv) Explain why we can define $g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ by $g(x) = \log f(x)$ and why $g$ is continuous. Show that

$$g (x) + g (y) = g (x + y)$$

for all $x$ and $y$. Use Exercise K.101 (i) (first proved by Cauchy) to show that $g(x) = ax$, and so $f(x) = b^{x}$ for some real positive number $b$.

(v) Find $b$ by considering $f(1)$. Deduce that

$$
(1 + w) ^ {x} = 1 + \sum_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} \frac {1}{n !} \prod_ {j = 0} ^ {n - 1} (x - j) w ^ {n}.
$$

[One problem faced by anyone teaching elementary analysis in France is that every theorem seems to be called Cauchy's theorem. A glance at the exercise above shows why$^{16}$.]


# app_k_272 [app_k_272]


K.272. [11.5, T, ↑↑] (Part (iii) of this exercise makes strong use of Exercise K.79.) Exercise 11.86 raises the question of when we can tell whether a differential equation of the form $u'(t) = f(t, u(t))$ has a power series solution. The following theorem of Cauchy (see the concluding remark of the previous exercise) gives a rather natural condition.

Suppose that $c_{n,m} \in \mathbb{R}$ $[n, m \geq 0]$ and that there exist a $\rho > 0$ and a $K > 0$ such that

$$
\left| c _ {n, m} \right| \leq K \rho^ {n + m} \text { for all } n, m \geq 0.
$$

(Exercise K.77 (c) shows why we use this condition.) Then we can find a $\delta > 0$ with $\rho > \delta$ and $a_n \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n t^n$ converges to $u(t)$, say, for all $|t| < \delta$ and such that, writing

$$
f (x, y) = \sum_ {n = 0} ^ {\infty} \sum_ {m = 0} ^ {\infty} c _ {n, m} x ^ {n} y ^ {m}
$$

for $|x|, |y| < \rho^{-1}$, we have $u(0) = 0$, $|u(t)| < \rho$ and

$$
u ^ {′} (t) = f (t, u (t))
$$

for all $|t| \leq \delta$.

(i) The object of this exercise is to prove Cauchy's theorem, but in the first two parts of the exercise we are merely interested in seeing what is going on and so we work in a non-rigorous manner. Assuming that a power series solution $u(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n t^n$ with the required properties actually exists, show, by formal manipulation, that $a_0 = 0$ and $k a_k$ should be the coefficient of $x^{k-1}$ in

$$
\sum_ {n = 0} ^ {\infty} \sum_ {m = 0} ^ {\infty} c _ {n, m} x ^ {n} \left(\sum_ {r = 0} ^ {\infty} a _ {r} x ^ {r}\right) ^ {m}.
$$

Explain why that coefficient is a finite sum only depending on $a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_{k-1}$. Show that, if we define $A_0 = 0$, and define $A_1, A_2, \ldots$ formally by means of the equation

$$
\star \quad \frac {d}{d t} \left(\sum_ {n = 0} ^ {\infty} A _ {n} t ^ {n}\right) = \sum_ {n = 0} ^ {\infty} \sum_ {m = 0} ^ {\infty} K \rho^ {n + m} t ^ {n} \left(\sum_ {r = 0} ^ {\infty} A _ {r} t ^ {r}\right) ^ {m},
$$

then $|a_{k}| \leq A_{k}$.

(ii) We continue with the ideas of (i). Show that $\star$ can be rewritten (at least, formally) as

$$
\star \star \quad \frac {d w}{d t} = \frac {K}{(1 - \rho t) (1 - \rho w)},
$$

where $w(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} A_n t^n$. Solve equation ★★ for $w(0) = 0$.

(iii) We now reverse the non-rigorous argument, but this time proceed rigorously. Show that equation $\star \star$ has a solution $w(t)$ with $w(0) = 0$ and the property that we can find $A_{j}$ and $\eta > 0$ such that $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} A_{n} t^{n}$ has radius of convergence at least $\eta$ and $w(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} A_{n} t^{n}$ for all $|t| < \eta$. Show that the $A_{n}$ satisfy equation $\star$ for $|t| < \eta$. Deduce that, if $a_{k}$ is given by equating coefficients in the equation

$$
\sum_ {n = 0} ^ {\infty} \sum_ {m = 0} ^ {\infty} a _ {n} x ^ {n} = \sum_ {m = 0} ^ {\infty} c _ {n, m} x ^ {n} \left(\sum_ {r = 0} ^ {\infty} a _ {r} x ^ {r}\right) ^ {m},
$$

then $|a_k| \leq A_k$ and $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n t^n$ has radius of convergence at least $\eta$. Show that, if we set $u(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n t^n$ for $|t| < \eta$, then $u(0) = 0$ and there exists a $\delta$ with $0 < \delta \leq \eta$ such that $|u(t)| < \rho$ for $|t| < \delta$. Show that $\delta$ and $u$ satisfy the conclusions of Cauchy's theorem.


# Convolution [app_k_273]


K.273. (Convolution.) [11.6, T] Let us write $C_P(\mathbb{R})$ for the set of continuous functions $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ which are periodic with period $2\pi$.

(i) Show that, if $f,g\in C_P(\mathbb{R})$, then the equation

$$
f * g (t) = \frac {1}{2 \pi} \int_ {- \pi} ^ {\pi} f (t - s) g (s) d s
$$

gives a well defined, continuous, $2\pi$ periodic function $f*g:\mathbb{R}\to \mathbb{R}$. (Thus $f*g\in C_P(\mathbb{R})$.)

(ii) Show that, if $f,g\in C_P(\mathbb{R})$, then

$$
f * g (t) = \frac {1}{2 \pi} \int_ {a} ^ {2 \pi + a} f (t - s) g (s) d s
$$

for all $t\in \mathbb{R}$ and $a\in \mathbb{R}$.

(iii) Show that, if $f,g,h\in C_P(\mathbb{R})$ and $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$, then

$$
(\lambda f) * g = \lambda (f * g), \quad f * (g + h) = f * g + f * h,$$
$$f * g = g * f \quad \text{and} \quad f * (g * h) = (f * g) * h.
$$

(iv) Suppose that $f, g \in C_P(\mathbb{R})$ and $f$ is differentiable with $f' \in C_P(\mathbb{R})$. Show that $f * g$ is differentiable and

$$
(f * g) ^ {′} = f ^ {′} * g.
$$

(v) Suppose that $f, u_n \in C_P(\mathbb{R})$, that $u_n(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, that $u_n(t) = 0$ for $\pi / n \leq |t| \leq \pi$, and that

$$
\frac {1}{2 \pi} \int_ {- \pi} ^ {\pi} u _ {n} (t) d t = 1.
$$

Show that $u_{n}*f\to f$ uniformly as $n\to \infty$.

(vii) Suppose that $f \in C_P(\mathbb{R})$ and $\epsilon > 0$. Show, by using parts (iv) and (v), that there exists a twice differentiable function $g$ with $g, g', g'' \in C_P(\mathbb{R})$ such that $\| f - g \|_{∞} \leq \epsilon$.

(viii) Let us write $C_P(\mathbb{C})$ for the set of continuous functions $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{C}$ which are periodic with period $2\pi$. Show, in as much detail as you consider desirable, that parts (i) to (vi) hold with $C_P(\mathbb{R})$ replaced by $C_P(\mathbb{C})$.


# app_k_274 [app_k_274]


K.274. [11.6, T, ↑] We continue with the notation of the previous exercise.

(i) Suppose that $f, g \in C_P(\mathbb{C})$. Show that

$$
\widehat {f * g} (n) = \hat {f} (n) \hat {g} (n)
$$

for all integers $n$. (We say that 'taking Fourier coefficients converts convolution to multiplication'.)

(ii) Show that, if $f \in C_P(\mathbb{C})$, then

$$
| \hat {f} (n) | \leq \| f \| _ {∞}.
$$

(iii) Show that, if $f,g\in C_P(\mathbb{C})$, then

$$
| \hat {f} (n) | \leq | \hat {g} (n) | + \| f - g \| _ {∞}.
$$

(iv) Use part (iii) of this exercise, part (vii) of Exercise K.273 and Exercise 11.97 to show that, if $f \in C_P(\mathbb{C})$, then $\hat{f}(n) \to 0$ as $|n| \to \infty$. (This is a version of the important Riemann-Lebesgue lemma.)

(v) Suppose, if possible, that there exists an $e \in C_P(\mathbb{C})$ such that $e * f = f$ for all $f \in C_P(\mathbb{C})$. Find $\hat{e}$ and use (iv) to show that no such $e$ can exist. (Informally, convolution on $C_P(\mathbb{C})$ has no unit.)


# app_k_275 [app_k_275]


K.275. [11.6, T, ↑] This exercise uses the version of the Riemann-Lebesgue lemma proved in Exercise K.274 (iv). If $f \in C_P(\mathbb{R})$, we write

$$
S _ {n} (f, t) = \sum_ {j = - n} ^ {n} \hat {f} (n) \exp (i n t).
$$

(i) Suppose that $f_1, g_1 \in C_P(\mathbb{R})$ and $f_1(t) = g_1(t)\sin t$ for all $t$. Show that $\hat{f}_1(j) = \left(\hat{g}_1(j - 1) - \hat{g}_1(j + 1)\right) / 2i$, and deduce that $S_n(f_1,0) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

(ii) Suppose that $f_{2} \in C_{P}(\mathbb{R})$, $f_{2}(n\pi) = 0$ and $f_{2}$ is differentiable at $n\pi$ for all integers $n$. Show that there exists a $g_{2} \in C_{P}(\mathbb{R})$ such that $f_{2}(t) = g_{2}(t)\sin t$ for all $t$, and deduce that $S_{n}(f_{2},0) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

(iii) Suppose that $f_{3} \in C_{P}(\mathbb{R})$, $f_{3}(0) = 0$ and $f_{3}$ is differentiable at 0. Write $f_{4}(t) = f_{3}(2t)$. Compute $\hat{f}_{4}(j)$ in terms of the Fourier coefficients of $f_{3}$. Show that $S_{n}(f_{4},0) \to 0$, and deduce that $S_{n}(f_{3},0) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

(iv) Suppose that $f \in C_P(\mathbb{R})$, and $f$ is differentiable at some point $x$. Show that $S_n(f, x) \to f(x)$ as $n \to \infty$.


# app_k_276 [app_k_276]


K.276. [12.1, H] The result of this exercise is a special case of part of Lemma 13.4. However, precisely because it is a special case, some readers may find it a useful introduction to the ideas of Section 13.1.

(i) Let $\delta > 0$ and let $f: \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C}$ be an everywhere differentiable function such that $f(0) = 0$ and $|f'(z) - 1| \leq 1/2$ for all $|z| \leq \delta$. Set $X = \{z \in \mathbb{C}: |z| \leq \delta\}$. If $|w| \leq \delta/2$ and we define $Tz = z - f(z) + w$ for $z \in X$, show, by using the mean value inequality (Exercise 11.67), that $Tz \in X$ whenever $z \in X$. Thus $T$ is a function $T: X \to X$.

(ii) We continue with the hypotheses and notation of part (i). Show that $T$ is a contraction mapping, and deduce that the equation $f(z) = w$ has exactly one solution with $|z| \leq \delta$.

(iii) Let $F: \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C}$ be an everywhere differentiable function with continuous derivative. Suppose further that $F(0) = 0$ and $F'(0) = 1$. Show that there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that, if $|w| \leq \delta / 2$, the equation $F(z) = w$ has exactly one solution with $|z| \leq \delta$.

(iv) Let $g: \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C}$ be an everywhere differentiable function with continuous derivative. Show that, if $g'(0) \neq 0$, there exist $\eta_1, \eta_2 > 0$ such that, if $|ω - g(0)| \leq \eta_1$, the equation $g(z) = w$ has exactly one solution with $|z| \leq \eta_2$.

(v) If we omit the condition $g'(0) \neq 0$ in (iv), does it remain true that there exists an $\eta > 0$ such that, if $|ω - g(0)| \leq \eta$, the equation $g(z) = w$ has a solution? Give a proof or counterexample.

(vi) If we omit the condition $g'(0) \neq 0$ in (iv) but add the condition $g''(0) \neq 0$, does it remain true that there exist $\eta_1, \eta_2 > 0$ such that, if $|ω - g(0)| \leq \eta_1$, the equation $g(z) = w$ has at most one solution with $|z| \leq \eta_2$? Give a proof or counterexample.


# app_k_277 [app_k_277]


K.277. [12.1, P, ↑] We work in $\mathbb{C}$. Consider the following statement.

There exists a $\delta > 0$ such that, if $|w| \leq \delta$, the equation $f_{j}(z) = w$ has a solution.

Give a proof or counterexample in each of the following cases.

(i) $f_{1}(z) = z^{*}$

(ii) $f_{2}(z) = z + z^{*}$

(iii) $f_{3}(z) = z + |z|$

(iv) $f_{4}(z) = z + |z|^{2}$

Is our statement true for all $F$ in the following cases? Give a proof or counterexample.

(v) $f_{5}(z) = F(z) + |z|$, where $F: \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C}$ is an everywhere differentiable function with continuous derivative, $F(0) = 0$ and $F'(0) = 1$.

(vi) $f_{6}(z) = F(z) + |z|^{2}$, where $F: \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C}$ is an everywhere differentiable function with continuous derivative, $F(0) = 0$ and $F'(0) = 1$.


# Newton-Raphson [app_k_278]


K.278. (Newton-Raphson.) [12.1, T]

(i) Suppose that $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a twice differentiable function such that

$$
\left| \frac {f (x) f ^ {″} (x)}{f ^ {′} (x) ^ {2}} \right| \leq \lambda
$$

(thus, implicitly, $f(x) \neq 0$) for all $x$ and some $|\lambda| < 1$. Show that the mapping

$$
T x = x - \frac {f (x)}{f ^ {′} (x)}
$$

is a contraction mapping, and deduce that $f$ has a unique root $y$.

(ii) Suppose that $F:\mathbb{R}\to \mathbb{R}$ is a twice differentiable function such that

$$
\left| \frac {F (x) F ^ {″} (x)}{F ^ {′} (x) ^ {2}} \right| \leq \lambda
$$

for all $|x|\leq a$ and some $|\lambda | < 1$, and that $F(0) = 0$. Consider the mapping

$$
T x = x - \frac {F (x)}{F ^ {′} (x)}.
$$

Show that $T^n x \to 0$ whenever $|x| \leq a$.

Suppose that $F$ is three times differentiable. By using a local Taylor theorem, or otherwise, show that there exists an $\eta > 0$ such that

$$
\left| T x \right| \leq \left(\left| \frac {F ^ {″} (0)}{2 F ^ {′} (0)} \right| + 1\right) x ^ {2}
$$

whenever $|x| \leq \eta$.

(iii) If you know what the Newton-Raphson method is, comment on the relevance of the results of (i) and (ii) to that method. Comment in particular on the speed of convergence.


# app_k_279 [app_k_279]


K.279. [12.1, G, P, S] (This is a short exercise and involves no analysis.) Suppose $f$, $g: X \to X$ are such that $fg = gf$. Show that, if $f$ has a unique fixed point, then $g$ has a fixed point. Can $g$ have more than one fixed point? (Give a proof or counterexample.)

Show that, if we merely know that $f$ has fixed points, it does not follow that $g$ has any.


# app_k_280 [app_k_280]


K.280. [12.1, P] If $(X,d)$ is a complete metric space and $T:X\to X$ is a surjective map such that

$$
d (T x, T y) \geq K d (x, y)
$$

for all $x, y \in X$ and some $K > 1$, show that $T$ has a unique fixed point.

By considering the map $T: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ defined by $T(n + x) = 1 + 4n + 2x$ for $0 \leq x < 1$ and $n$ an integer, or otherwise, show that the condition $T$ surjective cannot be dropped.


# app_k_281 [app_k_281]


K.281. [12.1, P] We work in $\mathbb{R}^m$ with the usual distance. Let $E$ be a closed non-empty subset of $\mathbb{R}^m$ and let $T$ be a map $T: E \to E$.

(i) Suppose $\| T(\mathbf{a}) - T(\mathbf{b}) \| < \| \mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b} \|$ for all $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b} \in E$ with $\mathbf{a} \neq \mathbf{b}$. We saw in Example 12.4 that $T$ need not have a fixed point. Show that, if $T$ has a fixed point, it is unique.

(ii) Suppose $\| T(\mathbf{a}) - T(\mathbf{b}) \| > \| \mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b} \|$ for all $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b} \in E$ with $\mathbf{a} \neq \mathbf{b}$. Show that $T$ need not have a fixed point but, if $T$ has a fixed point, it is unique.

(iii) Suppose $\| T(\mathbf{a}) - T(\mathbf{b}) \| = \| \mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b} \|$ for all $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b} \in E$. Show that $T$ need not have a fixed point and that, if $T$ has a fixed point, it need not be unique.

(iv) Suppose now that $E$ is non-empty, closed and bounded and

$$
\| T ({\mathbf {a}}) - T ({\mathbf {b}}) \| <   \| {\mathbf {a}} - {\mathbf {b}} \|
$$

for all $\mathbf{a}$, $\mathbf{b} \in E$ with $\mathbf{a} \neq \mathbf{b}$. By considering $\inf_{{\mathbf{x}} \in E} \| {\mathbf{x}} - T({\mathbf{x}}) \|$, or otherwise, show that $T$ has a fixed point.

(v) Suppose that $E = \mathbb{R}^m$ and

$$
\| T ({\mathbf {a}}) - T ({\mathbf {b}}) \| <   \| {\mathbf {a}} - {\mathbf {b}} \|
$$

for all $\mathbf{a}$, $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ with $\mathbf{a} \neq \mathbf{b}$. Show that, if there exist a point $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ and a $K > 0$ such that $\| T^n\mathbf{c}\| < K$ for all positive integers $n$, then $T$ has a fixed point.


# app_k_282 [app_k_282]


K.282. [12.1, G, P, S] (This is easy if you see what is going on.) Show by means of an example that an isometry (distance preserving map) $f: X \to X$ on a metric space $(X, d)$ need not be bijective.

Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space. Show that the set of bijective isometries $f:X\to X$ forms a group $G(X)$ under composition.

By choosing $X$ to be an appropriate closed bounded subset of $\mathbb{R}^2$ with the usual metric, or otherwise, prove the following statements about $G(X)$ when $X$ has the Bolzano-Weierstrass property.

(i) $G(X)$ need not be Abelian.

(ii) There exists an $X$ such that $G(X)$ has only one element.

(iii) There exists an $X$ such that $G(X)$ has infinitely many elements.

If $n \geq 2$, give an example of an $(X, d)$ with the Bolzano-Weierstrass property and a $T: X \to X$ such that $T^{m}$ has no fixed points for $1 \leq m \leq n - 1$ but $T^{n}$ does.


# app_k_283 [app_k_283]


K.283. [12.1, P] 

(i) Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space which has the Bolzano-Weierstrass property. Suppose $f:X\to X$ is expansive in the sense that

$$
d (f (x), f (y)) \geq d (x, y)
$$

for all $x, y \in X$. (Note that we do not assume that $f$ is continuous.) If $a, b \in X$, we write $a_0 = a$ and $a_{n+1} = f(a_n)$ and define a sequence $b_n$ similarly. Show that we can find a sequence of integers $n(1) < n(2) < \ldots$ such that $d(a_{n(k)}, a_0) \to 0$ and $d(b_{n(k)}, b_0) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. Deduce that $d(a, b) = d(f(a), f(b))$ for all $a, b \in X$ (that is, $f$ is an isometry).

Find a complete metric space $(Y,\rho)$ and maps $g,h:Y\to Y$ such that

$$
\rho (g (x), g (y)) \geq 2 \rho (x, y), \quad \rho (h (x), h (y)) \geq 2 \rho (x, y)
$$

and $g$ is continuous but $h$ is not.

(ii) Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space with the Bolzano-Weierstrass property. Suppose $f:X\to X$ is an isometry in the sense that

$$
d (f (x), f (y)) = d (x, y)
$$

for all $x, y \in X$. If $a \in X$, we write $a_0 = a$ and $a_{n+1} = f(a_n)$. Show (as in (i)) that we can find a sequence of integers $n(1) < n(2) < \ldots$ such that $d(a_{n(k)}, a_0) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, and deduce that $f$ is bijective.

Find a complete metric space $(Y,\rho)$ and a map $g:Y\to Y$ such that

$$
\rho (g (x), g (y)) = \rho (x, y)
$$

for all $x, y \in Y$, but $g$ is not surjective.


# app_k_284 [app_k_284]


K.284. [12.1, P, ↑] Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space which has the Bolzano-Weierstrass property, and $(Y,\rho)$ a metric space. Suppose $f:X\to Y$ and $g:Y\to X$ are such that $\rho (f(x),f(x'))\geq d(x,x')$ for all $x$, $x'\in X$ and $d(g(y),g(y'))\geq \rho (y,y')$ for all $y$, $y'\in Y$. By considering $g\circ f$ and using Exercise K.283, show that $\rho (f(x),f(x')) = d(x,x')$ for all $x$, $x'\in X$, $d(g(y),g(y')) = \rho (y,y')$ for all $y$, $y'\in Y$, and $f$ and $g$ are bijective. Is it necessarily true that $f$ and $g$ are inverse functions?

Give an example of two complete metric spaces $(X,d)$ and $(Y,\rho)$ having the Bolzano-Weierstrass property and functions $f:X\to Y$ and $g:Y\to X$ such that $f$ is bijective, $f$ and $f^{-1}$ are continuous but $\rho (f(x),f(x'))\geq 2d(x,x')$ and $g$ is an isometry but not bijective.


# app_k_285 [app_k_285]


K.285. [12.1, P] Let $(V, \| \cdot \|)$ be a complete normed space. We say that a subset $A \subseteq V$ is convex if whenever $\mathbf{a}$, $\mathbf{b} \in A$ and $0 \leq \lambda \leq 1$ we have

$$
\lambda {\mathbf {a}} + (1 - \lambda) {\mathbf {b}} \in A.
$$

Suppose that $A$ is a closed bounded convex subset of $V$ and ${\mathbf{f}}: A \to A$ satisfies

$$
\left\| {\mathbf {f}} ({\mathbf {a}}) - {\mathbf {f}} ({\mathbf {b}}) \right\| \leq \left\| {\mathbf {a}} - {\mathbf {b}} \right\|
$$

for all $\mathbf{a}$, $\mathbf{b} \in A$. By considering

$$
{\mathbf {g}} ({\mathbf {x}}) = (1 - \epsilon) {\mathbf {f}} ({\mathbf {x}}) + \epsilon {\mathbf {a}} _ {0}
$$

for some $\mathbf{a}_0\in A$ and $\epsilon >0$, show that

$$
\inf  \left\{\| {\mathbf {f}} ({\mathbf {a}}) - {\mathbf {a}} \|: {\mathbf {a}} \in A \right\} = 0,
$$

and deduce that $\mathbf{f}$ has a fixed point in $A$.

Give an example to show that the conclusion may be false if any one of the three conditions: convex, closed or bounded is omitted.

The following result is useful in the theory of Markov chains (see Exercise K.287). Suppose that $p_{ij} \geq 0$ for $1 \leq i, j \leq n$ and $\sum_{j=1}^{n} p_{ij} = 1$ for $1 \leq i \leq n$. Give $\mathbb{R}^n$ the norm $\| \cdot \|_1$, where $\| \mathbf{x} \|_1 = \sum_{j=1}^{n} |x_j|$. By choosing an appropriate $A$ and $\mathbf{f}$, show that we can find $\pi_i \geq 0$ for $1 \leq i \leq n$ with $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \pi_i = 1$ such that

$$
\sum_ {i = 1} ^ {n} \pi_ {i} p _ {i j} = \pi_ {j}.
$$


# app_k_286 [app_k_286]


K.286. [12.1, P] Throughout this exercise $(X,d)$ is a non-empty metric space with the Bolzano-Weierstrass property, and $T:X\to X$ is a map such that $d(Tx,Ty)\leq d(x,y)$ and such that, given any $x,y\in X$ with $x\neq y$, we can find a strictly positive integer $N(x,y)$ such that $d(T^{N(x,y)}x,T^{N(x,y)}y) < d(x,y)$.

(i) Show that the map $S: X \to \mathbb{R}$ given by $Sx = d(x, Tx)$ is continuous. Explain why this means that there is a $z \in X$ such that $d(z, Tz) \leq d(Tx, x)$ for all $x \in X$. Show that, in fact, $z = Tz$. Show that $z$ is the unique fixed point of $T$.

(ii) Let $\epsilon > 0$ and write

$$
U _ {n} = \{x \in X: d (T ^ {n} x, z) <   \epsilon \}.
$$

Show that $U_{n} \subseteq U_{n + 1}$ and that $U_{n}$ is open for all $n \geq 1$. Write $U = \bigcup_{n = 1}^{\infty} U_{n}$ and $F = X \setminus U$. Why does $F$ have the Bolzano-Weierstrass property? Show that $Tx \in F$ whenever $x \in F$. Use part (i) to show that, if $F$ were nonempty, there would exist a $w \in F$ with $Tw = w$. Deduce that $F$ is indeed empty.

(iii) Show that, if $x \in X$, then $d(T^n x, z) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.


# Finite Markov chains [app_k_287]


K.287. (Finite Markov chains.) [12.1, T!, ↑] In a finite Markov chain with states $1, 2, \ldots, m$, if the system is in state $i$ at time $n$ it will be in state $j$ at time $n + 1$ with probability $p_{ij}$ irrespective of any earlier history. Thus, if it is in state $i$ with probability $q_i$ at time $n$ and in state $j$ with probability $q_j'$ at time $n + 1$, we have

$$
q _ {j} ^ {′} = \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {m} q _ {i} p _ {i j}.
$$

The reader may ignore the previous paragraph, but it explains why we are interested in the space

$$
X = \left\{{\mathbf {q}} \in \mathbb {R} ^ {m}: \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {m} q _ {i} = 1, q _ {s} \geq 0 [ 1 \leq s \leq m ] \right\}
$$

and the map $T: X \to \mathbb{R}^m$ given by $T{\mathbf{q}} = {\mathbf{q}}'$, where $q_j' = \sum_{i=1}^{m} q_i p_{ij}$ for all $1 \leq j \leq m$. Verify that, if, as we shall do from now on, we take $p_{ij} \geq 0$ for all $1 \leq i, j \leq m$ and $\sum_{j=1}^{m} p_{ij} = 1$ for all $1 \leq i \leq m$, then $T{\mathbf{q}} \in X$ for all ${\mathbf{q}} \in X$, and so we may consider $T$ as a map from $X$ to $X$.

Finally we have to choose a metric $d$. In most of this book the particular choice of metric has not been important, but here it is. We take

$$
d ({\mathbf {u}}, {\mathbf {v}}) = \| {\mathbf {u}} - {\mathbf {v}} \| _ {1} = \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {m} | u _ {i} - v _ {i} |
$$

for all ${\mathbf{u}}$, ${\mathbf{v}} \in X$.

(i) Explain why $(X,d)$ has the Bolzano-Weierstrass property.

(ii) Show that $d(T{\mathbf{u}},T{\mathbf{v}})\leq d({\mathbf{u}},{\mathbf{v}})$ for all ${\mathbf{u}}$, ${\mathbf{v}}\in X$.

(iii) Show that, if $n \geq 1$, then

$$
\left(T ^ {n} {\mathbf {q}}\right) _ {j} = \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {m} q _ {i} p _ {i j} ^ {(n)}
$$

for all $1 \leq j \leq m$, where $p_{ij}^{(n)} \geq 0$ for all $1 \leq i, j \leq m$ and $\sum_{j=1}^{m} p_{ij}^{(n)} = 1$ for all $1 \leq i \leq m$. If you know some probability, prove this both by an algebraic and a probabilistic argument.

(iv) Show that, if

$$p_{ij}^{(N)} > 0 \text{ for all } 1\leq i, j\leq m$$

for some $N\geq 1$, then $d(T^{N}{\mathbf{u}},T^{N}{\mathbf{v}}) < d({\mathbf{u}},{\mathbf{v}})$ for all ${\mathbf{u}},{\mathbf{v}}\in X$ with ${\mathbf{u}}\neq {\mathbf{v}}$.

(v) Conclude, using Exercise K.286, that, provided only that condition (iv) holds for some $N \geq 1$, there is a unique $\boldsymbol{\pi} \in X$ such that $T\boldsymbol{\pi} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$ and that, if ${\mathbf{q}} \in X$, then $d(T^n{\mathbf{q}}, \boldsymbol{\pi}) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. If the associated Markov chain has been running for a long time, what can you say about the probability that it is in state $i$?

(vi) Suppose that $m = 2$, $p_{12} = p_{21} = 1$ and $p_{11} = p_{22} = 0$. Show that there is a unique $\boldsymbol{\pi} \in X$ such that $T\boldsymbol{\pi} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$. For which ${\mathbf{q}} \in X$ is it true that $d(T^{n}{\mathbf{q}},\boldsymbol{\pi}) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$? Why does your result not contradict (v)?

(vii) Suppose that $m = 2$, $p_{12} = p_{21} = 0$ and $p_{11} = p_{22} = 1$. Find all the $\boldsymbol{\pi} \in X$ such that $T\boldsymbol{\pi} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$. What can you say about the behaviour of $T^n{\mathbf{q}}$ as $n \to \infty$ for each ${\mathbf{q}} \in X$? Why does your result not contradict (v)?

(viii) Suppose that $m = 4$ and that $p_{ij} = 1/2$ if $1 \leq i, j \leq 2$ or $3 \leq i, j \leq 4$ and that $p_{ij} = 0$ otherwise. Find all the $\boldsymbol{\pi} \in X$ such that $T\boldsymbol{\pi} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$. What can you say about the behaviour of $T^n{\mathbf{q}}$ as $n \to \infty$ for each ${\mathbf{q}} \in X$?

(ix) Note that, by Exercise K.285, the equation $T\boldsymbol{\pi} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$ always has a solution in $X$ even if the condition in (iv) does not hold.


# Countable Markov chains [app_k_288]


K.288. (Countable Markov chains.) [12.1, T!] In this exercise and the next we consider Markov chains with an infinite number of states $1, 2, \ldots$ In algebraic terms we consider a collection of positive numbers $p_{ij}$ $[1 \leq i, j]$ such that $\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} p_{ij} = 1$ for each $i \geq 1$.

(i) Consider $l^1$, the space of real sequences whose sum is absolutely convergent, with norm $\| \cdot \|_1$ given by $\| \mathbf{x} \|_1 = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} |x_n|$. Show that, if we write $(T\mathbf{x})_j = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} x_i p_{ij}$, then $T$ is a well defined continuous linear map from $l^1$ to $l^1$ with operator norm $\| T \| = 1$. Show further that, if we write

$$
X = \left\{{\mathbf {q}} \in l ^ {1}: \| {\mathbf {q}} \| _ {1} = 1 \text { and } q _ {i} \geq 0 \text { for all } i \geq 1 \right\},
$$

then $T{\mathbf{q}} \in X$ whenever ${\mathbf{q}} \in X$. (Note that you are manipulating infinite sums and you must justify each step carefully. Results like Lemma 5.31 may be useful.) We write $(T^n{\mathbf{x}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} x_i p_{ij}^{(n)}$.

(ii) We shall only be interested in systems satisfying the following extension. If $k \geq 1$, there exists an $N(k)$ such that $p_{ij}^{(N(k))} > 0$ for all $i, j \leq k$.

Show that, under this condition, given any ${\mathbf{u}},{\mathbf{v}}\in X$ with ${\mathbf{u}}\neq {\mathbf{v}}$, we can find an $N\geq 1$ such that $d(T^{N}{\mathbf{u}},T^{N}{\mathbf{v}}) < d({\mathbf{u}},{\mathbf{v}})$.

(iii) Show, under the condition above, that, if the equation $T\boldsymbol{\pi} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$ has a solution $\boldsymbol{\pi} \in X$, then it is unique. Let

$$
Y = \{{\mathbf {h}} \in l ^ {1}: h _ {i} \geq 0 \text { for all } i \geq 1 \}.
$$

Find all the solutions of the equation $T{\mathbf{h}} = {\mathbf{h}}$ with ${\mathbf{h}} \in Y$, first under the assumption that the equation $T\boldsymbol{\pi} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$ has a solution $\boldsymbol{\pi} \in X$, and second under the assumption that it does not.

(iv) Show, under the condition above, that, if the equation $T\boldsymbol{\pi} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$ has a solution $\boldsymbol{\pi} \in X$, then $\pi_i > 0$ for all $i$.

(v) Consider the following systems. In each case show we have a Markov chain satisfying the condition above. By solving the appropriate difference equations, or otherwise, show that in case (a) the equation $T\boldsymbol{\pi} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$ has a solution $\boldsymbol{\pi} \in X$, but in cases (b) and (c) it does not.

(a) $p_{12} = 1$; $p_{i i - 1} = 1 / 2$, $p_{i i} = p_{i i + 1} = 1 / 4$ for $i \geq 2$; $p_{i j} = 0$ otherwise.

(b) $p_{12} = 1$; $p_{i i + 1} = 1 / 2$, $p_{i i} = p_{i i - 1} = 1 / 4$ for $i \geq 2$; $p_{i j} = 0$ otherwise.

(c) $p_{12} = 1$; $p_{i i - 1} = p_{i i} = p_{i i + 1} = 1 / 3$ for $i \geq 2$; $p_{i j} = 0$ otherwise.


# app_k_289 [app_k_289]


K.289. [12.1, T!, ↑↑] This exercise continues the previous one. We assume that the condition above holds and that the equation $T\boldsymbol{\pi} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$ has a solution $\boldsymbol{\pi} \in X$. We will need part (ii) of Exercise K.218.

(i) Let $J \geq 1$ be fixed. Show, using part (iv) of question K.288, that we can find an ${\mathbf{h}} \in Y$ such that $T{\mathbf{h}} = {\mathbf{h}}$ and $h_J = 1$.

(ii) We continue with the notation of (i). Explain why $X$ does not have the Bolzano-Weierstrass property but

$$
X _ {J} = \left\{{\mathbf {q}} \in X: h _ {i} \geq q _ {i} \text { for all } i \geq 1 \right\}
$$

does. Explain why $X_J$ is non-empty and show, carefully, that $T{\mathbf{q}} \in X_J$ whenever ${\mathbf{q}} \in X_J$. Deduce that, if ${\mathbf{q}} \in X_J$, then $\| T^n{\mathbf{q}} - \boldsymbol{\pi}\|_1 \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

(iii) Let ${\mathbf{e}}(p)\in l^1$ be given by ${\mathbf{e}}(p)_p = 1$, ${\mathbf{e}}(p)_i = 0$ otherwise. Explain why $\| T^n{\mathbf{e}}(p) - \boldsymbol{\pi}\| _1\to 0$ as $n\to \infty$. Deduce further that, if ${\mathbf{q}}\in X$, then $\| T^n{\mathbf{q}} - \boldsymbol{\pi}\| _1\to 0$. Interpret this result probabilistically.


# app_k_290 [app_k_290]


K.290. [12.2, P] Let $(X,d)$ be a complete metric space and $T:X\to X$ a mapping such that $T^n$ is a contraction mapping. Show that $T$ has a unique fixed point.

If you have done Exercise K.282, explain why the example asked for in the last paragraph of that exercise does not contradict the result of the first paragraph of this exercise.

Consider the space $C([a, b])$ with the uniform norm. Let $\phi$ be a continuous real-valued function on $\mathbb{R} \times [a, b]$ which satisfies the Lipschitz condition

$$
\left| \phi (x, t) - \phi (y, t) \right| \leq M | x - y | \text { for } x, y \in \mathbb {R} \text { and } t \in [ a, b ],
$$

and let $g \in C([a, b])$. Define $T: C([a, b]) \to C([a, b])$ by

$$(T h) (t) = g (t) + \int_ {a} ^ {t} \phi (h (s), s) d s.$$

Show, by induction or otherwise, that

$$
\left| \left(T ^ {n} h\right) (t) - \left(T ^ {n} k\right) (t) \right| _{∞} \leq \frac {1}{n !} M ^ {n} (t - a) ^ {n} \| h - k \| _ {∞}
$$

for all $h$, $k \in C[a, b]$ and $t \in [a, b]$.

Show that $T$ has a fixed point. If $g$ is constant, comment briefly on the associated theorem on the existence of solutions of a certain differential equation.


# app_k_291 [app_k_291]


K.291. [12.2, P, S] Suppose that $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable with $|f'(x)| < M$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$. Show that the system of equations

$$
x _ {i} = \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {n} a _ {i j} f (x _ {j}) + b _ {i} [ 1 \leq i \leq n ]
$$

with $a_{ij}$ and $b_{i}$ real has a unique solution provided that

$$
\sum_ {i = 1} ^ {n} \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {n} a _ {i j} ^ {2} <   \frac {1}{M ^ {2}}.
$$


# app_k_292 [app_k_292]


K.292. [12.2, P, S] Consider the differential equation for a function $y: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$

$$y ^ {′} (t) = | y (t) | ^ {α}, \quad y (0) = 0,$$

where $\alpha$ is real and strictly positive. For which values of $\alpha$ does this equation have only one solution and for which values of $\alpha$ does it have more than one? If it has only one solution, prove this. If it has more than one solution, give at least two solutions explicitly.


# app_k_293 [app_k_293]


K.293. [12.2, P] Dieudonné is associated in most mathematicians' minds with the abstract approach of Bourbaki and his own text *Foundations of Modern Analysis* [14]. He may have had this in mind when he wrote the determinedly concrete *Infinitesimal Calculus* [15], from which this exercise is taken.

(i) Suppose $f: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ is a continuous function with $f(t, u) < 0$ when $tu > 0$ and $f(t, u) > 0$ when $tu < 0$. Explain why $f(0, t) = f(u, 0) = 0$ for all $t$ and $u$. If $y: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a differentiable function with

$$y ^ {′} (t) = f (t, y (t)) \text { for all } t \in \mathbb {R}, \quad y (0) = 0,$$

show, by considering the behaviour of $y$ at a maximum and a minimum in each interval $[0,c]$ with $c > 0$, or otherwise, that $y(t) = 0$ for all $t \geq 0$. Show that $y(t) = 0$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, and so the equation has a unique solution.

(ii) Now define

$$f (t, u) = \begin{cases}
- 2 t & \text{if } u \geq t ^ {2}, \\
- 2 u / t & \text{if } t ^ {2} > u > - t ^ {2}, \\
2 t & \text{if } - t ^ {2} \geq u.
\end{cases}$$

Verify that $f$ is well defined and continuous everywhere and satisfies the hypotheses of (i). Now set $y_0(t) = t^2$ and define

$$y _ {n + 1} (t) = \int_ {0} ^ {t} f (s, y _ {n} (s)) d s.
$$

Show that $y_{n}(t)$ fails to converge as $n \to \infty$ for any $t \neq 0$ although, as we know from (i), the equation has a unique solution.


# app_k_294 [app_k_294]


K.294. [12.3, P] (This exercise makes repeated use of the mean value inequality.)

(i) Suppose that $a > 0$, $y:[0,a)\to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable, $y(0)\geq 1$ and

$$y ^ {′} (t) \geq | y (t) | ^ {β}
$$

with $\beta > 1$. Show that $y(t)$ is strictly increasing for $t \geq 0$ and $y(t) \geq t + 1$ for $a > t \geq 0$. Let $t_j$ be defined by taking $t_j = 0$ if $y(0) \geq 2^j$, $t_j = a$ if $y(t) < 2^j$ for all $0 \leq t < a$ and $y(t_j) = 2^j$ otherwise. Show that $t_{j+1} - t_j \leq 2^{(1-\beta)j}$, and deduce that $a \leq (1 - 2^{(1-\beta)})^{-1}$.

(ii) Deduce the result of Example 12.19.

(iii) Show that there exists a differentiable function $y:[0,\infty)\to \mathbb{R}$ such that $y(0) = e$ and

$$y ^ {′} (t) = y (t) \log y (t).
$$


# Euler's method [app_k_295]


K.295. (Euler's method.) [12.3, T] The next few exercises deal with Euler's method for finding approximate solutions of a differential equation. They were suggested by the typically neat treatment in [46]. You will find the discussion more interesting if you have used Euler's method before or if you try one of the excellent demonstrations which now exist on the Internet.

Suppose that we wish to solve the differential equation $y^′ (t) = f(t,y(t))$, $y(0) = y_{0}$ numerically on a computer. (We shall look at $y(t)$ for $t\geq 0$, but the same ideas apply when $t\leq 0$.) A natural approach is to seek (approximate) solutions at the points $x_{r} = rh$, where the strictly positive number $h$ is called the 'step length' and $r$ is a positive integer. Explain briefly why we expect

$$
y \big ((r + 1) h \big) \approx y (r h) + f (r h, y (r h)) h.
$$

This suggests that our approximation $y_{r}$ to $y(rh)$ could be obtained by solving the system of equations

$$y _ {r + 1} = y _ {r} + f \left(x _ {r}, y _ {r}\right) h. \tag{A}
$$

This is called Euler's method.

Euler's method seems reasonable and works on suitable test examples. In this exercise we investigate its behaviour in general. We shall assume stronger conditions on $f$ than we did in Theorem 12.8 and elsewhere in Sections 12.2 and 12.3. Specifically, we demand that $f: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ is once differentiable and there exist constants $K$ and $L$ such that $|f_{,2}(t,u)| \leq K$ and

$$
\left| f _ {, 1} (t, u) \right| + \left| f _ {, 2} (t, u) f (t, u) \right| \leq L
$$

for all $(t,u)\in \mathbb{R}^2$. Explain briefly why these conditions imply the existence of a unique differentiable $y:\mathbb{R}\to \mathbb{R}$ such that

$$y ^ {′} (t) = f (t, y (t)), \quad y (0) = y _ {0}. \tag{B}
$$

Explain why $y$ is twice differentiable, and express $y''(t)$ in terms of $f(t, y(t))$, $f_{,1}(t, y(t))$ and $f_{,2}(t, y(t))$. By using the 2nd mean value theorem (Exercise K.50), or otherwise, show that

$$
\left| y \big ((n + 1) h \big) - y (n h) - f (n h, y (n h)) h \right| \leq \frac {L h ^ {2}}{2}.
$$

Use this result to show that

$$
\left| y \big ((n + 1) h \big) - y _ {n + 1} \right| \leq (1 + K h) | y (n h) - y _ {n} | + \frac {L h ^ {2}}{2}.
$$

Deduce that

$$
\left| y (N h) - y _ {N} \right| \leq \frac {L h}{2 K} \big ((1 + K h) ^ {N + 1} - 1 \big).
$$

In order to emphasise the dependence on $h$, let us write $y_{n}^{[h]} = y_{n}$. Show that, if the positive number $a$ is fixed and we take $h_N = a / N$, then

$$
\left| y (a) - y _ {N} ^ {[ h _ {N} ]} \right| \leq \frac {L h _ {N}}{2 K} \big ((1 + K h _ {N}) ^ {N + 1} - 1 \big).
$$

Observe (or recall) that, if $x \geq 0$, then

$$
e ^ {x} - \left(1 + \frac {x}{m}\right) ^ {m} \geq \sum_ {r = 0} ^ {m} \left(\frac {1}{r !} - \binom {m} {r} \frac {1}{m ^ {r}}\right) x ^ {r} \geq 0,
$$

and deduce that

$$
\left| y (a) - y _ {N} ^ {[ h _ {N} ]} \right| \leq \frac {L h _ {N}}{2 K} \left(e ^ {a K} - 1\right) \tag{C}
$$

and, in particular, $y_N^{[h_N]} \to y(a)$ as $N \to \infty$.


# app_k_296 [app_k_296]


K.296. [12.3, T, ↑] We continue with the discussion of Exercise K.295. Informally, we may interpret the inequality (C) in Exercise K.295 as saying that 'our error estimate for Euler's method is roughly proportional to the step length'. Inequality (C) is, however, only an upper estimate for the error. Can we get a substantially better estimate? Consider the special case $f(t,u) = u$, $y_0 = 0$ and $a = 1$. Show that $y(t) = e^t$, $y_n = (1 + h)^n$ and that

$$
y (1) - y _ {N} ^ {[ h _ {N} ]} = e - (1 + N ^ {- 1}) ^ {N} 
$$
$$
\geq \sum_ {r = 0} ^ {N} \left(\frac {1}{r !} - \binom {N} {r} \frac {1}{N ^ {r}}\right) \geq \frac {1}{2 N} = \frac {h _ {N}}{2}.
$$

Informally, we may say 'in this case, the error for Euler's method decreases no faster than the step length'.

We see that (roughly speaking) halving the step length in Euler's method will double the amount of calculation and only halve the error. Thus Euler's method is a reasonable one if we want to solve one differential equation to a reasonable degree of accuracy, but unattractive if we want to solve many differential equations to a high degree of accuracy.


# app_k_297 [app_k_297]


K.297. [12.3, T, ↑] We continue with the discussion of Exercises K.295 and K.296. So far we have ignored the fact that computers only work to a certain degree of accuracy. As a simple model to take account of this, we replace equation (A) of Exercise K.295 by

$$
\tilde {y} _ {r + 1} = \tilde {y} _ {r} + f (x _ {r}, \tilde {y} _ {r}) h + \epsilon_ {r}, \tag{D}
$$

where the 'error' $\epsilon_r$ satisfies the condition $|\epsilon_r| \leq \epsilon$ for some fixed $\epsilon > 0$. As before, we impose the initial condition $\tilde{y}_0 = 0$.

(i) As in Exercise K.295, we take $y_{n}^{[h]} = y_{n}$, fix a positive number $a$, and take $h_N = a / N$. Show that

$$
| y (a) - y _ {N} ^ {[ h _ {N} ]} | \leq \left(\frac {L h _ {N}}{2 K} + \frac {\epsilon}{h _ {N} K}\right) (e ^ {a K} - 1).
$$

$$
\mathrm{(E)} \qquad \qquad | y (a) - y _ {N} ^ {[ h _ {N} ]} | \leq \left(\frac {L h _ {N}}{2 K} + \frac {\epsilon}{h _ {N} K}\right) (e ^ {a K} - 1).
$$

What happens to the error estimate (that is, the right hand side of (E)) as $N \to \infty$ (that is, as the step length approaches 0)? Explain in simple terms why we should expect this.

(ii) Which positive value of $h$ minimises $\frac{Lh}{2K} + \frac{\epsilon}{hK}$ ?

(iii) Show that the best bound on the error that we can guarantee using (E) is proportional to $\epsilon^{1/2}$ .

(iv) Just as in Exercise K.296, we ask whether we can improve substantially on (iii). Once again, we consider the special case $f(t,u) = u$ , $y_0 = 0$ and $a = 1$ . A little experimentation shows that we can solve (D) if we choose $\epsilon_r = -(1 - rh)\epsilon$ , giving

$$
\tilde {y} _ {r + 1} = (1 + N ^ {- 1}) \tilde {y} _ {r} - (1 - r N ^ {- 1}) \epsilon . \tag {F}
$$

Show that, for this choice of $\epsilon_r$ , we obtain

$$
\tilde {y} _ {r} = (1 - N ^ {- 1}) ^ {r} - r \epsilon ,
$$

and so

$$
y (1) - \tilde {y} _ {N} ^ {[ h _ {N} ]} = e - \left((1 + N ^ {- 1}) ^ {N} - N \epsilon\right) \geq \frac {h _ {N}}{2} + \frac {\epsilon}{h _ {N}} \geq 2 ^ {1 / 2} \epsilon^ {1 / 2}.
$$

Thus the answer to our question is no.

There exist several different ways of improving on Euler's method, but a good understanding of Euler's method is very helpful in understanding how anybody came to think of these more advanced techniques.


## app_k_298 [app_k_298]


K.298. [12.3, H, ↑] (i) Let $a > 0$ . By considering the differential equation $y'(x) = g(x)$ and using Exercise K.295, show that there exists a constant $\kappa_{a}$ such that, if $g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable, $|g'(t)| \leq M$ for all $t \in [0, a]$ , $N$ is a strictly positive integer and $Nh = a$ , then

$$
\left| \int_ {0} ^ {a} g (t) d t - h \sum_ {n = 0} ^ {N - 1} g (r h) \right| \leq M \kappa_ {a} h.
$$

(It is easy to obtain this result directly with better values for $\kappa_{a}$ . See, for example, Exercise K.140 (v), or just think a bit.)

(ii) Suppose that $a = \pi$ , $Nh = a$ with $N$ a strictly positive integer, and $G(t) = \sin^2 (Nt)$ . Show that

$$
\left| \int_ {0} ^ {a} G (t) d t - h \sum_ {n = 0} ^ {N - 1} G (r h) \right| \geq \sup  _ {t \in [ 0, a ]} | G (t) |.
$$

(iii) Consider the situation described in Exercise K.295. Suppose that we replace the condition $|f_{,1}(t,u)| + |f_{,2}(t,u)f(t,u)| \leq L$ by the weaker condition $|f(t,u)| \leq L$ for all $(t,u) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ . If $a > 0$ and $\epsilon > 0$ are fixed, is it possible to find an $N_0$ (depending only on $K$ , $L$ , $a$ and $\epsilon$ ) such that

$$
| y (a) - y _ {N} ^ {[ h _ {N} ]} | \leq \epsilon
$$

for all $N\geq N_0?$


### app_k_299 [app_k_299]


K.299. (Leibniz rule.) [12.3, G] (i) If $u, v: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ are $n$ times differentiable at $x$ , show, by induction or otherwise, that the product $uv$ is $n$ times differentiable at $x$ with

$$
(u v) ^ {(n)} (x) = \sum_ {r = 0} ^ {n} \binom {n} {r} u ^ {(n - r)} (x) v ^ {(r)} (x).
$$

(This is called the Leibniz rule.)

(ii) If $y:\mathbb{R}\to \mathbb{R}$ is defined by

$$
(1 + x ^ {2}) ^ {1 / 2} y (x) = \log (x + (1 + x ^ {2}) ^ {1 / 2}),
$$

show that $y$ is differentiable on $(0,\infty)$ with

$$
(1 + x ^ {2}) y ^ {\prime} (x) + x y (x) = 1.
$$

Show, by using induction and the Leibniz rule, that $y$ is infinitely differentiable, and find the Taylor series

$$
\sum_ {n = 0} ^ {\infty} \frac {y ^ {(n)} (0)}{n !} x ^ {n}.
$$

(iii) Find the radius of convergence of the Taylor series and, by using results on the differentiation of power series and the uniqueness of the solution of differential equations, show that, within the radius of convergence,

$$
y (x) = \sum_ {n = 0} ^ {\infty} \frac {y ^ {(n)} (0)}{n !} x ^ {n}.
$$


### app_k_300 [app_k_300]


K.300. [12.4, P, S] Let the two functions $K:[0,1]^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ and $g:[0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. Explain why there exists an $M > 0$ such that $|K(s,t)| \leq M$ for all $(s,t) \in [0,1]^2$ . Suppose that $|\lambda| < M^{-1}$ . By finding an appropriate

contraction mapping $T: C([0,1]) \to C([0,1])$ , show that there exists a unique $f \in C([0,1])$ such that

$$
f (t) = g (t) + \lambda \int_ {0} ^ {1} K (s, t) f (s) d s.
$$


### app_k_301 [app_k_301]


K.301. (The Wronskian.) [12.4, M] This should be treated as an exercise in calculus rather than analysis. We write

$$
\left| \begin{array}{c c c} a _ {1 1} & a _ {1 2} & a _ {1 3} \\ a _ {2 1} & a _ {2 2} & a _ {2 3} \\ a _ {3 1} & a _ {3 2} & a _ {3 3} \end{array} \right| = \det  \left( \begin{array}{c c c} a _ {1 1} & a _ {1 2} & a _ {1 3} \\ a _ {2 1} & a _ {2 2} & a _ {2 3} \\ a _ {3 1} & a _ {3 2} & a _ {3 3} \end{array} \right).
$$

(i) If $u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{3}$ are all differentiable, show that

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {d}{d t} \left| \begin{array}{c c c} u _ {1} (t) & u _ {2} (t) & u _ {3} (t) \\ v _ {1} (t) & v _ {2} (t) & v _ {3} (t) \\ w _ {1} (t) & w _ {2} (t) & w _ {3} (t) \end{array} \right| \\ = \left| \begin{array}{c c c} u _ {1} ^ {\prime} (t) & u _ {2} ^ {\prime} (t) & u _ {3} ^ {\prime} (t) \\ v _ {1} (t) & v _ {2} (t) & v _ {3} (t) \\ w _ {1} (t) & w _ {2} (t) & w _ {3} (t) \end{array} \right| + \left| \begin{array}{c c c} u _ {1} (t) & u _ {2} (t) & u _ {3} (t) \\ v _ {1} ^ {\prime} (t) & v _ {2} ^ {\prime} (t) & v _ {3} ^ {\prime} (t) \\ w _ {1} (t) & w _ {2} (t) & w _ {3} (t) \end{array} \right| + \left| \begin{array}{c c c} u _ {1} (t) & u _ {2} (t) & u _ {3} (t) \\ v _ {1} (t) & v _ {2} (t) & v _ {3} (t) \\ w _ {1} ^ {\prime} (t) & w _ {2} ^ {\prime} (t) & w _ {3} ^ {\prime} (t) \end{array} \right|. \\ \\end{array}
$$

(ii) If $u_{1}$ , $u_{2}$ and $u_{3}$ are three solutions of

$$
y ^ {\prime \prime \prime} (t) + a (t) y ^ {\prime \prime} (t) + b (t) y ^ {\prime} (t) + c (t) y (t) = 0,
$$

we define their Wronskian $W$ by

$$
W (t) = \left| \begin{array}{c c c} u _ {1} (t) & u _ {2} (t) & u _ {3} (t) \\ u _ {1} ^ {\prime} (t) & u _ {2} ^ {\prime} (t) & u _ {3} ^ {\prime} (t) \\ u _ {1} ^ {\prime \prime} (t) & u _ {2} ^ {\prime \prime} (t) & u _ {3} ^ {\prime \prime} (t) \end{array} \right|.
$$

Use part (i) and results about determinants to show that

$$
W ^ {\prime} (t) = - a (t) W (t).
$$

(iii) Generalise parts (i) and (ii). Reread the proof of Lemma 12.28 (i) in the light of this exercise.


### app_k_302 [app_k_302]


K.302. [12.4, T, S] The functions $f, g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ are everywhere differentiable and their Wronskian $f'g - g'f$ never vanishes. By applying Rolle's theorem to $f/g$ , or otherwise, show that if $f$ has zeros at $a$ and $b$ with $a < b$ , then $g$ must have a zero strictly between $a$ and $b$ . Deduce that 'the zeros of $f$ and $g$ are interlaced'.


### app_k_303 [app_k_303]


K.303. [12.4, P] (i) If $f_1, f_2 : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ are once differentiable, we define the Wronskian $W(f_1, f_2)$ by

$$
W (f _ {1}, f _ {2}) = \det  \left( \begin{array}{c c} f _ {1} & f _ {2} \\ f _ {1} ^ {\prime} & f _ {2} ^ {\prime} \end{array} \right)
$$

Show that, if $f_{1}$ and $f_{2}$ are linearly dependent (that is, we can find $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2} \in \mathbb{R}$ , not both zero such that $\lambda_{1}f_{1}(t) + \lambda_{2}f_{2}(t) = 0$ for all $t \in [a,b]$ ), then the Wronskian $W(f_{1},f_{2})$ is identically zero. By considering functions of the type $g$ with $g(x) = 0$ for $x \leq c$ , $g(x) = (x - c)^{2}$ for $x \geq c$ , or otherwise, show that the converse is false.

(ii) Suppose now that $f_{1}, f_{2}$ are twice continuously differentiable. Show, by considering the Wronskian

$$
W (f, f _ {1}, f _ {2}) = \det  \left( \begin{array}{c c c} f & f _ {1} & f _ {2} \\ f ^ {\prime} & f _ {1} ^ {\prime} & f _ {2} ^ {\prime} \\ f ^ {\prime \prime} & f _ {1} ^ {\prime \prime} & f _ {2} ^ {\prime \prime} \end{array} \right)
$$

and using results on the existence and uniqueness of solutions of differential equations with given initial conditions, that the following result holds.

There exists a differential equation of the form

$$
y ^ {\prime \prime} (x) + a _ {1} (x) y ^ {\prime} (x) + a _ {2} (x) y (x) = 0
$$

whose solutions are exactly the functions of the form $\lambda_1 f_1 + \lambda_2 f_2$ with $\lambda_1, \lambda_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ (in more sophisticated language, having $f_1$ and $f_2$ as a basis for the space of solutions) if and only if $W(f_1, f_2)$ is non-zero everywhere on $[a, b]$ .

Generalise the results of this exercise to $n$ functions $f_{1}, f_{2}, \ldots, f_{n}$ .


### app_k_304 [app_k_304]


K.304. (Method of reduction of order.) [12.4, M]

(This exercise should be treated as a mathematical methods one.)

(i) Consider a differential equation

$$
y ^ {\prime \prime} (x) + p (x) y ^ {\prime} (x) + q (x) y (x) = 0.
$$

Suppose that $u$ is a solution of this equation. Show that, if we write $y(x) = u(x)v(x)$ and $w(x) = u'(x)$ , then the equation takes the form of a first order differential equation in $w$ . Explain why we would expect $y(x) = (A + B\int_0^x w(t)dt)u(x)$ with $w$ a solution of the first order equation to give the general solution.

(ii) Show that $y(x) = \exp x$ gives a solution of

$$
y ^ {\prime \prime} (x) - (2 x + 1) y ^ {\prime} (x) + 2 (x + 1) y (x) = 0,
$$

and use the method of (i) to find the general solution.

(iii) We know that $y(x) = \exp x$ gives a solution of

$$
y ^ {\prime \prime} (x) - 2 y ^ {\prime} (x) + y (x) = 0.
$$

Find the general solution by the method of (i).

(iv) Show how, if we know one solution of

$$
y ^ {\prime \prime \prime} (x) + p _ {1} (x) y ^ {\prime \prime} (x) + p _ {2} (x) y ^ {\prime} (x) + p _ {3} (x) y (x) = 0,
$$

we can reduce the problem of finding a general solution to that of solving a second order linear differential equation. Generalise.


### app_k_305 [app_k_305]


K.305. (Method of variation of parameters.) [12.4, M, ↑]

(Like Exercise K.304, this exercise should be treated as a mathematical methods one.)

Consider a differential equation

$$
y ^ {\prime \prime} (x) + p (x) y ^ {\prime} (x) + q (x) y (x) = 0.
$$

Let $y_{1}$ and $y_{2}$ be solutions whose Wronskian $W(x) = y_1'(x)y_2(x) - y_1(x)y_2'(x)$ never vanishes. Suppose that we wish to solve the differential equation

$$
y ^ {\prime \prime} (x) + p (x) y ^ {\prime} (x) + q (x) y (x) = f (x).
$$

(i) In view of the success of the method of Exercise K.304 we might be tempted to look for a solution of the form

$$
y (x) = u _ {1} (x) y _ {1} (x) + u _ {2} (x) y _ {2} (x).
$$

Make this substitution in the second equation.

(ii) At the end of (i), you obtained a differential equation for $u_{1}$ and $u_{2}$ . It is a useful heuristic guide (though not always a reliable one) that two unknowns require two equations, so we add the further equation

$$
u _ {1} ^ {\prime} (x) y _ {1} (x) + u _ {2} ^ {\prime} (x) y _ {2} (x) = 0.
$$

Use this equation to simplify the one from (i), and then use the pair of equations to find $u_1'$ and $u_2'$ . Now find the general solution of the second equation.

(iii) Discuss the relation of your result to the Green's function result given in Theorem 12.31.

(iv) Use the method of this exercise to find the general solution of

$$
y ^ {\prime \prime} (x) - y (x) = \frac {2}{1 + e ^ {x}}.
$$

(v) Try and extend the ideas of this exercise to the solution of

$$
y ^ {\prime \prime \prime} (x) + p (x) y ^ {\prime \prime} (x) + q (x) y ^ {\prime} (x) + r (x) y = f (x)
$$

when three solutions $y_{1}, y_{2}, y_{3}$ with non-zero Wronskian (see Exercise K.301) are known.

[It would be hard to think of an example more opposed to the modern view that 'understanding precedes doing'. But even the most convinced proponent of modern ways must admit that it has a certain charm.]


### app_k_306 [app_k_306]


K.306. [12.4, P] Let $C(S)$ be the space of continuous functions on the unit square

$$
S = \{(x, y): 0 \leq x \leq 1, 0 \leq y \leq 1 \}
$$

equipped with the uniform norm $\| K\|_{\infty} = \sup_{(x,y)\in S}|K(x,y)|$ , and let $C(I)$ be the space of continuous functions on the unit interval $I = [0,1]$ equipped with the uniform norm. Let $\mathcal{L}$ be the space of continuous linear maps $L: C(I)\to C(I)$ equipped with the operator norm. If $K\in C(S)$ we set

$$
\left(T _ {K} (f)\right) (x) = \int_ {0} ^ {1} K (x, y) f (y) d y.
$$

Show that $T_K \in \mathcal{L}$ .

Prove or disprove each of the following statements.

(i) If $K_{n}\to K$ in $C(S)$ , then $T_{K_n}\rightarrow T_K$ in $\mathcal{L}$ .

(ii) If $T_{K_n} \to T_K$ in $\mathcal{L}$ , then $K_n \to K$ in $C(S)$ .

(iii) The mapping $K\mapsto T_K$ is an injective map from $C(S)$ to $\mathcal{L}$ .

(iv) The mapping $K\mapsto T_K$ is a surjective map from $C(S)$ to $\mathcal{L}$ .

(v) If there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that $K(x, y) \geq \delta$ for all $(x, y) \in S$ , then $K \mapsto T_K$ is injective.

(vi) If there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that $K(x, y) \geq \delta$ for all $(x, y) \in S$ , then $K \mapsto T_K$ is surjective.


### app_k_307 [app_k_307]


K.307. [12.4, T] (i) In Exercise K.147, you showed that if $g: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ is a differentiable function with continuous partial derivatives, then

$$
G (x) = \int_ {0} ^ {x} g (x, t) d t
$$

is differentiable with

$$
G ^ {\prime} (x) = g (x, x) + \int_ {0} ^ {x} g _ {, 1} (x, t) d t.
$$

Review this exercise.

(ii) Suppose that $a, b, f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ are continuous. Explain why there exists a unique twice differentiable $y_1: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that

$$
y _ {1} ^ {\prime \prime} (t) + a (t) y _ {1} ^ {\prime} (t) + b (t) y _ {1} (t) = 0, y _ {1} (0) = 0, y _ {1} ^ {\prime} (0) = 1,
$$

and a unique twice differentiable $y_{2}:\mathbb{R}\to \mathbb{R}$ such that

$$
y _ {2} ^ {\prime \prime} (t) + a (t) y _ {2} ^ {\prime} (t) + b (t) y _ {2} (t) = 0, y _ {2} (1) = 0, y _ {2} ^ {\prime} (1) = 1.
$$

We make the key assumption that $y _ {1} (1) \neq 0$.

Show that, if we define $H$ , $\tilde{H}:\mathbb{R}^2\to \mathbb{R}$ by $W = y_{1}y_{2}^{\prime} - y_{1}^{\prime}y_{2}$ and

$$
H (s, t) = y _ {1} (t) y _ {2} (s) W (s) ^ {- 1}, \quad \tilde {H} (s, t) = y _ {2} (t) y _ {1} (s) W (s) ^ {- 1},
$$

then $H$ and $\tilde{H}$ are twice differentiable functions with continuous second partial derivatives and

$$
H _ {, 2 2} (s, t) + a (t) H _ {, 2} (s, t) + b (t) H (s, t) = 0, \quad H (s, 0) = 0,
$$

$$
\tilde {H} _ {, 2 2} (s, t) + a (t) \tilde {H} _ {, 2} (s, t) + b (t) \tilde {H} (s, t) = 0, \quad \tilde {H} (s, 1) = 0.
$$

Check that $H(t,t) = \tilde{H}(t,t)$ , $\tilde{H}_{,2}(t,t) - H_{,2}(t,t) = 1$ .

(iii) We define $G: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ by $G(s, t) = H(s, t)$ for $t \leq s$ and $G(s, t) = \tilde{H}(s, t)$ for $s < t$ . If we set

$$
y (t) = \int_ {0} ^ {1} G (s, t) f (s) d s = \int_ {0} ^ {t} H (s, t) f (s) d s + \int_ {t} ^ {1} \tilde {H} (s, t) f (s) d s,
$$

show, using part (i) and the properties of $H$ and $\tilde{H}$ established in part (ii), that $y$ is twice differentiable and satisfies

$$
y ^ {\prime \prime} (t) + a (t) y ^ {\prime} + b (t) y = f (t)
$$

together with the conditions $y(0) = y(1) = 0$ .


### app_k_308 [app_k_308]


K.308. [12.4, M] Consider the differential equation

$$
y ^ {(4)} - k ^ {4} y = f
$$

for $y:[0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ subject to the boundary conditions $y(0) = y'(0) = 0$ , $y(1) = y'(1) = 0$ , where $f:[0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $k$ is real.

By extending the discussion of the Green's function in Section 12.4, show that, provided that $k$ does not take certain exceptional values to be identified, the system has the solution

$$
y (x) = \int_ {0} ^ {1} G (x, t) f (t) d t,
$$

where

$$
G (x, t) =
\begin{cases}
A (\sinh k x - \sin k x) + B (\cosh k x - \cos k x) & \text{if } 0 \leq x \leq t, \\
C (\sinh k (1 - x) - \sin k (1 - x)) + D (\cosh k (1 - x) - \cos k (1 - x)) & \text{if } t < x \leq 1,
\end{cases}
$$

and $A, B, C, D$ are given by

$$
\begin{pmatrix} A \\ B \\ C \\ D \end{pmatrix} = M ^ {- 1} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix},
$$

where $M$ is a $4 \times 4$ matrix to be given explicitly.


### app_k_309 [app_k_309]


K.309. [12.4, M] Consider the equation for a damped harmonic oscillator

$$
y ^ {\prime \prime} (t) + 2 \beta y ^ {\prime} (t) + \omega^ {2} y (t) = f (t),
$$

where $\beta$ and $\omega$ are strictly positive real numbers and $y:[0,\infty)\to \mathbb{R}$ satisfies $y(0) = y'(0) = 0$ . Use Green's function methods to show that, if $\beta >\omega$ ,

$$
y (t) = \alpha^ {- 1} \int_ {0} ^ {t} f (s) e ^ {- \beta (t - s)} \sinh (\alpha (t - s)) d s,
$$

where $\alpha$ is the positive square root of $\beta^2 - \omega^2$ , and obtain a similar result in the cases when $\omega > \beta$ and $\omega = \beta$ .

Suppose that the driving force $f$ is non-zero only when $t$ is very small. Sketch the behaviour of $y(t)$ for large $t$ . If $y$ represents the position of a pointer, we would like $y(t)$ to remain fairly close to zero after a fairly short time. Explain why it is not a good idea to take $\beta$ very small or very large. What rough value of $\beta$ would you choose? (You may feel this is an improper question, but it is one engineers have to answer every day.)


### app_k_310 [app_k_310]


K.310. [13.1, T] (Although this sequence of exercises seems to find a natural place here, it could have been placed earlier and linked with Section 11.1.) Let $(U, \| \cdot \|_U)$ be a complete normed vector space. By Exercise 11.15, the space $\mathcal{L}(U, U)$ of continuous linear maps with the operator norm $\| \cdot \|$ is complete.

(i) If $T \in \mathcal{L}(U, U)$ and $\| T \| < 1$ , show that the sequence $S_{n} = \sum_{j=0}^{n} T^{j}$ is Cauchy. Deduce that $S_{n}$ converges in the operator norm to a limit $S = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} T^{j}$ . Show also that $\| S \| \leq (1 - \| T \|)^{-1}$ and $\| I - S \| \leq \| T \| (1 - \| T \|)^{-1}$ .

(ii) We continue with the notation and assumptions of (i). By looking at $S_{n}(I - T)$ , show that $S(I - T) = I$ . Show also that $(I - T)S = I$ . Conclude that, if $A \in \mathcal{L}(U,U)$ and $\| I - A\| < 1$ , then $A$ is invertible and

$$
\| A ^ {- 1} \| \leq (1 - \| I - A \|) ^ {- 1} \quad \text{and} \quad \| I - A ^ {- 1} \| \leq \| I - A \| (1 - \| I - A \|) ^ {- 1}.
$$

(iii) Suppose that $B \in \mathcal{L}(U, U)$ is invertible and $\| B - C \| < \| B^{-1} \|^{-1}$ . Show that $A = B^{-1}C$ is invertible. Show that $A^{-1}B^{-1}C = I$ and that $CA^{-1}B^{-1} = BB^{-1}BA^{-1}B^{-1} = I$ , so $C$ is invertible with inverse $A^{-1}B^{-1}$ . Show, further, that

$$
\| C ^ {- 1} \| \leq \| B ^ {- 1} \| (1 - \| B ^ {- 1} \| \| B - C \|) ^ {- 1}, \\
\| B ^ {- 1} - C ^ {- 1} \| \leq \| B ^ {- 1} \| \| B - C \| (1 - \| B ^ {- 1} \| \| B - C \|) ^ {- 1}.
$$

(iv) Let $E$ be the set of invertible $C \in \mathcal{L}(U, U)$. Show that $E$ is open in $(\mathcal{L}(U, U), \| \cdot \|)$. Show further that, if we define $\Theta : E \to E$ by $\Theta(C) = C^{-1}$, then $\Theta$ is a continuous function.

(v) Returning to the discussion in (i) and (ii), show that if $\| T \| < 1$ , then

$$
\| (I - T) ^ {- 1} - I - T \| \leq \| T \| ^ {2} (1 - \| T \|) ^ {- 1}
$$

and

$$
\| (I + T) ^ {- 1} - I + T \| \leq \| T \| ^ {2} (1 - \| T \|) ^ {- 1}.
$$

Conclude that

$$
\Theta (I + T) = I - T + \epsilon (T) \| T \|,
$$

where $\epsilon : \mathcal{L}(U, U) \to \mathcal{L}(U, U)$ is such that $\| \epsilon(T) \| \to 0$ as $\| T \| \to 0$ . Conclude that $\Theta$ is differentiable at $I$ . [If you wish to confine yourself to finite-dimensional spaces, take $U$ finite-dimensional, but there is no need.]

(vi) Show that $\Theta$ is everywhere differentiable on $E$ with $D\Theta(B) = \Phi_B$ , where $\Phi_B: \mathcal{L}(U, U) \to \mathcal{L}(U, U)$ is the linear map given by

$$
\Phi_ {B} (S) = - B ^ {- 1} S B ^ {- 1}.
$$

Check that this reduces to a known result when $\dim U = 1$ .


### app_k_311 [app_k_311]


K.311. (Spectral radius.) [13.1, T, ↑] We continue with the ideas and notation of Exercise K.310.

(i) Suppose that $A\in \mathcal{L}(U,U)$ . Show that

$$
\| A ^ {j k + r} \| \leq \| A ^ {j} \| ^ {k} \| A \| ^ {r}.
$$

(ii) Continuing with the hypotheses of (i), show that $\triangle = \inf_{n\geq 1}\| A^n\|^{1 / n}$ exists and, by using the result of (i), or otherwise, that

$$
\| A ^ {n} \| ^ {1 / n} \rightarrow \triangle .
$$

We call $\triangle$ the spectral radius of $A$ , and write $\rho(A) = \triangle$ .

(iii) Show that $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} A^n$ converges in the operator norm if $\rho(A) < 1$ and diverges if $\rho(A) > 1$ .

(iv) If $\rho(A) < 1$ , show that $I - A$ is invertible and $(I - A)^{-1} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} A^n$ .


### app_k_312 [app_k_312]


K.312. [13.1, T, ↑] We continue with the ideas of Exercise K.311, but change our alphabet from Latin to Greek.

(i) If $\alpha \in \mathcal{L}(U,U)$ and $\lambda$ is a scalar, show that $\rho (\lambda \alpha) = |\lambda |\rho (\alpha)$ .

(ii) Write down a linear map $\beta : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ such that $\beta^{m-1} \neq 0$ but $\beta^m = 0$ . What is the value of $\rho(\beta)$ ?

(iii) If the linear map $\alpha : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ has $m$ distinct eigenvalues explain briefly why we can find an invertible linear map $\theta : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ such that $\theta \alpha \theta^{-1}$ has a diagonal matrix $D$ with respect to the standard basis. By considering the behaviour of $D^n$ , or otherwise, show that

$$
\rho (\alpha) = \max \{| \lambda |: \lambda \text{ an eigenvalue of } \alpha \}.
$$

(For an improvement of this result, see Exercise K.314.)

(iv) Give two linear maps $\alpha, \beta : \mathbb{C}^2 \to \mathbb{C}^2$ such that $\rho(\alpha) = \rho(\beta) = 0$ but $\rho(\alpha + \beta) = 1$ .


### app_k_313 [app_k_313]


K.313. (Cayley-Hamilton.) [13.1, T, ↑] This exercise requires you to know that any linear map $\alpha : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ has an upper triangular matrix with respect to some basis. Recall also that, if $\alpha : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ , we can define its characteristic polynomial $\chi_{\\alpha}$ by the condition $\det(t\iota - \alpha) = \chi_{\\alpha}(t)$ for all $t \in \mathbb{C}$ . (Here and elsewhere, $\iota : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ is the identity map.)

(i) We work with the standard basis on $\mathbb{C}^m$ . Explain why, if $\alpha : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ is linear, we can find an invertible linear map $\theta : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ such that $\beta = \theta \alpha \theta^{-1}$ has an upper triangular matrix with respect to the standard basis.

(ii) Show that, if $\beta : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ has an upper triangular matrix with respect to the standard basis and $\epsilon > 0$ , then we can find $\gamma : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ with $m$ distinct eigenvalues such that $\| \gamma - \beta \| < \epsilon$ .

(iii) Deduce that, if $\alpha : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ is linear, we can find linear $\alpha_k : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ with $m$ distinct eigenvalues and

$$
\| \alpha_ {k} - \alpha \| \rightarrow 0 \quad \text{as } k \rightarrow \infty .
$$

(iv) (Pure algebra) If $\alpha : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ has $n$ distinct eigenvalues, show, by considering the matrix of $\alpha$ with respect to a basis of eigenvectors, that

$$
\chi_ {\\alpha} (\alpha) = 0.
$$

(v) Show carefully, using (iii) and limiting arguments, that, if $\alpha : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ is linear, then

$$
\chi_ {\\alpha} (\alpha) = 0.
$$

This is the Cayley-Hamilton theorem.

[The argument given here is substantially longer than that used in algebra courses and is repugnant to the soul of any pure-minded algebraist. But it has its points of interest.]

(vi) Suppose that $\alpha, \beta : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ are linear maps. Observe that $\det(t\beta - \beta t) = 0$ for all $t \in \mathbb{C}$ , but show that, if $m \geq 2$ , we can choose $\alpha$ and $\beta$ such that

$$
\det (\alpha \beta - \beta \alpha) \neq 0.
$$

Thus arguments of the form 'replace $t$ by $\alpha$ ' cannot be used to prove results like the Cayley-Hamilton theorem.


### app_k_314 [app_k_314]


K.314. [13.1, T, ↑] We continue with the ideas of Exercise K.312.

(i) If $\alpha, \beta \in \mathcal{L}(U,U)$ and $\alpha$ and $\beta$ commute, show that

$$
\rho (\alpha + \beta) \leq \rho (\alpha) + \rho (\beta).
$$

(Compare Exercise K.312 (iv).)

(ii) By using the ideas of Exercise K.313, or otherwise, show that, if $\alpha : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ is linear and $\epsilon > 0$ , we can find a $\beta : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ such that $\|\beta\| < \epsilon$ , $\alpha$ and $\beta$ commute, $\alpha + \beta$ has $m$ distinct eigenvalues, and every eigenvalue of $\alpha + \beta$ lies within a distance $\epsilon$ of some eigenvalue of $\alpha$ and vice versa.

(iii) By using (ii), or otherwise, show that, if $\alpha : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ is linear, then

$$
\rho (\alpha) = \max \{ | \lambda |: \lambda \text{ an eigenvalue of } \alpha \}.
$$

[You may wish to read or reread Exercises K.109 to K.111 at this point.]

(iv) Explain why we can define the spectral radius $\rho(A)$ of an $n \times n$ matrix unambiguously as the spectral radius $\rho(\alpha)$ of a linear map represented by $A$ with respect to some basis.


### app_k_315 [app_k_315]


K.315. [13.1, T, ↑] In previous exercises we have shown that, if $\alpha : \mathbb{C}^m \to \mathbb{C}^m$ is linear, and we write

$$
\rho (\alpha) = \max \{ | \lambda |: \lambda \text{ an eigenvalue of } \alpha \},
$$

then $\| \alpha^n\|^{1 / n}\to \rho (\alpha)$ .

Suppose that $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{C}^m$ , we choose an arbitrary $\mathbf{x}_0 \in \mathbb{C}^m$ and we consider the sequence

$$
\mathbf {x} _ {n + 1} = \mathbf {b} + \alpha \mathbf {x} _ {n}.
$$

If $\rho (\alpha) < 1$ , give two proofs that

$$
\| \mathbf {x} _ {n} - (\iota - \alpha) ^ {- 1} \mathbf {b} \| \rightarrow 0 \quad \text{as } n \rightarrow \infty :
$$

(i) By finding $\mathbf{x}_n$ explicitly and using Exercise K.311.

(ii) By using the first paragraph of Exercise K.290.

By considering an eigenvector corresponding to an eigenvalue of largest modulus, show that the sequence will diverge if $\rho(\alpha) > 1$ and we choose $\mathbf{x}_0$ suitably. Show that, if $\rho(\alpha) = 1$ , either we can find an $\mathbf{x}_0$ such that the sequence diverges or, if the sequence always converges, we can find two starting points with different limits.


### app_k_316 [app_k_316]


K.316. [13.1, T, ↑] Consider the problem of solving the equation

$$
A \mathbf {x} = \mathbf {b},
$$

where $A$ is an $m \times m$ matrix and $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are column vectors of length $m$ . If $m$ is small, then there is no reason to go beyond standard computational

methods, and if $m$ is large and $A$ is a general matrix, we have no choice but to use standard methods. These involve storing all $m^2$ coefficients and, in the case of Gaussian elimination, require of the order of $m^3$ operations.

Suppose we have to deal with a matrix $A$ such that $A$ is close to $I$ , in some sense to be determined later in the exercise, and there are only of the order of $n$ non-zero coefficients in $I - A$ in a well organized pattern. (Such problems arise in the numerical solution of important partial differential equations.) The following method can then be employed. Choose $\mathbf{x}_0$ and define a sequence

$$
\mathbf {x} _ {n + 1} = \mathbf {b} + (I - A) \mathbf {x} _ {n}.
$$

Using the ideas of earlier exercises, show that, under certain conditions, to be stated, $\mathbf{x}_n$ will tend to a unique solution of $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ . Discuss the rapidity of convergence, and show that, under certain conditions, to be stated, only a few iterations will be required to get the answer to any reasonable degree of accuracy. Since each iteration requires, at worst, of the order of $m^2$ operations, and, in many cases, only of the order of $m$ operations, this method is much more efficient than any known general purpose method.

The rest of the exercise consists of elaboration of this idea. We require Exercise K.315. Suppose that $A$ is an $m \times m$ matrix and $A = D - U - L$ where $L$ is strictly lower triangular, $U$ is strictly upper triangular and $D$ is diagonal with all diagonal terms non-zero. We seek to solve $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ . The following iterative schemes have been proposed:

Jacobi: $\mathbf{x}_{n + 1} = D^{-1}(\mathbf{b} + (U + L)\mathbf{x}_n)$

Gauss-Seidel: $\mathbf{x}_{n + 1} = (D - L)^{-1}(\mathbf{b} + U\mathbf{x}_n)$

(Note that it is easy to invert a triangular matrix.) For each of these two schemes, give a necessary and sufficient condition in terms of the spectral radius of an appropriate matrix for the method to work.

A variation on the Gauss-Seidel method uses

$$
\mathbf {x} _ {n + 1} = (D - \omega L) ^ {- 1} (\omega \mathbf {b} + ((1 - \omega) D + \omega U) \mathbf {x} _ {n}),
$$

where $\omega$ is some fixed real number. Give a necessary and sufficient condition, in the form $\rho(H) < 1$ , where $H$ is an appropriate matrix, for the method to work. By showing that

$$
\det  H = (1 - \omega) ^ {m},
$$

or otherwise, show that the scheme must fail if $\omega < 0$ or $\omega > 2$ .


### app_k_317 [app_k_317]


K.317. [13.1, S, ↑↑] (This is a short exercise, but requires part (iv) of Exercise K.310.) Show that Theorem 13.13 can be strengthened by adding the following sentence at the end. 'Moreover, $D\mathbf{f}|_{B}^{-1}$ is continuous on $B$ .'


### app_k_318 [app_k_318]


K.318. [13.1, T, ↑↑] This is another exercise in the ideas of Exercise K.310. We work in a complete normed space $(U, \| \cdot \|_U)$ as before.

(i) Show that, if $\alpha \in \mathcal{L}(U,U)$ , we can find $\exp \alpha \in \mathcal{L}(U,U)$ such that

$$
\left\| \sum_ {r = 0} ^ {n} \frac {\alpha^ {r}}{r !} - \exp \alpha \right\| \rightarrow 0
$$

as $n\to \infty$ .

(ii) Show carefully that, if $\alpha$ and $\beta$ commute,

$$
\exp \alpha \exp \beta = \exp (\alpha + \beta).
$$

(iii) Show that, if $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are general (not necessarily commuting) elements of $\mathcal{L}(U,U)$ , then

$$
\left\| h ^ {- 2} \left(\exp (h \alpha) \exp (h \beta) - \exp (h \beta) \exp (h \alpha)\right) - (\alpha \beta - \beta \alpha) \right\| \rightarrow 0
$$

as the real number $h\to 0$ .

Conclude that, in general, $\exp (\alpha)\exp (\beta)$ and $\exp (\beta)\exp (\alpha)$ need not be equal. Deduce also that $\exp (\alpha +\beta)$ and $\exp (\alpha)\exp (\beta)$ need not be equal.

(iv) Show carefully (you must bear part (iii) in mind) that the map $\exp : \mathcal{L}(U, U) \to \mathcal{L}(U, U)$ is everywhere continuous.


### app_k_319 [app_k_319]


K.319. [13.1, P, ↑] (i) Consider the map $\Theta_3: \mathcal{L}(U, U) \to \mathcal{L}(U, U)$ given by $\Theta_3(\alpha) = \alpha^3$ . Show that $\Theta$ is everywhere differentiable with

$$
D \Theta_ {3} (\alpha) \beta = \beta \alpha^ {2} + \alpha \beta \alpha + \alpha^ {2} \beta .
$$

(ii) State and prove the appropriate generalisation to the map $\alpha \mapsto \alpha^m$ with $m$ a positive integer.

(iii) Show that exp, defined in Exercise K.318, is everywhere differentiable. [This requires care.]


### app_k_320 [app_k_320]


K.320. [13.1, P, S, ↑↑] Suppose $U$ is a finite-dimensional vector space over $\mathbb{C}$ . Let $\alpha : U \to U$ be a linear map. If $\alpha$ has matrix representation $A$ with respect to some basis, explain why, as $N \to \infty$ , the entries of the matrix $\sum_{n=0}^{N} A^n / n!$ converge to the entries of a matrix $\exp A$ which represents $\exp \alpha$ with respect to the given basis. (See Exercise K.318.)

It is a theorem that any $\alpha \in \mathcal{L}(U,U)$ has an upper triangular matrix with respect to some basis. By using this fact, or otherwise, show that

$$
\det  (\exp \alpha) = e ^ {\operatorname {Trace} \alpha}.
$$


### app_k_321 [app_k_321]


K.321. [13.1, P] We work in the space $M_2(\mathbb{R})$ of $2 \times 2$ real matrices. We give $M_2(\mathbb{R})$ the associated operator norm.

(i) Show that the map $S: M_2(\mathbb{R}) \to M_2(\mathbb{R})$ given by $S(A) = A^2$ is everywhere differentiable with $DS(A)B = AB + BA$ . (If you have done Exercise K.319, you may just quote it.)

(ii) Show that the matrix equation

$$
A ^ {2} = \left( \begin{array}{c c} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array} \right)
$$

has no solution.

(iii) Calculate explicitly all the solutions of

$$
\left( \begin{array}{c c} a & b \\ c & d \end{array} \right) ^ {2} = I.
$$

What are the possible values of $\det A$ ? Describe geometrically the linear maps associated with the matrices $A$ such that $A^2 = I$ and $\det A = 1$ . Describe geometrically the linear maps associated with the matrices $A$ such that $A^2 = I$ and $A$ is diagonal.

(iv) Show that there are open sets $G$ and $H$ containing 0 (the zero matrix) such that the equation

$$
A ^ {2} = I + X
$$

has exactly one solution of the form $A = I + Y$ with $Y \in H$ for each $X \in G$ .

(v) Show that we cannot find open sets $G$ and $H$ containing 0 (the zero matrix) such that the equation

$$
A ^ {2} = I + X
$$

has exactly one solution of the form

$$
A = \left( \begin{array}{c c} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & - 1 \end{array} \right) + Y
$$

with $Y \in G$ for each $X \in H$ . Identify which hypothesis of the inverse function theorem (Theorem 13.13) fails to hold and show, by direct calculation, that it does indeed fail.

(vi) For which $B$ is it true that $B^2 = I$ and we can find open sets $H$ and $G$ containing 0 (the zero matrix) such that the equation

$$
A ^ {2} = I + X
$$

has exactly one solution of the form $A = B + Y$ with $Y \in G$ for each $X \in H$ ? Give reasons for your answer.


### app_k_322 [app_k_322]


K.322. [13.1, P, G] This exercise requires some knowledge of eigenvectors of symmetric linear maps. We work in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with the usual inner product. Suppose $\alpha : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^3$ is an antisymmetric linear map (that is, $\alpha^T = -\alpha$ ). Show that $\mathbf{x}$ and $\alpha \mathbf{x}$ are orthogonal and that the eigenvalues of $\alpha^2$ must be non-positive real numbers. By considering eigenvectors of $\alpha$ and $\alpha^2$ , show that we can always find $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ and three orthonormal vectors $\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2$ and $\mathbf{e}_3$ such that

$$
\alpha \mathbf {e} _ {1} = \mu \mathbf {e} _ {2}, \quad \alpha \mathbf {e} _ {2} = - \mu \mathbf {e} _ {1}, \quad \alpha \mathbf {e} _ {3} = \mathbf {0}.
$$

By choosing appropriate axes and using matrix representations, show that $\exp \alpha$ is a rotation.


### app_k_323 [app_k_323]


K.323. [13.1, P, G, S] Show that every linear map $\alpha : \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}^m$ is the sum of a symmetric and an antisymmetric linear map. Suppose $\alpha$ is an orthogonal map (that is, $\alpha \alpha^T = \iota$ ) with $\|\iota - \alpha\| < \epsilon$ and $\epsilon$ very small. Show that

$$
\alpha = \iota + \epsilon \beta + \epsilon^ {2} \gamma ,
$$

where $\beta$ is antisymmetric, $\gamma$ is antisymmetric and $\| \beta \|, \| \gamma \| \leq 2$ .


### app_k_324 [app_k_324]


K.324. [13.1, P, G, ↑↑] In this exercise we work with real $n \times n$ matrices. Although we use the exponential

$$
\exp A = \sum_ {j = 0} ^ {\infty} \frac {A ^ {n}}{n !}
$$

obtained in Exercise K.320, the main requirement is some elementary matrix theory. Our object is to find all 'isotropic matrices', that is to say, all matrices $A$ such that $U A U^T = A$ whenever $U U^T = I$ and $\det U = 1$ .

(i) Show that, if $UU^T = I$ , then $\det U = \pm 1$ .

(ii) If $B^T = -B$ , show that $(\exp B)(\exp B)^T = 1$ .

(iii) Show that the function $\theta : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ given by $\theta(h) = \det(\exp hB)$ is continuous. Deduce, using part (i), that, if $B^T = -B$ , then $\det(\exp B) = 1$ .

(iv) Let $A$ be isotropic and $B^T = -B$ . Explain why $(\exp hB)A = A(\exp hB)$ for all $h \in \mathbb{R}$ and, by considering what happens when $h \to 0$ , show that $BA = AB$ .

(v) By considering particular simple choices of $B$ , or otherwise, show that, if $n \geq 3$ , then the only isotropic matrices have the form $\lambda I$ with $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ .

(vi) Find all the isotropic matrices when $n = 2$ . (Note that you must check that the matrices you find are indeed isotropic.) Give a simple geometric characterisation of those isotropic matrices with determinant 1, and use it to characterise all isotropic matrices.

(vii) What happens when $n = 1$ ?

(viii) How would the results of (v), (vi) and (vii) change if we omitted the condition $\det U = 1$ in the definition of isotropic? Prove your statements.


### app_k_325 [app_k_325]


K.325. [13.3, M] (Treat this as a 'methods question'.) The four vertices $A$ , $B$ , $C$ , $D$ of a quadrilateral lie in anti-clockwise order on a circle of radius $a$ and center $O$ . We write $2\theta_{1} = \angle AOB$ , $2\theta_{2} = \angle BOC$ , $2\theta_{3} = \angle COD$ , $2\theta_{4} = \angle DOA$ . Find the area of the quadrilateral and state the relation that $\theta_{1}$ , $\theta_{2}$ , $\theta_{3}$ and $\theta_{4}$ must satisfy.

Use Lagrange's method to find the form of a quadrilateral of greatest area inscribed in a circle of radius $a$ .

Use Lagrange's method to find the form of an $n$ -gon of greatest area inscribed in a circle $[n \geq 3]$ .

Use Lagrange's method to find the form of an $n$ -gon of least area circumscribing a circle $[n \geq 3]$ .

[Compare Exercise K.41.]


### app_k_326 [app_k_326]


K.326. [13.3, T] Let $p$ and $q$ be strictly positive real numbers with $p^{-1} + q^{-1} = 1$ . Suppose that $y_{1}, y_{2}, \ldots, y_{n}, c > 0$ . Explain why there must exist $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n} \geq 0$ with $\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{j}^{p} = c$ and

$$
\sum_ {j = 1} ^ {n} x _ {j} y _ {j} \geq \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {n} t _ {j} y _ {j} \quad \text{whenever } t _ {1}, t _ {2}, \ldots, t _ {n} \geq 0 \quad \text{with } \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {n} t _ {j} ^ {p} = c.
$$

Use the Lagrange multiplier method to find the $x_{j}$ . Deduce from your answer that

$$
\sum_ {j = 1} ^ {n} \left| a _ {j} b _ {j} \right| \leq \left(\sum_ {j = 1} ^ {n} \left| a _ {j} \right| ^ {p}\right) ^ {1 / p} \left(\sum_ {j = 1} ^ {n} \left| b _ {j} \right| ^ {q}\right) ^ {1 / q}
$$

whenever $a_{j}$ , $b_{j} \in \mathbb{C}$ . Under what conditions does equality hold?

This gives an alternative proof of the first result in Exercise K.211 (i).


### app_k_327 [app_k_327]


K.327. (The parallelogram law.) [14.1, T] Except in the last part of this exercise, we work in a real normed vector space $(V, \| \cdot \|)$.

(i) Suppose that $V$ has a real inner product $\langle ,\rangle$ such that $\langle \mathbf{x},\mathbf{x}\rangle = \| \mathbf{x}\|^2$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in V$ . Show that

$$
\| \mathbf {x} + \mathbf {y} \| ^ {2} + \| \mathbf {x} - \mathbf {y} \| ^ {2} = 2 \| \mathbf {x} \| ^ {2} + 2 \| \mathbf {y} \| ^ {2}
$$

for all $\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}\in V$ . (This is called the parallelogram law.)

(ii) Show also that

$$
4 \langle {\\bf x}, {\\bf y} \\rangle = \| {\\bf x} + {\\bf y} \| ^ {2} - \| {\\bf x} - {\\bf y} \| ^ {2}
$$

for all $\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}\in V$ . (This is called the polarisation identity.)

(iii) Use the parallelogram law to obtain a relation between the lengths of the sides and the diagonals of a parallelogram in Euclidean space.
